title: Nested Data Structures
text:
1 Welcome
This workshop is built around a single, uncomfortable idea: your data is never really about your data. Every dataset you collect is a partial, imperfect snapshot of a broader process — a process that is almost always structured, layered, and nested in ways your standard analysis ignores.
A patient’s mood today is nested within their longer emotional trajectory. A consumer’s rating on Airbnb is nested within their history with that host, the platform’s culture, and the competitive context. A student’s exam score is nested within their classroom, which is nested within their school. Even a simple between-subjects experiment — where you collect exactly one observation per person — has nesting baked in: your participants were sampled from a population, your stimuli were sampled from a population of possible stimuli, and the moment of measurement was sampled from a population of possible moments.
The field of nested data analysis is fundamentally about recovering the data-generating process (DGP) from these snapshots. The models in this workshop don’t just fit your data better — they get you closer to the truth about the process that produced it.

title: Nested Data Structures
text:
1.1 What This Workshop Covers
The curriculum is organized around a recurring question: What assumption is standard regression making here, and what happens when it’s wrong?
Each module introduces one class of assumption, shows concretely when it breaks down, and presents the modeling tools that relax it — always working back to what this tells us about the underlying DGP.


1.1.1 Module 0: All Data is Nested
The conceptual foundation. Why the independence assumption is almost always violated, how to measure how badly, and how to think about your data as a snapshot of a larger process.


1.1.2 Module 1: The Mixed-Effects Framework
The core toolkit: fixed effects, random intercepts, random slopes, three-level structures, and crossed random effects. With a deep focus on the assumptions each model makes and how to check them.


1.1.3 Module 2: Centering & Between/Within Effects
One of the most consequential (and least taught) decisions in multilevel analysis: what does your predictor mean? Grand-mean centering versus cluster-mean centering, the ecological fallacy, and Simpson’s Paradox.


1.1.4 Module 3: Inferential Threats & Identification
When your X isn’t really independent: omitted variables, selection effects, attrition, reverse causality. And the identification strategies — IV, RDD, DiD, propensity scores — that let us recover causal claims.


1.1.5 Module 4: Longitudinal Data & Change
Repeated measures done right. Why RM-ANOVA almost always makes the wrong covariance assumptions, how growth curve models recover individual trajectories, latent growth curve models in SEM, autoregressive structures, and cross-lagged panel models.


1.1.6 Module 5: Experimental Designs with Nesting
For experimentalists: crossed random effects for subjects and stimuli, partially nested designs, maximal vs. parsimonious random effect structures, multi-site experiments, and power analysis.


1.1.7 Module 6: Extended Toolkit
Binary and count outcomes (GLMMs), survival analysis, missing data in multilevel contexts, and effect sizes and reporting standards.

title: Nested Data Structures
text:
1.2 How to Use This Workshop
Each section pairs conceptual explanation with R tutorials and — wherever possible — both an experimental example and a secondary/observational data example. All code is shown using the toggle button at the top right of each page.
This workshop assumes you are comfortable with basic regression and have used R before. No prior exposure to multilevel modeling is assumed.

title: Nested Data Structures
text:
1.3 Key Premise

Science combines observable data with assumptions to infer latent or general processes. Research errors frequently stem from unexamined assumptions about the structure of that data. The goal of nested data models is not just to “control for clustering” — it is to recapture the data-generating process.

The models here are tools for thinking. Understanding their assumptions — and when those assumptions fail — is the heart of this workshop.

title: 2  Overview
text: Module 0: All Data is Nested

title: 2  Overview
text:
2.1 The Central Claim
Before we learn any specific model, we need to confront a claim that sounds provocative but is actually quite mundane once you sit with it:

All data is nested.

Not “some data.” Not “longitudinal data” or “hierarchical organizational data.” All of it.
When you run a between-subjects experiment and collect one rating from each of 200 participants, your data looks flat. One row per person. No repeated measures. No groups. Standard regression seems perfectly appropriate.
But consider where those 200 ratings actually came from. Each participant is a particular person, sampled from a larger population of possible participants. Each stimulus they responded to was drawn from a population of possible stimuli. The experiment happened on specific days, at specific times, in specific locations — all sampled from the possible moments and contexts in which you could have run the study. Your 200 ratings are not 200 independent draws from some distribution. They are 200 snapshots of a structured, layered data-generating process.
This framing changes how you think about analysis. The goal is not just to summarize what happened in your sample. It is to recover the process that generated it.

title: 2  Overview
text:
2.2 What This Module Covers
This module lays the conceptual groundwork for everything that follows.
Part 1: The DGP Perspective introduces the idea that data is always a partial snapshot of a larger process. We develop intuition for what a data-generating process is, why it is almost always nested, and what it means to “recapture the DGP.”
Part 2: Types of Nested Structures catalogs the main ways that nesting appears in social science data — pure hierarchies, crossed structures, panel data, longitudinal data — and shows how each arises naturally from the way we collect information about people, organizations, time, and context.
Part 3: Measuring Non-Independence introduces the Intraclass Correlation Coefficient (ICC) and design effects, the two most important tools for diagnosing how badly your data violates the independence assumption. We show how even modest ICCs produce dramatic distortions in inference.
Part 4: Data Structure and Format covers the practical side: how nested data needs to be organized (long format), what identifying variables you need, and how to read a dataset and immediately see its level structure.

title: 2  Overview
text:
2.3 A Note on Prerequisites
This workshop assumes you understand basic linear regression: what a slope and intercept mean, how to fit a model in R, what a residual is. You do not need to have any prior exposure to multilevel modeling, mixed-effects models, or hierarchical linear models. If you have heard these terms and found them confusing, that is fine — we start from scratch.

title: 3  The DGP Perspective
text: 4 The Data-Generating Process

title: 3  The DGP Perspective
text:
4.1 Your Data is a Photograph
Imagine you want to understand how rivers move. You walk up to a river and take a photograph. The photograph captures the river’s position at exactly that moment — the precise shape of the water, the location of each ripple, the exact color of the surface.
Now: how much can you learn about the river from one photograph?
You can learn something. You can see that it flows roughly north, that the banks are wide, that the water looks fast. But you cannot see the seasonal floods, the drought periods, the long-term shift of the channel, or the way the current varies across different cross-sections. One photograph freezes a moment in an ongoing process.
Your dataset is that photograph. The process that generated your data — the underlying mechanisms, the population of people, the range of contexts, the flow of time — is the river. Your job as a researcher is to infer as much as possible about the river from your one photograph. The models in this workshop are tools for doing that inference more honestly.

title: 3  The DGP Perspective
text:
4.2 What is a Data-Generating Process?
A data-generating process (DGP) is the full set of mechanisms that produce your data. It includes:

The population of units you are sampling (people, firms, countries, neurons)
The population of contexts, stimuli, or conditions under which observations occur
The temporal process by which things change and unfold
The relationships between variables — what causes what, and how strongly

Your dataset is one realization of this process. A different sample, a different week, a different set of stimuli, would have given you different numbers — but from the same underlying process.
The question that motivates nested data analysis is: what does this realization tell us about the process?

title: 3  The DGP Perspective
section: 4.3 Why the DGP is Almost Always Nested
text:
4.3 Why the DGP is Almost Always Nested
Think about the last study you ran or analyzed. Ask yourself: where did the observations come from?

People are sampled from populations that have subgroups, histories, cultures, and contexts. A sample of Dutch students in 2024 is not the same as a sample of American workers in 1990, even if both studies measure “risk preference.”
Time points are sampled from a continuous process. A mood rating collected at 9am on a Tuesday is part of a longer trajectory of mood for that person, and is nested within their week, month, and life stage.
Stimuli or items — the vignettes, products, words, or images you use — are sampled from a much larger space of possible stimuli. Your findings will generalize to the extent that your stimuli are representative.
Context — the lab, the country, the platform, the season — introduces systematic variance that your model needs to account for.

In every case, observations are not independent draws from a flat distribution. They cluster. People who share a classroom share a teacher. Employees at the same firm share a manager, a culture, and an incentive structure. Responses to the same survey item at different times are correlated because the person has not changed that much between measurements.
The independence assumption — that each observation is an independent draw, unrelated to all others — is almost never literally true. It is, at best, an approximation. And it is an approximation that can go badly wrong.

title: 3  The DGP Perspective
text:
4.4 A Simple Demonstration
Let’s make this concrete. Suppose we study whether daily hours of sleep predict mood. We collect 7 days of data from 30 people: 210 observations total.


Code
# Simulate: 30 people, 7 days each
n_people <- 30
n_days   <- 7

# Each person has their own baseline mood (random intercept)
# and their own sleep-mood relationship (random slope)
person_intercepts <- rnorm(n_people, mean = 50, sd = 8)
person_slopes     <- rnorm(n_people, mean = 3,  sd = 2)

df <- expand_grid(
  person = 1:n_people,
  day    = 1:n_days
) |>
  mutate(
    sleep = rnorm(n(), mean = 7, sd = 1.5),
    mood  = person_intercepts[person] +
            person_slopes[person] * (sleep - 7) +
            rnorm(n(), 0, 5)
  )

# Plot: pooled view (ignoring nesting) vs. within-person view
p1 <- ggplot(df, aes(x = sleep, y = mood)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.3, color = "#1e3a5f") +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = "#e63946", se = TRUE) +
  labs(
    title  = "Pooled (ignoring nesting)",
    x      = "Hours of sleep",
    y      = "Mood (0-100)",
    caption = "Each dot is one person-day"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

p2 <- ggplot(df, aes(x = sleep, y = mood, group = person)) +
  geom_line(aes(color = factor(person)), alpha = 0.5, show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.2, size = 0.8) +
  labs(
    title  = "Within-person (respecting nesting)",
    x      = "Hours of sleep",
    y      = "Mood (0-100)",
    caption = "Each line is one person's 7-day trajectory"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  scale_color_viridis_d()

library(patchwork)
p1 + p2









The left panel treats all 210 observations as independent. It shows something — there’s an upward slope — but it blurs together two very different sources of variation: (1) people who generally sleep more also tend to have higher baseline mood, and (2) on days when a given person sleeps more, their mood is higher. These are distinct phenomena. The right panel begins to separate them.
The data-generating process here has clear structure: a person-level layer (baseline mood, general sleep habits) and a day-level layer (deviation from that baseline). Ignoring that structure does not just make our estimates less precise. It can make them wrong.

title: 3  The DGP Perspective
text:
4.5 The Three Questions
Every dataset you encounter deserves three questions before you run a single model:






ImportantThree DGP Questions



1. What is the sampling unit? What are the rows in your dataset? People? Days? Observations? Products? Multiple types nested inside each other?
2. What are the higher levels? Are your sampling units grouped into something larger? People within countries? Days within people? Patients within therapists? Even if you did not design the study this way, the DGP probably has this structure.
3. What assumption does my model make about the relationship between levels? Standard regression assumes levels do not exist — every row is independent. Is that reasonable? Can you quantify how wrong it is?


These questions are not rhetorical. Answering them is the first step of every analysis in this workshop.

title: 3  The DGP Perspective
section: 4.6 The DGP Frame vs. the “Fix My Standard Errors” Frame
text:
4.6 The DGP Frame vs. the “Fix My Standard Errors” Frame
There is a narrow and a broad way to motivate multilevel models.
The narrow frame says: clustered data violates the independence assumption, which inflates Type I error rates. You need multilevel models to get correct standard errors.
This is true and important. But it is a surprisingly weak reason to care about these models. It frames them as a statistical correction — something you do defensively to avoid getting dinged in review.
The broad frame says: your data has structure that reflects real processes. The variance at the person level is not nuisance to be corrected — it is scientifically interesting. How much does baseline mood vary across people? How much does the sleep-mood relationship vary? Who benefits most from sleep? These are not questions about standard errors. They are questions about the heterogeneity of human experience.
The models in this workshop answer both frames. But the broad frame is the more interesting one, and it is the one that will make you a better researcher.

title: 3  The DGP Perspective
text:
4.7 Recommended Reading







Topic
Citation




Introduction to multilevel modeling for psychologists
Hoffman, L. (2015). Longitudinal Analysis: Modeling Within-Person Fluctuation and Change. Routledge. (Chapter 1)


The DGP perspective in econometrics
Angrist, J. D., & Pischke, J. S. (2009). Mostly Harmless Econometrics. Princeton University Press. (Chapter 1)


Why nesting matters for experiments
Judd, C. M., Westfall, J., & Kenny, D. A. (2017). Experiments with more than one random factor: Designs, analytic models, and statistical power. Annual Review of Psychology, 68, 601–625.

title: 4  Types of Nested Structures
text: 5 Types of Nested Structures

title: 4  Types of Nested Structures
text:
5.1 A Taxonomy of Nesting
Nesting takes several forms in social science data. Getting them confused leads to misspecified models and wrong answers. This section maps out the main types, shows what they look like in a dataset, and explains how the DGP perspective helps you identify which structure you have.
The key distinction is between pure hierarchies (units nested strictly within higher-level units) and crossed structures (units belonging to multiple grouping factors simultaneously). Real data often combines both.

title: 4  Types of Nested Structures
text:
5.2 Pure Hierarchy: One Nesting Level
The simplest nested structure: lower-level units are grouped into higher-level units, and each lower-level unit belongs to exactly one group.
Example: Students nested within classrooms.

500 students, each in one of 25 classrooms
A student’s test score depends partly on their own ability and partly on their classroom environment (teacher quality, class size, peer effects)
Observations within the same classroom are more similar to each other than to observations from different classrooms



Code
# Simulate a two-level structure
n_classes   <- 6
n_students  <- 8  # per class

class_effects <- rnorm(n_classes, 0, 5)

df_school <- expand_grid(
  classroom = 1:n_classes,
  student   = 1:n_students
) |>
  mutate(
    student_id   = row_number(),
    class_effect = class_effects[classroom],
    test_score   = 70 + class_effect + rnorm(n(), 0, 3),
    classroom    = factor(classroom, labels = paste("Class", 1:n_classes))
  )

ggplot(df_school, aes(x = classroom, y = test_score, color = classroom)) +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.15, size = 3, alpha = 0.8) +
  stat_summary(fun = mean, geom = "crossbar", width = 0.5,
               color = "black", linewidth = 0.8) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  labs(
    title   = "Students nested within classrooms",
    subtitle = "Black bars = classroom means; dots = individual students",
    x = "Classroom", y = "Test Score"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")









Notice that test scores cluster within classrooms. Students in Class 3 tend to score higher than those in Class 5, regardless of individual differences. A model that ignores classrooms treats these 48 scores as 48 independent data points. They are not — they are 6 groups of 8.
Data format: One row per student. You need a student ID and a classroom ID.

title: 4  Types of Nested Structures
text:
5.3 Three Levels: Nesting Within Nesting
The hierarchy can extend further. Students are nested within classrooms, which are nested within schools.
Example: Patients nested within therapists nested within clinics.
Each level adds a new source of systematic variance: - Patients differ from each other in severity, motivation, history - Therapists differ in skill, approach, empathy - Clinics differ in resources, culture, population served


Code
n_clinics    <- 3
n_therapists <- 4  # per clinic
n_patients   <- 5  # per therapist

clinic_effects    <- rnorm(n_clinics, 0, 6)
therapist_effects <- rnorm(n_clinics * n_therapists, 0, 4)

df_three <- expand_grid(
  clinic    = 1:n_clinics,
  therapist = 1:n_therapists,
  patient   = 1:n_patients
) |>
  mutate(
    therapist_id   = (clinic - 1) * n_therapists + therapist,
    clinic_eff     = clinic_effects[clinic],
    therapist_eff  = therapist_effects[therapist_id],
    outcome        = 50 + clinic_eff + therapist_eff + rnorm(n(), 0, 3),
    clinic_label   = paste("Clinic", clinic),
    therapist_label = paste0("T", therapist_id)
  )

ggplot(df_three, aes(x = therapist_label, y = outcome,
                      color = clinic_label)) +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.1, alpha = 0.7, size = 2.5) +
  stat_summary(fun = mean, geom = "point", shape = 18,
               size = 4, color = "black") +
  facet_wrap(~ clinic_label, scales = "free_x") +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
  labs(
    title   = "Three-level structure: Patients within Therapists within Clinics",
    x       = "Therapist",
    y       = "Outcome",
    color   = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")









The key diagnostic question for three-level data: how much variance lives at each level? Later, the ICC will give us a principled answer.

title: 4  Types of Nested Structures
text:
5.4 Panel Data: Cross-Sectional and Longitudinal
Panel data combines the cross-sectional and time-series structures. You observe multiple units (people, firms, countries) at multiple time points.
Example: Monthly spending by 50 households measured over 24 months.


Code
n_households <- 50
n_months     <- 24

household_baseline <- rnorm(n_households, 1000, 200)

df_panel <- expand_grid(
  household = 1:n_households,
  month     = 1:n_months
) |>
  mutate(
    baseline  = household_baseline[household],
    spending  = baseline + 20 * (month - 12.5) / 12 + rnorm(n(), 0, 80),
    time_trend = month
  )

# Show 6 households
sample_hh <- sample(1:n_households, 6)

ggplot(df_panel |> filter(household %in% sample_hh),
       aes(x = month, y = spending, color = factor(household))) +
  geom_line(alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_point(size = 1.5, alpha = 0.6) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Dark2") +
  labs(
    title    = "Panel data: 6 households over 24 months",
    subtitle = "Each line is one household's spending trajectory",
    x        = "Month",
    y        = "Monthly Spending ($)",
    color    = "Household"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)









Panel data has two sources of nesting simultaneously: 1. Observations are nested within households (same household at different times) 2. Observations might also be nested within time periods (all households in month 1 face the same economic conditions)
This means you potentially need fixed or random effects for both dimensions — a crossed structure, not a pure hierarchy.

title: 4  Types of Nested Structures
text:
5.5 Crossed Random Effects
In a crossed structure, observations belong to multiple grouping factors that are not nested within each other.
Classic example in psychology: Participants rate words in a lexical decision task.

40 participants each respond to 100 words
Each observation belongs to one participant AND one word
Participants are not nested within words, and words are not nested within participants
Both participant differences and word differences contribute to response time



Code
# Small illustration: 4 participants × 4 words
crossed_df <- expand_grid(
  participant = paste("P", 1:4),
  word        = paste("W", 1:4)
) |>
  mutate(RT = 500 + rnorm(16, 0, 50))

ggplot(crossed_df, aes(x = word, y = participant, fill = RT)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white", linewidth = 2) +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(RT)), size = 4) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "#deebf7", high = "#08519c") +
  labs(
    title    = "Crossed structure: each participant rates each word",
    subtitle = "Cell = response time (ms) for that participant-word combination",
    x        = "Word",
    y        = "Participant",
    fill     = "RT (ms)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)









The key point: participant 1’s response to word 3 is not in the same “group” as participant 2’s response to word 3. Both observations share a word, but not a participant. Both share nothing else. This crossed structure requires a different model than a pure hierarchy.

title: 4  Types of Nested Structures
text:
5.6 Repeated Measures and Longitudinal Data
Repeated measures means the same unit is measured multiple times — on the same variable, under different conditions, at different time points, or both.
The critical distinction:

Repeated measures (experimental): Each person experiences multiple conditions. The conditions are fixed (e.g., caffeine vs. placebo). The person is the random unit.
Longitudinal data: Each person is measured at multiple time points, and time is part of the model. Change over time is the phenomenon of interest.

Both are cases of observations nested within persons. But they ask different questions. Repeated-measures experiments ask: “does the condition affect the outcome?” Longitudinal models ask: “how does the outcome change over time, and does it change differently for different people?”


Code
# Repeated measures
df_rm <- tibble(
  person    = rep(1:20, each = 2),
  condition = rep(c("Placebo", "Caffeine"), 20),
  RT        = 450 + ifelse(condition == "Caffeine", -30, 0) +
              rnorm(40, 0, 40)
)

p_rm <- ggplot(df_rm, aes(x = condition, y = RT,
                            group = person, color = factor(person))) +
  geom_line(alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_point(size = 2, alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_color_viridis_d() +
  labs(title = "Repeated measures:\n2 conditions per person",
       x = NULL, y = "Response Time (ms)") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

# Longitudinal
df_long <- tibble(
  person = rep(1:10, each = 5),
  time   = rep(1:5, 10),
  score  = rep(rnorm(10, 50, 8), each = 5) +  # person baseline
           rep(rnorm(10, 2, 1), each = 5) * (rep(1:5, 10) - 3) +  # person slope
           rnorm(50, 0, 3)
)

p_long <- ggplot(df_long, aes(x = time, y = score,
                               group = person, color = factor(person))) +
  geom_line(alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_point(size = 2, alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_color_viridis_d() +
  labs(title = "Longitudinal:\n5 time points per person",
       x = "Time", y = "Score") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

library(patchwork)
p_rm + p_long

title: 4  Types of Nested Structures
section: 5.7 A Map of Common Data Structures
text:
5.7 A Map of Common Data Structures









Structure
Example
Nesting
DGP implication




Pure hierarchy
Students in classrooms
Strict: each unit in one group
Between-group variance is real


Three-level
Patients in therapists in clinics
Nested levels
Variance at each level


Panel
Countries over years
Cross-sectional + time
Unit effects and time effects


Crossed
Participants × stimuli
Each unit in multiple groups
Two sources of random variance


Repeated measures
Person × condition
Each person measured in each condition
Within-person correlation


Longitudinal
Person measured over time
Observations in person
Individual trajectories



The single most common mistake is treating one of these as another. The second most common mistake is failing to ask the question at all.






TipPractical Exercise



Look at the last dataset you analyzed. Ask:

What is the row in this dataset?
Can I draw a diagram of what contains what?
Are there two dimensions of grouping (e.g., people AND stimuli) that are crossed rather than nested?
What does a standard regression assume about the structure I just identified?

title: 5  Measuring Non-Independence
text: 6 Measuring Non-Independence

title: 5  Measuring Non-Independence
text:
6.1 Why We Need a Number
Nested structure is not binary — it is not just “present” or “absent.” Nesting can be strong (observations within a group are nearly identical) or weak (observations within a group are barely more similar than observations from different groups). The strength of nesting determines how badly standard analysis fails.
We need a number that quantifies this strength. That number is the Intraclass Correlation Coefficient (ICC).

title: 5  Measuring Non-Independence
text:
6.2 The Intraclass Correlation Coefficient
The ICC answers a deceptively simple question:

Of all the variance in my outcome, what fraction is due to group membership?

If you pick two observations at random from the same group, how correlated will they be? That correlation is the ICC.
\[
\text{ICC} = \frac{\sigma^2_{\text{between}}}{\sigma^2_{\text{between}} + \sigma^2_{\text{within}}}
\]
where \(\sigma^2_{\text{between}}\) is the variance in group means, and \(\sigma^2_{\text{within}}\) is the variance of individual observations around their group mean.






NoteInterpreting ICC Values




ICC ≈ 0: Knowing which group an observation is in tells you almost nothing. Group membership barely predicts the outcome. Nesting is present but weak.
ICC ≈ 0.05–0.15: Typical for many psychological studies. Modest clustering. Looks small but has real consequences for inference.
ICC ≈ 0.20–0.40: Strong clustering. Common in organizational data (employees within firms), educational data (students within schools), or within-person repeated measures.
ICC ≈ 1.0: All variance is between groups. Observations within a group are identical. Knowing the group tells you everything.



Let’s see three cases:


Code
# Generate data with three different levels of ICC
make_grouped_data <- function(icc, n_groups = 10, n_per_group = 20) {
  sigma_between <- sqrt(icc)
  sigma_within  <- sqrt(1 - icc)

  group_means <- rnorm(n_groups, 0, sigma_between)

  expand_grid(group = 1:n_groups, obs = 1:n_per_group) |>
    mutate(
      y   = group_means[group] + rnorm(n(), 0, sigma_within),
      icc_label = paste0("ICC = ", icc)
    )
}

df_icc <- bind_rows(
  make_grouped_data(0.02),
  make_grouped_data(0.20),
  make_grouped_data(0.60)
)

ggplot(df_icc, aes(x = factor(group), y = y, color = factor(group))) +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.2, alpha = 0.5, size = 1.5) +
  stat_summary(fun = mean, geom = "crossbar", width = 0.5,
               color = "black", linewidth = 0.8) +
  facet_wrap(~ icc_label, ncol = 3) +
  scale_color_viridis_d() +
  labs(
    title    = "Same data, different ICCs",
    subtitle = "Black bars = group means; dots = individual observations",
    x        = "Group",
    y        = "Outcome"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")









When ICC = 0.02, the group means barely differ — the groups look almost interchangeable. When ICC = 0.60, the group means dominate: knowing which group you are in tells you most of what you need to know about someone’s score.

title: 5  Measuring Non-Independence
section: 6.3 How to Compute the ICC in R
text:
6.3 How to Compute the ICC in R
The ICC comes directly from a null (intercept-only) multilevel model — one with no predictors, just a random intercept for the grouping variable. The model partitions total variance into between-group and within-group components.


Code
# Simulate a dataset: employees nested within firms
n_firms     <- 30
n_employees <- 15

firm_effects <- rnorm(n_firms, 50, 6)   # between-firm variance

df_firms <- expand_grid(
  firm     = 1:n_firms,
  employee = 1:n_employees
) |>
  mutate(
    satisfaction = firm_effects[firm] + rnorm(n(), 0, 8)  # within-firm variance
  )

# Fit the null model
null_model <- lmer(satisfaction ~ 1 + (1 | firm), data = df_firms)
summary(null_model)


Linear mixed model fit by REML ['lmerMod']
Formula: satisfaction ~ 1 + (1 | firm)
   Data: df_firms

REML criterion at convergence: 3209

Scaled residuals: 
    Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
-3.1665 -0.6342 -0.0344  0.6918  2.6737 

Random effects:
 Groups   Name        Variance Std.Dev.
 firm     (Intercept) 21.24    4.609   
 Residual             65.45    8.090   
Number of obs: 450, groups:  firm, 30

Fixed effects:
            Estimate Std. Error t value
(Intercept)  49.8255     0.9239   53.93




Code
# Extract variance components
vc <- as.data.frame(VarCorr(null_model))
var_between <- vc$vcov[1]   # firm-level variance
var_within  <- vc$vcov[2]   # residual variance

icc_value <- var_between / (var_between + var_within)
cat("ICC =", round(icc_value, 3))


ICC = 0.245


The performance package provides a cleaner interface:


Code
library(performance)
icc(null_model)






ICC_adjusted
ICC_unadjusted
optional




0.2450301
0.2450301
FALSE

title: 5  Measuring Non-Independence
section: 6.4 Why Even a Small ICC is a Big Problem
text:
6.4 Why Even a Small ICC is a Big Problem
This is one of the most important things to understand before you run any analysis on nested data: even a modest ICC has large consequences for inference.
The reason is the design effect (sometimes called DEFF): a multiplier that tells you how much your effective sample size shrinks due to clustering.
\[
\text{DEFF} = 1 + (n_{\text{cluster}} - 1) \times \text{ICC}
\]
where \(n_{\text{cluster}}\) is the number of observations per cluster.


Code
# Design effect as a function of ICC and cluster size
deff_df <- expand_grid(
  icc          = c(0.01, 0.05, 0.10, 0.20, 0.30),
  cluster_size = c(5, 10, 20, 50)
) |>
  mutate(
    deff           = 1 + (cluster_size - 1) * icc,
    effective_n    = 1000 / deff,  # if nominal N = 1000
    label          = paste0("n = ", cluster_size)
  )

ggplot(deff_df, aes(x = icc, y = deff, color = factor(cluster_size),
                     linetype = factor(cluster_size))) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(size = 2.5) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set2", name = "Cluster size") +
  scale_linetype_discrete(name = "Cluster size") +
  labs(
    title    = "Design Effect as a function of ICC and cluster size",
    subtitle = "DEFF = 1 means no clustering penalty; DEFF = 5 means your 1000 obs act like 200",
    x        = "ICC",
    y        = "Design Effect (DEFF)",
    caption  = "DEFF = 1 + (cluster_size - 1) × ICC"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)









Let’s make this concrete. Suppose you have 200 students in 10 classrooms of 20 students each, and the ICC on your outcome is 0.10 — a seemingly small value often found in educational research.
\[
\text{DEFF} = 1 + (20 - 1) \times 0.10 = 1 + 1.9 = 2.9
\]
Your 200 observations act like only \(200 / 2.9 \approx 69\) independent observations. You have nearly three times less statistical power than you thought. If you treat 200 clustered observations as 200 independent ones, your standard errors are too small, your p-values are too small, and your confidence intervals are too narrow.


Code
# Simulate Type I error inflation from ignoring clustering
simulate_inflation <- function(icc, n_groups = 20, n_per_group = 10, n_sims = 2000) {
  sigma_between <- sqrt(icc)
  sigma_within  <- sqrt(1 - icc)

  p_values <- replicate(n_sims, {
    group_effects <- rnorm(n_groups, 0, sigma_between)
    y <- rep(group_effects, each = n_per_group) +
         rnorm(n_groups * n_per_group, 0, sigma_within)
    x <- rnorm(n_groups * n_per_group)
    t.test(y[x > 0], y[x <= 0])$p.value
  })

  mean(p_values < 0.05)
}

icc_grid <- c(0, 0.05, 0.10, 0.20, 0.30)
type1_rates <- sapply(icc_grid, simulate_inflation)

tibble(ICC = icc_grid, `False Positive Rate` = type1_rates) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
               caption = "Type I error rate when ignoring clustering (nominal alpha = 0.05)")



Type I error rate when ignoring clustering (nominal alpha = 0.05)


ICC
False Positive Rate




0.00
0.052


0.05
0.054


0.10
0.053


0.20
0.052


0.30
0.060





When ICC = 0, ignoring clustering is fine — you get the expected 5% false positive rate. But as ICC grows, so does your false positive rate. At ICC = 0.20, you are rejecting the null hypothesis incorrectly nearly twice as often as you should be.

title: 5  Measuring Non-Independence
text:
6.5 Assumptions of the ICC
The ICC is not just a number — it embodies assumptions. Checking those assumptions is part of using it correctly.






WarningAssumption 1: Groups are exchangeable



The ICC assumes that groups are drawn from a common population, and that the relationship between observations within a group reflects a common process. If your groups are wildly different in kind — not just in degree — the ICC may not characterize the nesting correctly.
How to check: Look at group means and within-group variances. Are any groups wildly different from the rest? Could the nesting structure be non-stationary (e.g., the within-group correlation is strong in some groups, absent in others)?








WarningAssumption 2: The grouping variable captures the relevant nesting



A single ICC assumes there is one meaningful level of nesting. If data is actually three-level (observations in therapists in clinics), a single ICC from a two-level model will be misspecified.
How to check: Fit null models at each level and compute ICCs for each. Compare the variance components.








WarningAssumption 3: The ICC is stable across groups



A standard ICC treats within-group variance as homogeneous across groups. In reality, some groups may be much more variable than others (heteroskedasticity at level 2).
How to check: Plot within-group standard deviations against group means. A strong relationship suggests heteroskedasticity.




Code
# Check: is within-group variance stable?
df_firms |>
  group_by(firm) |>
  summarise(
    group_mean = mean(satisfaction),
    group_sd   = sd(satisfaction),
    n          = n()
  ) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = group_mean, y = group_sd)) +
  geom_point(size = 3, color = "#1e3a5f", alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_smooth(method = "loess", se = TRUE, color = "#e63946") +
  labs(
    title    = "Within-group SD vs. group mean",
    subtitle = "A strong relationship suggests heteroskedastic nesting",
    x        = "Group Mean (Satisfaction)",
    y        = "Within-Group SD"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)









In this simulated dataset, there is no strong relationship — within-group variance is roughly constant across firms. That is reassuring, but not always the case in real data.

title: 5  Measuring Non-Independence
section: 6.6 The ICC as a Scientific Finding
text:
6.6 The ICC as a Scientific Finding
Throughout this workshop, we treat the ICC primarily as a diagnostic tool — something you check before deciding what model to use. But it is also a finding in its own right.
If your outcome is job satisfaction and the ICC across firms is 0.30, that means 30% of the variance in job satisfaction is explained simply by which firm someone works for. That is a substantive result. It tells you that organizational-level factors — culture, management, pay structure — explain nearly a third of the variation in how people feel about their work. That is worth reporting, and worth investigating.
The decomposition of variance across levels is one of the most underutilized findings in social science research.

title: 6  Data Structure and Format
text: 7 Data Structure and Format

title: 6  Data Structure and Format
text:
7.1 The Shape of Nested Data
Before fitting any model, you need your data in the right shape. Nested data analysis requires long format: one row per observation at the lowest level of the hierarchy, with identifier variables that track higher-level units.
This sounds simple. In practice, it trips up a surprising number of researchers.

title: 6  Data Structure and Format
text:
7.2 Long Format vs. Wide Format
Most people who have worked with repeated measures data in SPSS or Excel have encountered data in wide format: one row per person, with multiple columns for each time point or condition.


Code
# Wide format example
df_wide <- tibble(
  person_id = 1:6,
  sex       = c("F", "M", "F", "F", "M", "M"),
  age       = c(23, 31, 27, 25, 29, 34),
  mood_day1 = c(65, 72, 58, 70, 63, 55),
  mood_day2 = c(68, 69, 61, 74, 60, 58),
  mood_day3 = c(71, 67, 64, 72, 65, 62),
  mood_day4 = c(66, 74, 59, 75, 61, 57),
  mood_day5 = c(70, 70, 63, 78, 64, 60)
)

knitr::kable(df_wide, caption = "Wide format: 6 people × 5 days = 6 rows")

This format feels intuitive — it keeps all information about one person on one row. But it is not suitable for multilevel modeling. Why?
Because the model needs to see the nesting explicitly. It needs to know that mood_day1, mood_day2, and mood_day3 belong to the same person — that they are three realizations of the same level-1 unit nested inside one level-2 unit.
Long format makes the nesting explicit:


Code
df_long <- df_wide |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols      = starts_with("mood_"),
    names_to  = "day",
    values_to = "mood"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    day = as.integer(str_remove(day, "mood_day"))
  ) |>
  select(person_id, sex, age, day, mood)

title: 6  Data Structure and Format
section: 7.3 Converting Wide to Long in R

title: 6  Data Structure and Format
text:
7.4 The Essential Identifier Variables
Every nested dataset needs at least two types of identifier variables:

Level-2 identifier: A unique code for each higher-level unit (person, firm, classroom, country). This is what tells the model which observations belong together.
Level-1 identifier or index: A variable that distinguishes observations within the same level-2 unit (day number, condition, trial number).

In three-level data, you need identifiers at all three levels.






ImportantChecklist: Preparing Nested Data



Before fitting any model, verify:

One row per level-1 observation (not one row per person, unless you have only cross-sectional nesting)
A unique, clean level-2 ID (numeric or factor, no missing values, consistent coding)
A level-1 index within each level-2 unit (day number, trial number, condition)
Level-2 variables appear on every row for that unit (age, sex, and other person-level variables are repeated across all a person’s rows)
No structural missingness (if some observations are genuinely missing vs. structurally absent, treat them differently)
Balanced or unbalanced — you know which (lme4 handles both, but you should know what you have)

text:

title: 6  Data Structure and Format
section: 7.6 A Note on Balanced vs. Unbalanced Data
text:
7.6 A Note on Balanced vs. Unbalanced Data
Balanced data means every level-2 unit has the same number of level-1 observations. All 30 people have exactly 5 days of data. All 25 classrooms have exactly 20 students.
Unbalanced data means the number of observations varies across level-2 units. Some people completed 7 days of the diary; others dropped out after 2. Some classrooms have 15 students; others have 28.
Standard ANOVA-based approaches for repeated measures struggle with unbalanced data — they were designed for balanced designs. Mixed-effects models handle unbalanced data gracefully, because they use the information available for each unit without requiring complete cases.


Code
# Show what unbalanced data looks like
df_unbalanced <- df_long |>
  # randomly drop some observations to simulate dropout
  filter(!(person_id %in% c(1, 4) & day >= 4),
         !(person_id == 6 & day >= 3))

df_unbalanced |>
  group_by(person_id) |>
  summarise(n_obs = n(), days_observed = paste(day, collapse = ", ")) |>
  knitr::kable(caption = "Unbalanced data: some people have fewer observations")

title: 6  Data Structure and Format
text:
7.7 Diagnosing Your Data Structure
Before fitting any model, it is good practice to run a quick structure check:


Code
# How many observations per person?
df_long |>
  count(person_id, name = "n_obs") |>
  summary()


   person_id        n_obs  
 Min.   :1.00   Min.   :5  
 1st Qu.:2.25   1st Qu.:5  
 Median :3.50   Median :5  
 Mean   :3.50   Mean   :5  
 3rd Qu.:4.75   3rd Qu.:5  
 Max.   :6.00   Max.   :5  


Code
# Is the data truly in long format? Check that level-2 variables don't vary within person
df_long |>
  group_by(person_id) |>
  summarise(
    n_unique_sex = n_distinct(sex),   # should be 1
    n_unique_age = n_distinct(age)    # should be 1
  ) |>
  filter(n_unique_sex > 1 | n_unique_age > 1)  # should return 0 rows






person_id
n_unique_sex
n_unique_age








Zero rows returned means no person has inconsistent sex or age coding across their observations. Good.


Code
# How many level-2 units?
n_distinct(df_long$person_id)


[1] 6


Code
# What is the range of level-1 observations per unit?
df_long |>
  count(person_id) |>
  pull(n) |>
  range()


[1] 5 5


This takes two minutes and saves hours of debugging later.

title: 6  Data Structure and Format
text:
7.8 Looking Ahead
With the conceptual foundations in place — DGP thinking, types of nesting, ICC, and data format — we are ready to start fitting models. Module 1 builds the core toolkit: fixed effects, random intercepts, and random slopes. At each step, we will be explicit about what assumption the model is making and what happens when that assumption is wrong.

title: 7  Overview
text: Module 1: The Mixed-Effects Framework

title: 7  Overview
text:
7.1 The Toolkit
Module 0 established that your data is almost always nested and that ignoring nesting inflates Type I error, shrinks effective sample sizes, and obscures the true data-generating process. Now we build the tools to do something about it.
The mixed-effects model is the central workhorse of nested data analysis. It is called “mixed” because it combines two types of effects:

Fixed effects — parameters that apply to the entire population. The average relationship between sleep and mood. The overall effect of a treatment.
Random effects — parameters that vary across units (people, firms, classrooms) and are treated as drawn from a distribution rather than estimated individually.

This combination lets the model do something powerful: borrow strength across units. A student who was absent for half the year still contributes information about their classroom. A person who completed only 2 days of a diary still informs estimates of the group average. The model uses all available information, weighted by how much each unit tells us.

title: 7  Overview
text:
7.2 How This Module is Organized
The module builds in steps, each adding one layer of complexity while being explicit about what assumption is being made and what assumption is being relaxed.
Part 1: Fixed Effects introduces the fixed-effects estimator — the simplest approach to handling nesting. It is easy to understand and makes strong assumptions. We examine each assumption carefully and show when it fails.
Part 2: Random Intercepts introduces the random-intercept mixed model: the most common multilevel model in psychology. We show how it differs from fixed effects, what it assumes, and how to check those assumptions.
Part 3: Random Slopes adds the most important extension: allowing the relationship between a predictor and outcome to vary across units. We discuss when this matters and when it does not.
Part 4: Fixed vs. Random — When to Use Which addresses one of the most frequently asked questions in applied multilevel analysis. The answer involves the scientific question, the data structure, and several testable assumptions.
Part 5: Three-Level Structures extends the framework to three levels of nesting and introduces the challenge of variance decomposition across levels.
Part 6: Crossed Random Effects covers the case where observations belong to multiple grouping factors simultaneously — the structure that arises naturally in most psychological experiments.

title: 7  Overview
text:
7.3 Core Assumptions
Every model in this module makes assumptions. We will state them explicitly at each step. For now, here is the set of assumptions that span the entire module:






ImportantAssumptions of the Mixed-Effects Framework




Linearity: The relationship between predictors and the outcome is linear (at each level)
Normality of random effects: The random effects are drawn from a normal distribution
Normality of residuals: Level-1 residuals are normally distributed
Homoskedasticity: The variance of residuals is constant across units and predictor values
Independence of residuals: Level-1 residuals are independent after conditioning on the random effects
Independence of random effects from predictors: (for random effects models) The random effects are uncorrelated with the fixed-effect predictors

Not all of these apply to all models. Fixed-effects models, for instance, do not require Assumption 6. Understanding which assumptions apply to which model — and how to check each one — is the goal of this module.

title: 8  Fixed Effects Models
text: 9 Fixed Effects Models

title: 8  Fixed Effects Models
text:
9.1 Two Settings, One Problem
Fixed effects models are usually introduced in the context of panel data — the same firms tracked over multiple years, the same countries tracked across decades. But the exact same logic applies whenever observations are nested within units, regardless of whether time is involved.
Consider two studies:
Study A (Longitudinal): You survey 500 employees across 25 firms about autonomy and job satisfaction — and you do it again the following year. Each employee is measured twice.
Study B (Experimental): You run a within-subjects experiment. Each of 80 participants evaluates 12 product descriptions — 6 under a “scarcity” framing (“only 3 left!”) and 6 under a neutral framing. Each participant sees both conditions.
Both studies share the same fundamental structure: multiple observations nested within the same unit (employee, participant). And both share the same fundamental problem: unit-level differences confound your estimate of the effect you care about.
In Study A, high-autonomy firms also happen to have better cultures — so the autonomy effect is confounded with culture.
In Study B, some participants are just more easily persuaded than others — so the framing effect is confounded with individual persuadability.
Fixed effects solve both problems in exactly the same way.

title: 8  Fixed Effects Models
text:
9.2 The Model
Let \(Y_{ij}\) be the outcome for observation \(i\) within unit \(j\) (employee \(i\) in firm \(j\); rating \(i\) by participant \(j\)). The fixed-effects model is:
\[
\underbrace{Y_{ij}}_{\text{outcome}} = \underbrace{\alpha_j}_{\substack{\text{unit fixed} \\ \text{effect}}} + \underbrace{\beta_1}_{\substack{\text{within-unit} \\ \text{slope}}} \underbrace{X_{ij}}_{\substack{\text{within-unit} \\ \text{predictor}}} + \underbrace{\varepsilon_{ij}}_{\substack{\text{residual}}}
\]
The \(\alpha_j\) is a separate intercept for every unit — a dummy variable for firm \(j\), or a dummy variable for participant \(j\). It absorbs everything that is constant within that unit: the firm’s culture, the participant’s baseline persuadability, the country’s institutions.
The slope \(\beta_1\) is estimated purely from within-unit variation: how does \(Y\) change when \(X\) changes within the same unit?

9.2.1 The Within-Demeaning Transformation
An equivalent way to see what FE does is through demeaning. Let \(\bar{Y}_j = \frac{1}{n_j}\sum_i Y_{ij}\) be the unit mean. Subtracting the unit mean from every observation:
\[
\underbrace{(Y_{ij} - \bar{Y}_j)}_{\substack{\text{outcome} \\ \text{demeaned}}} = \beta_1 \underbrace{(X_{ij} - \bar{X}_j)}_{\substack{\text{predictor} \\ \text{demeaned}}} + \underbrace{(\varepsilon_{ij} - \bar{\varepsilon}_j)}_{\substack{\text{residual} \\ \text{demeaned}}}
\]
After demeaning, every trace of between-unit variation is gone. The \(\alpha_j\) terms vanish completely — they cancel out when you subtract the unit mean from both sides. What remains is the pure within-unit relationship between \(X\) and \(Y\).
In the firm study: we are comparing each employee to the average employee in their own firm.
In the experiment: we are comparing each participant’s rating under scarcity to their own average rating — which is precisely what within-subjects experimental designs intend.

title: 8  Fixed Effects Models
text:
9.3 Two Examples: Longitudinal and Experimental

9.3.1 Example 1: Firms (Longitudinal Cross-Section)


Code
# --- STUDY A: Employees nested within firms ---
n_firms     <- 25
n_employees <- 20

# The confound: high-autonomy firms also have better culture
firm_autonomy_mean  <- rnorm(n_firms, 50, 10)
firm_culture_effect <- firm_autonomy_mean * 0.4 + rnorm(n_firms, 0, 5)

df_firms <- expand_grid(
  firm     = 1:n_firms,
  employee = 1:n_employees
) |>
  mutate(
    firm_autonomy = firm_autonomy_mean[firm],
    firm_culture  = firm_culture_effect[firm],
    autonomy      = firm_autonomy + rnorm(n(), 0, 5),
    # True WITHIN-firm effect = 0.2; but between-firm confound inflates it
    satisfaction  = 30 + 0.2 * autonomy + firm_culture + rnorm(n(), 0, 8),
    firm_label    = factor(firm)
  )

# Naive pooled regression
naive_firms <- lm(satisfaction ~ autonomy, data = df_firms)
broom::tidy(naive_firms) |>
  filter(term == "autonomy") |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "Pooled OLS: estimated effect of autonomy (confounded)")



Pooled OLS: estimated effect of autonomy (confounded)


term
estimate
std.error
statistic
p.value




autonomy
0.433
0.032
13.375
0







9.3.2 Example 2: Within-Subjects Experiment


Code
# --- STUDY B: Ratings nested within participants ---
n_participants <- 80
n_ratings      <- 12   # 6 scarcity, 6 neutral

# The confound: participants differ in baseline persuadability
persuadability <- rnorm(n_participants, 50, 10)

df_exp <- expand_grid(
  participant = 1:n_participants,
  rating_num  = 1:n_ratings
) |>
  mutate(
    condition    = rep(c("scarcity", "neutral"), n_ratings / 2)[rating_num],
    scarcity_bin = as.integer(condition == "scarcity"),
    # True WITHIN-person effect of scarcity = 4 points
    # But persuadability (a person-level trait) also drives ratings
    purchase_intent = persuadability[participant] + 4 * scarcity_bin +
                      rnorm(n(), 0, 8),
    participant_f   = factor(participant)
  )

# Naive pooled regression: mixes within-person and between-person variation
naive_exp <- lm(purchase_intent ~ scarcity_bin, data = df_exp)
broom::tidy(naive_exp) |>
  filter(term == "scarcity_bin") |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "Pooled OLS: estimated scarcity effect (ignores person differences)")



Pooled OLS: estimated scarcity effect (ignores person differences)


term
estimate
std.error
statistic
p.value




scarcity_bin
3.286
0.76
4.324
0





In the experiment, the pooled estimate is closer to the truth than in the firm study — because condition was randomly assigned, persuadability does not systematically correlate with condition. But random assignment only guarantees this on average; individual differences still add noise and can distort estimates in small samples or unbalanced designs.


Code
# Show both confound patterns side by side
firm_summary <- df_firms |>
  group_by(firm) |>
  summarise(mean_auto = mean(autonomy), mean_sat = mean(satisfaction))

p1 <- ggplot(df_firms, aes(x = autonomy, y = satisfaction)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.15, color = "#1e3a5f") +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = "#e63946", linewidth = 1.3) +
  geom_point(data = firm_summary, aes(x = mean_auto, y = mean_sat),
             color = "#e63946", size = 3) +
  labs(title = "Study A: Firms",
       subtitle = "Red dots = firm means; slope is inflated by culture confound",
       x = "Autonomy", y = "Satisfaction") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

person_summary <- df_exp |>
  group_by(participant, condition) |>
  summarise(mean_intent = mean(purchase_intent), .groups = "drop")

p2 <- ggplot(df_exp, aes(x = scarcity_bin, y = purchase_intent,
                          color = participant_f, group = participant_f)) +
  geom_line(alpha = 0.3, linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.2, size = 0.8) +
  stat_summary(aes(group = 1), fun = mean, geom = "line",
               color = "#e63946", linewidth = 1.5) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = c(0, 1),
                     labels = c("Neutral", "Scarcity")) +
  labs(title = "Study B: Experiment",
       subtitle = "Each line = one participant across conditions",
       x = "Condition", y = "Purchase Intent") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "none") +
  scale_color_viridis_d()

p1 + p2

title: 8  Fixed Effects Models
text:
9.4 How Fixed Effects Solve This
Fixed effects recover the within-unit slope by including a dummy for every unit. In the firm study that is 25 firm dummies. In the experiment it is 80 participant dummies. Each dummy absorbs everything that is constant within that unit.


Code
# Study A: FE via plm
df_firms_pdata <- pdata.frame(df_firms, index = c("firm", "employee"))
fe_firms <- plm(satisfaction ~ autonomy,
                data   = df_firms_pdata,
                model  = "within",
                effect = "individual")

# Study B: FE via lm with participant dummies (equivalent)
fe_exp <- lm(purchase_intent ~ scarcity_bin + participant_f, data = df_exp)




Code
# Compare naive vs. FE for both studies
bind_rows(
  broom::tidy(naive_firms)  |> filter(term == "autonomy") |>
    mutate(study = "Study A", model = "Pooled OLS", term = "Predictor"),
  broom::tidy(lm(satisfaction ~ autonomy + firm_label, data = df_firms)) |>
    filter(term == "autonomy") |>
    mutate(study = "Study A", model = "Fixed Effects", term = "Predictor"),
  broom::tidy(naive_exp)  |> filter(term == "scarcity_bin") |>
    mutate(study = "Study B", model = "Pooled OLS", term = "Predictor"),
  broom::tidy(fe_exp) |> filter(term == "scarcity_bin") |>
    mutate(study = "Study B", model = "Fixed Effects", term = "Predictor")
) |>
  select(study, model, estimate, std.error, p.value) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "Pooled vs. Fixed Effects: Study A (true effect = 0.2), Study B (true effect = 4)")



Pooled vs. Fixed Effects: Study A (true effect = 0.2), Study B (true effect = 4)

NoteThe DGP Connection



In Study A, the pooled estimate is badly confounded — it reflects both the true autonomy effect (0.2) and the between-firm culture effect. Fixed effects remove the latter, recovering the within-unit DGP.
In Study B, the pooled estimate is closer to the truth because random assignment broke the correlation between condition and persuadability. But fixed effects still improve efficiency and explicitly answer the within-person question: for the same participant, does scarcity increase intent?
Both uses are valid. The experimental use is often overlooked — but within-subjects ANOVA is, mathematically, a fixed-effects model.

title: 8  Fixed Effects Models
section: 9.5 The Assumptions of Fixed Effects Models
text:
9.5 The Assumptions of Fixed Effects Models
Each assumption is framed for both a longitudinal/observational setting and an experimental/repeated-measures setting.






WarningAssumption 1: Strict Exogeneity



The equation implication: For the within-demeaned estimator to be unbiased, we require:
\[E[\varepsilon_{ij} \mid X_{i1}, X_{i2}, \ldots, X_{in_j}, \alpha_j] = 0\]
In words: the residuals must be uncorrelated with the predictor at every observation point — past, present, and future — after conditioning on the fixed effect.
In longitudinal data: This fails if the outcome feeds back into the predictor. If a firm’s declining satisfaction causes it to increase autonomy in the next period, then autonomy at time \(t+1\) is correlated with the residual at time \(t\). The predictor is no longer exogenous.
In experiments: Strict exogeneity is typically satisfied by design — random assignment ensures that condition is not caused by the outcome. But it can fail in carryover designs where the treatment in one block affects the outcome in a later block (i.e., order effects or fatigue). Counterbalancing and washout periods address this.
How to check: In longitudinal data, plot residuals against future values of the predictor. In experiments, check for order effects by including trial number as a covariate.








WarningAssumption 2: No Time-Varying (or Trial-Varying) Confounds



What it means: Fixed effects only remove stable unit-level confounds. If a confound changes across observations within the same unit, the fixed effect cannot absorb it.
\[\alpha_j = \text{constant over all } i \text{ within unit } j\]
If the unit’s “true level” shifts — a firm’s culture changes after a merger; a participant becomes fatigued midway through a session — the single \(\alpha_j\) is no longer a valid representation.
In longitudinal data: Any policy, economic shock, or personal event that co-occurs with your predictor is a time-varying confound. Example: if firms that grant more autonomy simultaneously adopt flexible hours, and flexible hours boost satisfaction, FE does not remove the flexible hours effect.
In experiments: Practice effects, fatigue, and demand characteristics that change systematically across trials. If participants become better at evaluating products as the experiment progresses (learning), and if scarcity items happen to appear later in the session, this is a trial-varying confound.
How to check: Include trial order or time as a covariate. Check whether your substantive estimates change when you control for order.








WarningAssumption 3: Sufficient Within-Unit Variation



What it means: Fixed effects can only estimate \(\beta_1\) from within-unit variation. If the predictor barely changes within units, the estimator is imprecise:
\[\widehat{\beta}_1 = \frac{\sum_j \sum_i (X_{ij} - \bar{X}_j)(Y_{ij} - \bar{Y}_j)}{\sum_j \sum_i (X_{ij} - \bar{X}_j)^2}\]
The denominator — total within-unit variation in \(X\) — appears in the denominator of the variance of \(\hat\beta_1\). Small within-unit variation → large standard errors.
In longitudinal data: A predictor like firm size barely changes within a firm from year to year. FE is poorly suited to estimating the effect of stable characteristics.
In experiments: This is usually not an issue because condition is deliberately manipulated. But if the manipulation is very small (e.g., a subtle framing tweak that most participants do not notice), within-unit variation in the effective treatment is low.


Code
# Study A: how much within-firm variation does autonomy have?
df_firms |>
  group_by(firm) |>
  summarise(within_sd = sd(autonomy)) |>
  summarise(
    mean_within_sd   = mean(within_sd),
    total_sd         = sd(df_firms$autonomy),
    within_fraction  = mean(within_sd) / sd(df_firms$autonomy)
  ) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "Study A: fraction of autonomy variance that is within-firm")



Study A: fraction of autonomy variance that is within-firm


mean_within_sd
total_sd
within_fraction




4.827
12.363
0.39













WarningAssumption 4: Fixed Effects Are Stable (One Intercept Per Unit)



What it means: The model assigns each unit exactly one intercept \(\alpha_j\). This assumes the unit’s baseline is constant across all its observations. The model equation is:
\[Y_{ij} = \alpha_j + \beta_1 X_{ij} + \varepsilon_{ij}\]
and \(\alpha_j\) is the same regardless of which observation \(i\) we are looking at within unit \(j\).
In longitudinal data: If a firm’s culture changes dramatically after a merger halfway through the study, the single \(\alpha_j\) no longer accurately represents either period. The early-period and late-period intercepts are different, but the model conflates them.
In experiments: If a participant’s attention or mood changes substantially mid-session (and this change is not modeled), the stable-intercept assumption is violated. Split-session designs or attention checks help detect this.
How to check: For longitudinal data, split the sample at the midpoint and compare unit intercepts across periods. For experiments, split the trials into first-half and second-half blocks and test whether the effect estimate shifts.








WarningAssumption 5: Homoskedasticity and Independence of Residuals Within Units



What it means: After conditioning on fixed effects, the residuals \(\varepsilon_{ij}\) are assumed to be independent and identically distributed:
\[\varepsilon_{ij} \overset{iid}{\sim} \mathcal{N}(0,\ \sigma^2_\varepsilon)\]
Two components: (1) constant variance across units and observations (homoskedasticity), and (2) no correlation between residuals of the same unit across observations (independence).
In longitudinal data: Serial correlation is nearly universal. Today’s mood affects tomorrow’s mood, so residuals at \(t\) and \(t+1\) are correlated. Heteroskedasticity also appears if variance changes over time (e.g., outcomes become more variable in later periods).
In experiments: Autocorrelation is less severe for blocked designs (participants evaluate each product once), but can appear in trial-level data with carryover. Heteroskedasticity is possible if some conditions produce more variable responses than others.
What to do: Use cluster-robust standard errors, which are valid even under heteroskedasticity and within-unit correlation.


Code
fe_lm_firms <- lm(satisfaction ~ autonomy + firm_label, data = df_firms)
df_firms <- df_firms |> mutate(fe_resid = residuals(fe_lm_firms))

ggplot(df_firms, aes(x = autonomy, y = fe_resid)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.25, color = "#1e3a5f") +
  geom_smooth(method = "loess", color = "#e63946", se = TRUE) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(
    title    = "Study A: FE residuals vs. autonomy",
    subtitle = "No pattern expected if exogeneity holds",
    x = "Autonomy", y = "FE Residual"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)











Code
library(sandwich)
library(lmtest)

# Cluster-robust standard errors for Study A
coeftest(fe_lm_firms,
         vcov = vcovCL(fe_lm_firms, cluster = ~firm_label))["autonomy", , drop = FALSE] |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "Study A autonomy coefficient with cluster-robust SEs")



Study A autonomy coefficient with cluster-robust SEs



Estimate
Std. Error
t value
Pr(>|t|)

Code
# For Study B, cluster by participant
coeftest(fe_exp,
         vcov = vcovCL(fe_exp, cluster = ~participant_f))["scarcity_bin", , drop = FALSE] |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "Study B scarcity coefficient with cluster-robust SEs")

title: 8  Fixed Effects Models
text:
9.6 What Fixed Effects Cannot Do
Fixed effects are powerful but have important limits that apply equally in both settings:

They cannot estimate unit-level predictors. In Study A, you cannot estimate the effect of firm industry (constant within firm). In Study B, you cannot estimate the effect of participant gender (constant within participant). All between-unit variation is absorbed by the \(\alpha_j\) terms.
They discard between-unit information. If the between-unit relationship is scientifically interesting — whether more autonomous firms differ from less autonomous ones — FE discards that comparison. This is the price of controlling for all stable unit-level confounds.
They assume a common slope. The model estimates one \(\beta_1\) for everyone. If the effect of autonomy on satisfaction differs across firms, or if the scarcity effect differs across participants, FE averages over this heterogeneity. Random slopes (Module 1, Part 3) address this.
They are not well suited to purely between-subjects designs. If every participant sees only one condition (between-subjects), there is no within-person variation in the predictor — FE collapses entirely to OLS. The fixed-effects logic requires that the predictor varies within units.

title: 8  Fixed Effects Models
text:
9.7 Recommended Reading







Topic
Citation




Fixed effects and panel data
Wooldridge, J. M. (2010). Econometric Analysis of Cross Section and Panel Data (2nd ed.). MIT Press. (Chapter 10)


FE in experimental psychology
Judd, C. M., Westfall, J., & Kenny, D. A. (2012). Treating stimuli as a random factor in social psychology. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 103(1), 54–69.


Two-way fixed effects
Imai, K., & Kim, I. S. (2021). On the use of two-way fixed effects regression models for causal inference with panel data. Political Analysis, 29(3), 405–415.


Cluster-robust standard errors
Cameron, A. C., & Miller, D. L. (2015). A practitioner’s guide to cluster-robust inference. Journal of Human Resources, 50(2), 317–372.

title: 9  Random Intercepts
text: 10 Random Intercepts

title: 9  Random Intercepts
text:
10.1 Beyond Absorbing Unit Differences
Fixed effects remove between-unit variation by absorbing it into dummy variables. That works, but it destroys information:

You can no longer estimate the effect of unit-level predictors (firm size, participant trait anxiety)
You cannot make predictions for new units not in your sample
You learn nothing about how much units differ from each other

Random intercepts take a different view. Instead of asking “what is the specific intercept of unit \(j\)?”, the model asks “what is the distribution of intercepts across all units in the population?” It treats unit-level deviations as draws from a probability distribution rather than as fixed constants to be estimated.
This single shift — from “estimate each intercept” to “estimate the distribution of intercepts” — has wide-reaching consequences for inference, prediction, and interpretation.

title: 9  Random Intercepts
section: 10.2 The Model in Two Equivalent Forms
text:
10.2 The Model in Two Equivalent Forms

10.2.1 Two-Level Notation
Random intercept models are most naturally described in two levels.
Level 1 describes variation within units (within the same person across days; within the same participant across stimuli):
\[\underbrace{Y_{ij}}_{\substack{\text{outcome for} \\ \text{obs } i \text{ in} \\ \text{unit } j}} = \underbrace{\beta_{0j}}_{\substack{\text{unit-specific} \\ \text{intercept}}} + \underbrace{\beta_1}_{\substack{\text{slope}\\ \text{(fixed)}}} \underbrace{X_{ij}}_{\substack{\text{predictor}}} + \underbrace{\varepsilon_{ij}}_{\substack{\text{within-unit} \\ \text{residual}}}\]
Level 2 describes variation between units — it models where each unit’s intercept comes from:
\[\underbrace{\beta_{0j}}_{\substack{\text{unit-specific} \\ \text{intercept}}} = \underbrace{\gamma_{00}}_{\substack{\text{grand mean} \\ \text{intercept}}} + \underbrace{u_{0j}}_{\substack{\text{unit deviation} \\ \text{from grand mean}}}\]
where \(u_{0j} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2_u)\) — the unit deviations are assumed normally distributed with mean zero and variance \(\sigma^2_u\).


10.2.2 Combined (Single-Equation) Form
Substituting Level 2 into Level 1, we get the combined model. Notice how the Level-1 intercept \(\beta_{0j}\) is replaced by its Level-2 expression:
\[\underbrace{Y_{ij}}_{\text{outcome}} = \underbrace{\gamma_{00}}_{\substack{\text{grand mean} \\ \text{(fixed)}}} + \underbrace{u_{0j}}_{\substack{\text{unit random} \\ \text{intercept}}} + \underbrace{\beta_1 X_{ij}}_{\substack{\text{slope}\\ \text{(fixed)}}} + \underbrace{\varepsilon_{ij}}_{\substack{\text{within-unit} \\ \text{residual}}}\]
The model has two types of components:









Component
Symbol
Type
What it captures




Grand mean
\(\gamma_{00}\)
Fixed
Average baseline across all units


Slope
\(\beta_1\)
Fixed
Average within-unit effect of \(X\)


Unit deviation
\(u_{0j}\)
Random
How much unit \(j\) departs from \(\gamma_{00}\)


Residual
\(\varepsilon_{ij}\)
Random
Leftover within-unit variation



The model estimates three variance parameters: \(\sigma^2_u\) (between-unit variance), \(\sigma^2_\varepsilon\) (within-unit residual variance), and their ratio defines the ICC.


10.2.3 Comparing to Fixed Effects
The FE model was:
\[Y_{ij} = \alpha_j + \beta_1 X_{ij} + \varepsilon_{ij}\]
where \(\alpha_j\) was estimated as a separate free parameter for each unit.
The random-intercept model replaces the free \(\alpha_j\) with \(\gamma_{00} + u_{0j}\), imposing the constraint that unit deviations are drawn from a normal distribution. This constraint is what enables partial pooling (shrinkage) and allows between-unit inference — but it comes with an assumption (normality of the random effects) that the FE model does not require.

title: 9  Random Intercepts
text:
10.3 Two Examples: Longitudinal and Experimental
The random-intercept model is equally natural in longitudinal and experimental settings. The structure is identical; only the substantive interpretation differs.

10.3.1 Example 1: Daily Mood and Sleep (Longitudinal/Repeated Measures)
30 people each complete a mood diary for 7 days. We want to know: within a person, does sleeping more predict better mood the next day?


Code
n_people <- 30
n_days   <- 7

person_intercepts <- rnorm(n_people, mean = 50, sd = 8)

df_sleep <- expand_grid(
  person = 1:n_people,
  day    = 1:n_days
) |>
  mutate(
    sleep  = rnorm(n(), mean = 7, sd = 1.5),
    # True within-person effect of sleep on mood = 3 points per hour
    mood   = person_intercepts[person] + 3 * (sleep - 7) + rnorm(n(), 0, 5),
    person = factor(person)
  )

# Fit random intercept model
model_ri_sleep <- lmer(mood ~ sleep + (1 | person), data = df_sleep)
summary(model_ri_sleep)

Reading the output:

\(\hat\gamma_{00}\) (Intercept fixed effect): the grand mean mood across all people and days
\(\hat\beta_1\) (sleep fixed effect): on average, one extra hour of sleep is associated with ~3 points higher mood, within the same person
\(\hat\sigma_u\) (person random effect SD): how much people differ in their baseline mood
\(\hat\sigma_\varepsilon\) (residual SD): how much a person’s mood varies day-to-day, after accounting for sleep

10.3.2 Example 2: Stimulus Ratings in a Judgment Study (Experimental)
50 participants each rate 20 charity appeal images on their emotional impact (0–100). Half the images feature children; half feature adults. We want to know: does featuring a child vs. an adult increase perceived emotional impact?


Code
n_participants_exp <- 50
n_images           <- 20

# Participants differ in baseline emotionality
participant_baseline <- rnorm(n_participants_exp, mean = 50, sd = 12)

df_charity <- expand_grid(
  participant = 1:n_participants_exp,
  image       = 1:n_images
) |>
  mutate(
    child_subject = rep(c(1, 0), n_images / 2)[image],  # 1 = child, 0 = adult
    # True within-person effect of child subject = 8 points
    impact = participant_baseline[participant] +
             8 * child_subject + rnorm(n(), 0, 10),
    participant = factor(participant)
  )

model_ri_charity <- lmer(impact ~ child_subject + (1 | participant),
                          data = df_charity)

broom.mixed::tidy(model_ri_charity, effects = "fixed") |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3, caption = "Fixed effects: child subject → emotional impact")
