Notice the parallel: in the mood study, \(u_{0j}\) captures person differences in baseline mood. In the charity study, \(u_{0j}\) captures person differences in baseline emotionality. The math is identical. The interpretation is substantively different.


Code
# Visualize random intercepts for both examples
re_sleep   <- ranef(model_ri_sleep)$person[, 1]
re_charity <- ranef(model_ri_charity)$participant[, 1]

p1 <- tibble(unit = seq_along(re_sleep), re = sort(re_sleep)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(unit, re), y = re)) +
  geom_point(color = "#1e3a5f", size = 2) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray50") +
  labs(title = "Study 1: Person random intercepts",
       subtitle = "Each dot = one person's deviation from grand mean mood",
       x = "Person (sorted)", y = expression(hat(u)[0*j])) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) + theme(axis.text.x = element_blank())

p2 <- tibble(unit = seq_along(re_charity), re = sort(re_charity)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(unit, re), y = re)) +
  geom_point(color = "#2d6a4f", size = 2) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray50") +
  labs(title = "Study 2: Participant random intercepts",
       subtitle = "Each dot = one participant's deviation from grand mean emotionality",
       x = "Participant (sorted)", y = expression(hat(u)[0*j])) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) + theme(axis.text.x = element_blank())

p1 + p2

title: 9  Random Intercepts
section: 10.4 The Assumptions of Random Intercept Models
text:
10.4 The Assumptions of Random Intercept Models
The same four assumptions apply to both the longitudinal and the experimental settings. Where the assumption plays out differently across contexts, both are discussed.






WarningAssumption 1: Normality of Random Effects



The distributional claim: The model assumes: \[u_{0j} \overset{iid}{\sim} \mathcal{N}(0,\ \sigma^2_u)\]
This is stronger than the FE model, which makes no distributional claim about the \(\alpha_j\) parameters. Here, the unit deviations are required to be normally distributed around zero.
In longitudinal data: If your units are firms, some industries may have systematically higher satisfaction for structural reasons (not random deviations). A bimodal or skewed distribution of \(u_{0j}\) can arise when there are latent subgroups.
In experiments: If participant emotionality is bimodal (high-empathy vs. low-empathy types), the normality assumption is violated. With many participants (n > 50), the model is reasonably robust to mild non-normality.
How to check:


Code
re_vals <- ranef(model_ri_sleep)$person[, 1]

p_hist <- tibble(re = re_vals) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = re)) +
  geom_histogram(fill = "#deebf7", color = "#1e3a5f", bins = 10) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(title = "Random intercepts: histogram",
       x = expression(hat(u)[0*j]), y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

p_qq <- tibble(re = re_vals) |>
  ggplot(aes(sample = re)) +
  stat_qq(color = "#1e3a5f", alpha = 0.7) +
  stat_qq_line(color = "#e63946", linewidth = 1) +
  labs(title = "Random intercepts: Q-Q plot",
       subtitle = "Points off the line = non-normality",
       x = "Theoretical quantiles", y = "Sample quantiles") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

p_hist + p_qq









A Q-Q plot that follows the diagonal closely indicates the normality assumption is met. Heavy tails or S-curves indicate non-normality.








WarningAssumption 2: Normality and Homoskedasticity of Level-1 Residuals



The distributional claim: \[\varepsilon_{ij} \overset{iid}{\sim} \mathcal{N}(0,\ \sigma^2_\varepsilon)\]
where \(\sigma^2_\varepsilon\) is the same for every unit \(j\) and every observation \(i\) — that is, within-unit variation is constant.
In longitudinal data: Mood variability might be higher for some people (high neuroticism → more volatile mood). This produces heteroskedastic residuals where \(\sigma^2_\varepsilon\) differs by person.
In experiments: Some stimuli might produce more variable responses than others (ambiguous images → higher disagreement). This would show up as heteroskedasticity across stimulus conditions.
How to check: A residuals vs. fitted values plot. A funnel shape means variance increases with the fitted value (heteroskedasticity). A curve means the relationship is nonlinear.


Code
df_sleep <- df_sleep |>
  mutate(fitted = fitted(model_ri_sleep), resid = residuals(model_ri_sleep))

p_rv <- ggplot(df_sleep, aes(x = fitted, y = resid)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.3, color = "#1e3a5f") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_smooth(method = "loess", color = "#e63946", se = FALSE) +
  labs(title = "Residuals vs. Fitted",
       subtitle = "Funnel = heteroskedasticity; curve = nonlinearity",
       x = "Fitted values", y = "Residuals") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

p_qq2 <- ggplot(df_sleep, aes(sample = resid)) +
  stat_qq(color = "#1e3a5f", alpha = 0.5) +
  stat_qq_line(color = "#e63946") +
  labs(title = "Q-Q: Level-1 residuals",
       x = "Theoretical", y = "Sample") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

p_rv + p_qq2

















WarningAssumption 3: Independence of Random Effects from Predictors



The critical assumption: The random intercepts \(u_{0j}\) must be uncorrelated with the Level-1 predictor \(X_{ij}\):
\[\text{Cov}(u_{0j},\ X_{ij}) = 0\]
Why this is the key difference from fixed effects: Fixed effects do not require this. They absorb unit-level variation without assuming anything about its relationship to \(X\). Random effects assume the two are unrelated.
In longitudinal data: If people who score high on baseline mood (\(u_{0j} > 0\)) tend to sleep more on average — perhaps because they have low anxiety — then \(u_{0j}\) and \(X_{ij}\) (sleep) are positively correlated. The random-effects estimator attributes some of this between-person relationship to the within-person slope, biasing \(\hat\beta_1\) upward.
In experiments: When stimuli are randomly assigned, this assumption is met by design for the treatment variable. However, if participants with higher baseline emotionality select into seeing more child images (selection into condition), the assumption fails. Randomization is what makes experiments safe for random effects.
How to check: The Hausman test compares the FE and RE estimates. If they agree, the independence assumption is plausible. If the RE estimate is noticeably larger than the FE estimate, the random intercepts are probably correlated with the predictor.


Code
library(plm)

df_panel <- df_sleep |> mutate(person_id = as.integer(person))
df_pdata <- pdata.frame(df_panel, index = c("person_id", "day"))

fe_plm <- plm(mood ~ sleep, data = df_pdata, model = "within")
re_plm <- plm(mood ~ sleep, data = df_pdata, model = "random")

phtest(fe_plm, re_plm)



    Hausman Test

data:  mood ~ sleep
chisq = 0.017224, df = 1, p-value = 0.8956
alternative hypothesis: one model is inconsistent


A non-significant Hausman test (p > 0.05) suggests the RE assumption is plausible. A significant result — especially when the FE estimate is substantially smaller than the RE estimate — means random intercepts are probably correlated with sleep, and you should use fixed effects or cluster-mean centering (Module 2).








WarningAssumption 4: Independence of Level-1 Residuals Within Units



The assumption: \[\text{Cov}(\varepsilon_{ij},\ \varepsilon_{i'j}) = 0 \quad \text{for } i \neq i'\]
After conditioning on the random intercept and the fixed effects, the residuals at different observations within the same unit are assumed to be uncorrelated.
In longitudinal data: This assumption is almost never literally true. Mood on day \(t\) predicts mood on day \(t+1\). The random intercept absorbs the stable between-day baseline difference, but it cannot capture temporal autocorrelation in the within-day fluctuations. If yesterday’s mood was unexpectedly high, today’s is also likely to be above the person’s mean — a pattern the random intercept cannot model.
In experiments: This assumption is usually more defensible because stimuli are presented in random order and participants are asked to evaluate each one independently. However, contrast effects (rating a stimulus relative to the previous one) can induce trial-to-trial correlation.
How to check: For longitudinal data, compute the lag-1 autocorrelation of residuals within each unit and examine the distribution:


Code
acf_df <- df_sleep |>
  group_by(person) |>
  arrange(day) |>
  summarise(lag1 = cor(head(resid, -1), tail(resid, -1)),
            .groups = "drop")

ggplot(acf_df, aes(x = lag1)) +
  geom_histogram(fill = "#1e3a5f", color = "white", bins = 15) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "#e63946") +
  labs(
    title    = "Lag-1 autocorrelation of residuals, by person",
    subtitle = "Distribution centered near 0 = independence assumption roughly met",
    x        = "Lag-1 autocorrelation",
    y        = "Number of people"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)









If the distribution is substantially right-shifted (most values positive), residuals are serially correlated and you need to model the autocorrelation explicitly — see Module 4 (Autoregressive Structures).
In experiments, you can do the same check ordered by trial number rather than time.

title: 9  Random Intercepts
section: 10.5 Partial Pooling: What Random Intercepts Do Uniquely
text:
10.5 Partial Pooling: What Random Intercepts Do Uniquely
Neither pooled OLS nor fixed effects can do what random intercepts do: borrow strength across units to produce better estimates for units with little data.

Pooled OLS: forces all units to have the same intercept (complete pooling)
Fixed effects: estimates each unit’s intercept independently (no pooling)
Random intercepts: shrinks each unit’s estimate toward the grand mean, weighted by how much information that unit contributes (partial pooling)

The combined equation makes this explicit. The random-intercept estimate for unit \(j\) is a weighted average:
\[\hat\beta_{0j} = \underbrace{\lambda_j \bar{Y}_j}_{\substack{\text{unit's own}\\ \text{mean} \times \text{reliability}}} + \underbrace{(1 - \lambda_j) \gamma_{00}}_{\substack{\text{grand mean} \times\\ \text{(1 - reliability)}}}\]
where \(\lambda_j = \frac{\sigma^2_u}{\sigma^2_u + \sigma^2_\varepsilon / n_j}\) is the reliability of unit \(j\)’s estimate. Units with many observations (\(n_j\) large) or high ICC (\(\sigma^2_u\) large relative to \(\sigma^2_\varepsilon\)) get reliability close to 1 — their own data dominates. Units with few observations get reliability close to 0 — the grand mean dominates.


Code
unit_means <- df_sleep |>
  group_by(person) |>
  summarise(unpooled = mean(mood), n_obs = n(), .groups = "drop")

re_ests <- tibble(
  person           = rownames(ranef(model_ri_sleep)$person),
  random_intercept = ranef(model_ri_sleep)$person[, 1]
) |>
  mutate(
    grand_mean = fixef(model_ri_sleep)["(Intercept)"],
    pooled_re  = grand_mean + random_intercept
  )

comparison <- left_join(
  unit_means |> mutate(person = as.character(person)),
  re_ests, by = "person"
)

ggplot(comparison, aes(x = unpooled, y = pooled_re)) +
  geom_point(size = 3, color = "#1e3a5f", alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray50") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = mean(comparison$pooled_re),
             color = "#e63946", linetype = "dotted", linewidth = 1) +
  annotate("text", x = min(comparison$unpooled) + 1,
           y = mean(comparison$pooled_re) + 1.5,
           label = "Grand mean", color = "#e63946", size = 3.5) +
  labs(
    title    = "Partial pooling: unpooled means vs. random-intercept estimates",
    subtitle = "Estimates are pulled toward the grand mean (red line) — that is shrinkage",
    x        = "Unpooled unit mean (no pooling)",
    y        = "Random-intercept estimate (partial pooling)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)









Points fall below the diagonal for high units and above it for low units — every estimate is pulled toward the grand mean. This is not a flaw; it is the model being appropriately uncertain about extreme values based on limited within-unit data.

title: 9  Random Intercepts
text:
10.6 Recommended Reading







Topic
Citation




Random effects for psychologists
Baayen, R. H., Davidson, D. J., & Bates, D. M. (2008). Mixed-effects modeling with crossed random effects for subjects and items. Journal of Memory and Language, 59(4), 390–412.


Partial pooling and shrinkage
Gelman, A., & Hill, J. (2007). Data Analysis Using Regression and Multilevel/Hierarchical Models. Cambridge University Press. (Chapter 12)


RE vs FE in experimental designs
Judd, C. M., Westfall, J., & Kenny, D. A. (2012). Treating stimuli as a random factor in social psychology. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 103(1), 54–69.


Hausman test
Hausman, J. A. (1978). Specification tests in econometrics. Econometrica, 46(6), 1251–1271.

title: 10  Random Slopes
text: 11 Random Slopes

title: 10  Random Slopes
section: 11.1 When the Effect Varies Across Units
text:
11.1 When the Effect Varies Across Units
The random-intercept model assumes that the slope — the relationship between the predictor and the outcome — is identical across all units. Everyone’s mood responds to sleep in exactly the same way; only their baseline mood differs.
But is that realistic?
Some people are highly sensitive to sleep: one bad night and they feel terrible. Others are remarkably resilient: they function almost the same regardless of how much they sleep. The magnitude of the sleep-mood relationship varies across people. If we force a single slope for everyone, we are misrepresenting the data-generating process.
The random-slope model allows the slope to vary across units, treating individual slopes as draws from a distribution.

title: 10  Random Slopes
text:
11.2 The Model
Level 1: \[Y_{ij} = \beta_{0j} + \beta_{1j} X_{ij} + \varepsilon_{ij}\]
Level 2: \[\beta_{0j} = \gamma_{00} + u_{0j}\] \[\beta_{1j} = \gamma_{10} + u_{1j}\]
Combined: \[Y_{ij} = \gamma_{00} + \gamma_{10} X_{ij} + u_{0j} + u_{1j} X_{ij} + \varepsilon_{ij}\]
The new component is \(u_{1j}\): each unit’s deviation from the average slope. We estimate \(\gamma_{10}\) (the average slope), \(\sigma^2_{u_0}\) (variance in intercepts), \(\sigma^2_{u_1}\) (variance in slopes), and crucially — \(\sigma_{u_0 u_1}\) (the correlation between intercepts and slopes).


Code
n_people <- 40
n_days   <- 7

person_intercepts <- rnorm(n_people, mean = 50, sd = 8)
person_slopes     <- rnorm(n_people, mean = 3, sd = 2.5)  # slopes vary!

df <- expand_grid(
  person = 1:n_people,
  day    = 1:n_days
) |>
  mutate(
    sleep  = rnorm(n(), mean = 7, sd = 1.5),
    mood   = person_intercepts[person] +
             person_slopes[person] * (sleep - 7) +
             rnorm(n(), 0, 5),
    person = factor(person)
  )

# Random intercept only
model_ri <- lmer(mood ~ sleep + (1 | person), data = df)

# Random intercept + slope
model_rs <- lmer(mood ~ sleep + (sleep | person), data = df)

# Compare
broom.mixed::tidy(model_rs, effects = "ran_pars") |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3, caption = "Variance components: random intercepts and slopes")



Variance components: random intercepts and slopes


effect
group
term
estimate




ran_pars
person
sd__(Intercept)
17.089


ran_pars

sd__sleep
2.263


ran_pars
person
cor__(Intercept).sleep

ran_pars
Residual
sd__Observation
4.947







Code
# Show individual slopes
person_lines <- df |>
  group_by(person) |>
  summarise(
    intercept = coef(lm(mood ~ sleep, data = cur_data()))[[1]],
    slope     = coef(lm(mood ~ sleep, data = cur_data()))[[2]],
    .groups   = "drop"
  )

# Plot individual regression lines
sleep_range <- seq(4, 10, by = 0.1)

lines_df <- person_lines |>
  mutate(
    x = list(sleep_range),
    y = map2(intercept, slope, ~ .x + .y * (sleep_range - 7))
  ) |>
  unnest(c(x, y)) |>
  rename(sleep = x, mood = y)

ggplot() +
  geom_line(data = lines_df,
            aes(x = sleep, y = mood, group = person),
            color = "#1e3a5f", alpha = 0.3, linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_abline(
    slope     = fixef(model_rs)["sleep"],
    intercept = fixef(model_rs)["(Intercept)"] - fixef(model_rs)["sleep"] * 7,
    color     = "#e63946", linewidth = 2
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Random slopes: each person's sleep-mood relationship",
    subtitle = "Red line = average slope (fixed effect); grey lines = individual slopes",
    x        = "Hours of sleep",
    y        = "Mood"
  ) +
  xlim(4.5, 9.5) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)









The grey lines show enormous variation in how strongly sleep predicts mood across people. The red line — the fixed effect — is the average story. But for some people, the relationship is strong; for others, nearly flat. A model that forces a single slope misses this heterogeneity entirely.

title: 10  Random Slopes
text:
11.3 The Intercept-Slope Correlation
The random-slope model also estimates the correlation between intercepts and slopes: \(\rho_{u_0, u_1}\).
This is substantively meaningful. A positive correlation would mean people with higher baseline mood also benefit more from sleep. A negative correlation would mean people with low baseline mood are more sensitive to sleep deprivation.


Code
# Plot the intercept-slope relationship
ggplot(person_lines, aes(x = intercept, y = slope)) +
  geom_point(size = 3, color = "#1e3a5f", alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = "#e63946", se = TRUE) +
  labs(
    title    = "Intercept-slope correlation",
    subtitle = "Is the sleep effect stronger for people with higher or lower baseline mood?",
    x        = "Individual intercept (baseline mood)",
    y        = "Individual slope (sleep effect)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

title: 10  Random Slopes
text:
11.4 The Assumptions of Random-Slope Models






WarningAssumption 1: Multivariate Normality of Random Effects



What it means: The random intercepts and slopes are jointly drawn from a bivariate normal distribution: \[\begin{pmatrix} u_{0j} \\ u_{1j} \end{pmatrix} \sim \mathcal{N}\left(\begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}, \begin{pmatrix} \sigma^2_{u_0} & \sigma_{u_0 u_1} \\ \sigma_{u_0 u_1} & \sigma^2_{u_1} \end{pmatrix}\right)\]
How to check: Plot the estimated random effects (from ranef()) as a scatterplot. Look for elliptical distributions, not banana-shaped or clustered patterns.


Code
re_df <- as.data.frame(ranef(model_rs)$person)
names(re_df) <- c("intercept", "slope")

ggplot(re_df, aes(x = intercept, y = slope)) +
  geom_point(size = 3, color = "#1e3a5f", alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray60") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray60") +
  labs(
    title    = "Bivariate distribution of random effects",
    subtitle = "Should look elliptical for bivariate normality",
    x        = "Random intercept",
    y        = "Random slope"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

















WarningAssumption 2: The Correct Random Effects Structure is Specified



What it means: The model must include random slopes for every predictor whose effect varies meaningfully across units. Failing to include a necessary random slope biases standard errors downward — producing anti-conservatively small p-values.
Judd, Westfall, & Kenny (2012, 2017) showed that omitting random slopes that should be in the model is one of the most common sources of Type I error inflation in experimental psychology, particularly when stimuli are treated as fixed rather than random.
How to check: Likelihood ratio tests (LRT) comparing models with and without the random slope. If the LRT is significant, the random slope is needed.


Code
# Test: does adding the random slope significantly improve fit?
model_ri_refit <- lmer(mood ~ sleep + (1 | person), data = df, REML = FALSE)
model_rs_refit <- lmer(mood ~ sleep + (sleep | person), data = df, REML = FALSE)

anova(model_ri_refit, model_rs_refit)

Note: use REML = FALSE when comparing models with likelihood ratio tests. Switch back to REML = TRUE for final parameter estimation.








WarningAssumption 3: Convergence



What it means: Complex random-slope models — especially with many predictors and crossed random effects — frequently fail to converge. A convergence warning from lme4 means the optimization algorithm did not find a stable minimum. Estimates from non-converged models should not be trusted.
How to check: Look for convergence warnings in lmer() output. Use check_convergence() from the performance package.
What to do if it fails: - Rescale continuous predictors (center and divide by SD) - Simplify the random effects structure (remove the correlation between intercept and slope: use (1 | person) + (0 + sleep | person)) - Try alternative optimizers: control = lmerControl(optimizer = "bobyqa") - Switch to Bayesian estimation (brms), which is more robust for complex models


Code
library(performance)
check_convergence(model_rs)


[1] FALSE

title: 10  Random Slopes
section: 11.5 Model Comparison: Do You Need Random Slopes?
text:
11.5 Model Comparison: Do You Need Random Slopes?
Adding random slopes adds parameters and complexity. You should include them when:

Theory predicts heterogeneous effects: Do you have a reason to believe the relationship varies across units?
The LRT supports them: The model with random slopes fits significantly better than the model without.
The design requires them: In within-subject experimental designs, random slopes for experimental conditions are almost always needed (see Module 5).

title: 10  Random Slopes
text:
11.6 Recommended Reading







Topic
Citation




Random slopes in experimental designs
Barr, D. J., Levy, R., Scheepers, C., & Tily, H. J. (2013). Random effects structure for confirmatory hypothesis testing: Keep it maximal. Journal of Memory and Language, 68(3), 255–278.


When random slopes matter
Heisig, J. P., & Schaeffer, M. (2019). Why you should always include a random slope for the lower-level variable involved in a cross-level interaction. European Sociological Review, 35(2), 258–279.

title: 11  Fixed vs. Random: When to Use Which
text: 12 Fixed vs. Random Effects: When to Use Which
crumbs: 11  Fixed vs. Random: When to Use Which

title: 11  Fixed vs. Random: When to Use Which
text:
12.1 The Most Frequently Asked Question
If you have been following along, you have noticed that fixed effects and random effects make different assumptions, answer slightly different questions, and have different costs and benefits. The obvious question is: which one should I use?
The honest answer is: it depends on your data and your scientific question. But it is not arbitrary. There are principled criteria for making this decision.
crumbs: 11  Fixed vs. Random: When to Use Which

title: 11  Fixed vs. Random: When to Use Which
text:
12.2 A Side-by-Side Comparison








Property
Fixed Effects
Random Intercepts




Assumption about group effects
Arbitrary constants to be estimated
Drawn from a normal distribution


Correlation with predictors
Allowed (and corrected for)
Must be approximately zero


Between-unit variation
Removed entirely
Modeled and retained


Unit-level predictors
Cannot estimate
Can estimate


Predictions for new units
Not possible
Possible (using variance components)


Efficiency
Less efficient (uses more df)
More efficient if RE assumption holds


Robustness
Robust to correlated random effects
Biased if RE-predictor correlation exists
crumbs: 11  Fixed vs. Random: When to Use Which

title: 11  Fixed vs. Random: When to Use Which
text:
12.3 The Decisive Criterion
The most important question is whether the random effects (unit-level deviations) are correlated with your predictors.

If yes: Random effects models are biased. Use fixed effects.
If no: Random effects models are more efficient. Use them.

The Hausman test formalizes this check (covered in Part 2). But you should also think about it substantively:
Example 1 — Use FE: You are studying whether neighborhood green space predicts depression. People choose where to live based on many factors — income, family ties, preferences — that also affect mental health. The random effects (neighborhood-level deviations in mental health) are surely correlated with neighborhood characteristics. Use fixed effects.
Example 2 — Use RE: You run a multi-site experiment where participants at 20 different universities complete a survey. The sites were chosen roughly at random, and the random effects (site-level deviations in mean response) are unlikely to correlate with your experimental manipulation. Use random effects.
crumbs: 11  Fixed vs. Random: When to Use Which

title: 11  Fixed vs. Random: When to Use Which
text:
12.4 A Third Option: Hybrid Models
In many cases, you actually want both: the within-unit consistency of fixed effects and the ability to model between-unit effects. This is achievable using cluster-mean centering, covered in Module 2.
The hybrid approach: 1. Center the predictor around each unit’s mean (within-unit component) 2. Include the unit mean as a separate predictor (between-unit component) 3. Use a random-intercept model
This separates within and between effects explicitly, avoids the confound problem, and retains the ability to study unit-level variation. It is often the best default for panel and longitudinal data.
crumbs: 11  Fixed vs. Random: When to Use Which

title: 11  Fixed vs. Random: When to Use Which
section: 12.5 Scientific Questions Also Drive the Choice
text:
12.5 Scientific Questions Also Drive the Choice
Beyond the statistical criteria, your scientific question matters:







Question
Preferred Model




What is the causal effect of X on Y within units?
Fixed effects


How does X relate to Y on average, accounting for clustering?
Random effects


How much do units vary in their Y levels?
Random effects (ICC)


How much do units vary in the X-Y relationship?
Random slopes


Can I predict a new unit’s Y from its X?
Random effects


Does unit-level characteristic Z predict Y?
Random effects
crumbs: 11  Fixed vs. Random: When to Use Which

title: 11  Fixed vs. Random: When to Use Which
text:
12.6 What About Bayesian Approaches?
Bayesian mixed-effects models (brms package) extend the random-effects framework with explicit priors on variance components. They are particularly useful when:

The model is complex and frequentist estimation has convergence problems
You want proper uncertainty quantification for variance components (frequentist intervals for variances can be poorly behaved near zero)
You want to incorporate prior knowledge about reasonable effect sizes

The conceptual structure is identical to the frequentist random-effects model. The assumptions are the same. The key difference is in how uncertainty is expressed and how parameters are estimated.
crumbs: 11  Fixed vs. Random: When to Use Which

title: 11  Fixed vs. Random: When to Use Which
text:
12.7 Summary Decision Tree
Is the random effect correlated with your predictors?
├── Yes → Fixed Effects
└── No or Unknown →
    ├── Do you want to estimate unit-level predictor effects?
    │   ├── Yes → Random Effects
    └── ├── Do you care about between-unit variance (ICC)?
        │   ├── Yes → Random Effects
        └── └── Do you want predictions for new units?
                ├── Yes → Random Effects
                └── No → Either (use Hausman test to choose)
crumbs: 11  Fixed vs. Random: When to Use Which

title: 12  Three-Level Structures
text: 13 Three-Level Structures

title: 12  Three-Level Structures
text:
13.1 When Two Levels Aren’t Enough
Real organizational and social data is almost always more than two levels deep. Patients are nested within therapists, who are nested within clinics. Students are nested within classrooms, which are nested within schools, which are nested within districts. Employees are nested within teams, which are nested within departments, which are nested within firms.
A three-level model extends the random-effects framework by adding a third random effect — one for each of the three levels of the hierarchy.

title: 12  Three-Level Structures
text:
13.2 The Model
Level 1 (observations within units within groups): \[Y_{ijk} = \beta_{0jk} + \beta_1 X_{ijk} + e_{ijk}\]
Level 2 (units within groups): \[\beta_{0jk} = \gamma_{00k} + u_{0jk}\]
Level 3 (groups): \[\gamma_{00k} = \delta_{000} + v_{0k}\]
Combined: \[Y_{ijk} = \delta_{000} + \beta_1 X_{ijk} + v_{0k} + u_{0jk} + e_{ijk}\]
Three variance components: \(\sigma^2_v\) (level-3 variance), \(\sigma^2_u\) (level-2 variance), \(\sigma^2_e\) (level-1 variance).


Code
# Simulate: patients within therapists within clinics
n_clinics    <- 8
n_therapists <- 4  # per clinic
n_patients   <- 10 # per therapist

clinic_effects    <- rnorm(n_clinics, 0, 4)     # clinic-level variance
therapist_effects <- rnorm(n_clinics * n_therapists, 0, 3)  # therapist-level

df_3l <- expand_grid(
  clinic    = 1:n_clinics,
  therapist = 1:n_therapists,
  patient   = 1:n_patients
) |>
  mutate(
    therapist_id    = (clinic - 1) * n_therapists + therapist,
    sessions        = round(runif(n(), 5, 20)),
    outcome         = 50 + 0.5 * sessions +
                      clinic_effects[clinic] +
                      therapist_effects[therapist_id] +
                      rnorm(n(), 0, 5),
    clinic          = factor(clinic, labels = paste0("C", 1:n_clinics)),
    therapist_f     = factor(therapist_id)
  )

# Three-level model: patients in therapists in clinics
model_3l <- lmer(outcome ~ sessions + (1 | clinic/therapist_f), data = df_3l)
summary(model_3l)

# Variance components and level-specific ICCs
vc <- as.data.frame(VarCorr(model_3l))
vc |>
  select(grp, vcov) |>
  mutate(
    total_var = sum(vcov),
    icc       = vcov / total_var
  ) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3, caption = "Variance decomposition across levels")

Reading the variance components:

clinic: How much of the outcome variance is due to differences between clinics?
therapist_f:clinic (therapists within clinics): How much is due to differences between therapists within the same clinic?
Residual: How much is due to differences between patients within the same therapist?

This decomposition tells us where the action is — and where intervention should be targeted.

title: 12  Three-Level Structures
text:
13.3 ICC for Three-Level Data
In a three-level model, there are two ICCs:
Level-3 ICC: Proportion of variance attributable to the highest-level unit (e.g., clinic): \[\text{ICC}_3 = \frac{\sigma^2_v}{\sigma^2_v + \sigma^2_u + \sigma^2_e}\]
Level-2 ICC: Proportion of variance attributable to level-2 units and above (e.g., therapist or above): \[\text{ICC}_2 = \frac{\sigma^2_v + \sigma^2_u}{\sigma^2_v + \sigma^2_u + \sigma^2_e}\]


Code
library(performance)
icc(model_3l)






ICC_adjusted
ICC_unadjusted
optional

title: 12  Three-Level Structures
text:
13.4 The Assumptions of Three-Level Models
All assumptions from the two-level model carry over. Additionally:






WarningAssumption: Correct Level Specification



What it means: The model must correctly identify which units belong to which level. If therapists are incorrectly attributed to clinics (data entry errors, therapists who work at multiple clinics), the variance components will be misattributed.
How to check: Cross-tabulate your level-2 and level-3 identifiers. Each level-2 unit should belong to exactly one level-3 unit (in a pure hierarchy).


Code
df_3l |>
  distinct(clinic, therapist_id) |>
  group_by(therapist_id) |>
  filter(n() > 1)  # should return 0 rows for a pure hierarchy






clinic
therapist_id
















WarningAssumption: Sample Size at Each Level



What it means: Variance components at higher levels require sufficient numbers of level-3 units to be estimated reliably. As a rough guide, you want at least 20–30 units at each level where you are estimating a random effect.
Why it matters: With only 5 clinics, the clinic-level variance estimate is very noisy. Confidence intervals for level-3 variance components are wide with small level-3 samples.
How to check: Count units at each level. Be cautious interpreting level-3 variance with fewer than 20 level-3 units.

title: 12  Three-Level Structures
text:
13.5 Adding Level-2 and Level-3 Predictors
One of the major advantages of the three-level model is the ability to include predictors at each level and examine cross-level interactions.


Code
# Add clinic-level predictor (e.g., clinic size)
df_3l <- df_3l |>
  group_by(clinic) |>
  mutate(clinic_size = n_distinct(therapist_f) * n_patients + rnorm(1, 0, 2)) |>
  ungroup() |>
  group_by(clinic, therapist_f) |>
  mutate(therapist_experience = round(runif(1, 1, 20))) |>
  ungroup()

model_3l_pred <- lmer(
  outcome ~ sessions + therapist_experience + clinic_size +
    (1 | clinic/therapist_f),
  data = df_3l
)

broom.mixed::tidy(model_3l_pred, effects = "fixed") |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3, caption = "Three-level model with predictors at all levels")

title: 13  Crossed Random Effects
text: 14 Crossed Random Effects

title: 13  Crossed Random Effects
text:
14.1 The Problem with Pure Hierarchies
Pure hierarchies assume each lower-level unit belongs to exactly one higher-level unit. But in many real data structures — especially experimental designs in psychology — units belong to multiple grouping factors simultaneously.
The classic example: A reaction time experiment. 40 participants each respond to 80 words. Each observation belongs to one participant AND one word. Participants are not nested within words. Words are not nested within participants. Both grouping factors are fully crossed.
If you only include a random effect for participants (as most psychologists do), you are assuming that all variance due to words is captured by the residual. But words differ systematically — some words are rare and hard to process; others are frequent and easy. This word-level variance inflates your residuals and biases your standard errors.
Judd, Westfall, and Kenny (2012, 2017) showed that failing to include random effects for stimuli is one of the most widespread sources of Type I error inflation in social and cognitive psychology.

title: 13  Crossed Random Effects
text:
14.2 The Model
For crossed random effects with participants (\(p\)) and stimuli (\(s\)):
\[Y_{ps} = \gamma_{00} + \beta_1 X_{ps} + u_{0p} + w_{0s} + \varepsilon_{ps}\]
Where: - \(u_{0p} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2_u)\): participant random intercepts - \(w_{0s} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2_w)\): stimulus random intercepts - \(\varepsilon_{ps}\): residual


Code
# Simulate a lexical decision task
n_participants <- 40
n_words        <- 80

participant_effects <- rnorm(n_participants, 0, 50)   # people differ in baseline RT
word_effects        <- rnorm(n_words, 0, 40)           # words differ in baseline RT
word_frequency      <- runif(n_words, 1, 10)           # word frequency predictor

df_ld <- expand_grid(
  participant = 1:n_participants,
  word        = 1:n_words
) |>
  mutate(
    participant_eff = participant_effects[participant],
    word_eff        = word_effects[word],
    frequency       = word_frequency[word],
    RT              = 550 - 15 * frequency +
                      participant_eff + word_eff +
                      rnorm(n(), 0, 30),
    participant     = factor(participant),
    word            = factor(word)
  )

# Crossed random effects model
model_crossed <- lmer(RT ~ frequency + (1 | participant) + (1 | word),
                      data = df_ld)

summary(model_crossed)

title: 13  Crossed Random Effects
section: 14.3 Comparing: With and Without Word Random Effects
text:
14.3 Comparing: With and Without Word Random Effects


Code
# Without word random effects (wrong)
model_no_words <- lmer(RT ~ frequency + (1 | participant), data = df_ld)

# With word random effects (correct)
model_with_words <- lmer(RT ~ frequency + (1 | participant) + (1 | word), data = df_ld)

# Compare standard errors for the frequency effect
wrong_se  <- broom.mixed::tidy(model_no_words)[2, "std.error"][[1]]
correct_se <- broom.mixed::tidy(model_with_words)[2, "std.error"][[1]]

tibble(
  Model = c("Without word RE", "With word RE"),
  Frequency_SE = c(wrong_se, correct_se)
) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "Standard error of frequency effect: ignoring vs. modeling word-level variance")



Standard error of frequency effect: ignoring vs. modeling word-level variance


Model
Frequency_SE




Without word RE
0.342


With word RE
1.740





When word-level variance is ignored, the standard error for frequency is smaller — the model is treating word-level variance as residual noise, which artificially deflates uncertainty. This produces anti-conservative inference: p-values are too small.

title: 13  Crossed Random Effects
section: 14.4 The Assumptions of Crossed Random-Effects Models
text:
14.4 The Assumptions of Crossed Random-Effects Models






WarningAssumption 1: Independence of Participant and Stimulus Random Effects



What it means: The random effect for participant \(p\) and the random effect for word \(w\) are assumed to be independent of each other. Person 7 having an unusually fast RT is not related to whether word 42 is unusually easy.
How to check: In a balanced fully-crossed design, this is usually reasonable. In unbalanced designs (not all participants see all stimuli), check whether missingness is systematic.








WarningAssumption 2: Random Effects are Independent of the Predictor



What it means: As with all random-effects models, the random intercepts for participants and words should be uncorrelated with the predictors. Word frequency should not predict which words were seen by which participants (in a balanced design, it cannot).
Why this is usually satisfied in experiments: In a fully-crossed, counterbalanced design, every participant sees every word — so assignment is orthogonal to word characteristics. This is one reason well-designed experiments support random-effects modeling.








WarningAssumption 3: The Same Model Applies to All Participant-Stimulus Pairs



What it means: The frequency effect is assumed to be the same for all participants (unless a random slope for frequency by participants is included). If some participants are more sensitive to frequency than others, a random slope for frequency by participants is needed.
How to check: As with all random-slope decisions, use likelihood ratio tests and theoretical reasoning.


Code
# Can add random slopes for frequency by participants
model_maximal <- lmer(RT ~ frequency + (frequency | participant) + (1 | word),
                      data   = df_ld,
                      control = lmerControl(optimizer = "bobyqa"))

anova(model_with_words, model_maximal)

title: 13  Crossed Random Effects
text:
14.5 The “Keep It Maximal” Debate
Barr et al. (2013) argued that you should always include the maximal random effects structure — every random slope that is theoretically justified by the design. This minimizes Type I error.
Bates et al. (2015) and Matuschek et al. (2017) pushed back, arguing that maximal models frequently fail to converge, and that parsimonious models (with only the random effects supported by the data) often provide better estimates.
The current consensus: include random slopes for all within-unit manipulations (this is critical), but simplify the correlation structure or remove slopes not supported by the data if the model fails to converge.
This debate is covered in depth in Module 5.

title: 13  Crossed Random Effects
text:
14.6 Recommended Reading







Topic
Citation




Subjects and items as crossed random effects
Judd, C. M., Westfall, J., & Kenny, D. A. (2012). Treating stimuli as a random factor in social psychology. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 103(1), 54–69.


Keep it maximal
Barr, D. J., Levy, R., Scheepers, C., & Tily, H. J. (2013). Random effects structure for confirmatory hypothesis testing: Keep it maximal. Journal of Memory and Language, 68(3), 255–278.


Parsimonious random effects
Matuschek, H., Kliegl, R., Vasishth, S., Baayen, H., & Bates, D. (2017). Balancing Type I error and power in linear mixed models. Journal of Memory and Language, 94, 305–315.

title: 14  Overview
text: Module 2: Centering & Between/Within Effects

title: 14  Overview
text:
14.1 The Subtlest Assumption
You have built a mixed-effects model, checked your residuals, verified convergence, and are ready to interpret your coefficients. There is one more question you need to answer before you do:
What does your predictor’s value actually mean?
This sounds almost philosophical, but it has concrete, testable consequences. A predictor like “daily sleep hours” can mean two different things in a within-person longitudinal dataset:

Between-person effect: People who tend to sleep more also tend to have higher mood (perhaps because of a third factor — stable health, lifestyle, genetics — or because sleep causally improves mood on average across people)
Within-person effect: On days when this specific person sleeps more than their own average, their mood is higher

These two effects are not the same. They can point in opposite directions. They answer different scientific questions. And the standard random-intercept model, without careful centering, conflates them.
Module 2 is about learning to see the difference — and to model it explicitly.

title: 14  Overview
text:
14.2 What This Module Covers
Part 1: Centering Your Variables introduces grand-mean centering (GMC) and cluster-mean centering (CMC), explains what each does to the interpretation of your coefficients, and provides a decision framework.
Part 2: Between-Unit vs. Within-Unit Effects shows how to simultaneously estimate both effects in a single model — the “Mundlak device” — and when the between and within effects diverge.
Part 3: Simpson’s Paradox uses vivid examples to show how the relationship at the aggregate level can be completely opposite to the relationship at the individual level. This is not a statistical curiosity — it is a systematic feature of nested data that researchers frequently misinterpret.

title: 14  Overview
text:
14.3 The Core Insight
Standard regression assumes your predictor has one meaning. In nested data, it has (at least) two: a within-unit meaning and a between-unit meaning. Centering is the tool that separates them.

“What do your means mean?” — this question, asked before every analysis, will save you from some of the most common and consequential errors in social science research.

title: 15  Centering as Decomposition
text: 16 Centering as Decomposition

title: 15  Centering as Decomposition
text:
16.1 One Variable, Two Mechanisms
When you measure “hours of sleep” across multiple days from multiple people, that variable carries two completely different stories at the same time:

The within-person story: On days when this person sleeps more than they usually do, how does their mood change?
The between-person story: Do people who sleep more overall have higher mood than people who sleep less?

These are different empirical questions. They may have different answers. They probably operate through different mechanisms — the within-person effect may reflect acute physiological recovery, while the between-person effect may reflect stable personality traits (conscientious people both sleep more and have better mood, not because sleep causes mood but because conscientiousness causes both).
Centering is not a cosmetic choice about where the intercept falls. It is a decision about which mechanism you are modeling — and whether you want to see both.

title: 15  Centering as Decomposition
section: 16.2 The Equations: From Conflation to Decomposition
text:
16.2 The Equations: From Conflation to Decomposition

16.2.1 The Conflated Model
When you include an uncentered (or grand-mean-centered) predictor in a random-effects model, a single coefficient \(\beta_1\) blends two mechanisms:
\[
\underbrace{Y_{ij}}_{\text{outcome}} = \underbrace{\gamma_{00}}_{\substack{\text{grand} \\ \text{mean}}} + \underbrace{\beta_1}_{\substack{\text{blended} \\ \text{slope}}} \underbrace{X_{ij}}_{\substack{\text{predictor}}} + \underbrace{u_{0j}}_{\substack{\text{person} \\ \text{RE}}} + \underbrace{\varepsilon_{ij}}_{\substack{\text{residual}}}
\]
\(\beta_1\) absorbs variation from two sources simultaneously. Its value depends on which source dominates and how the two correlate.


16.2.2 The Decomposition
CMC separates the predictor into its two components before entering the model:
\[
\underbrace{X_{ij}}_{\substack{\text{observed} \\ \text{predictor}}} = \underbrace{(X_{ij} - \bar{X}_{j})}_{\substack{\text{within-unit} \\ \text{deviation}}} + \underbrace{\bar{X}_{j}}_{\substack{\text{unit mean} \\ \text{(between)}}}
\]
Adding both as separate predictors gives the decomposed model. Notice how the single \(\beta_1\) from before splits into two:
\[
\underbrace{Y_{ij}}_{\text{outcome}} = \underbrace{\gamma_{00}}_{\substack{\text{grand} \\ \text{mean}}} + \underbrace{\beta_W}_{\substack{\text{within-unit} \\ \text{slope}}} \underbrace{(X_{ij} - \bar{X}_j)}_{\substack{\text{deviation from} \\ \text{own mean}}} + \underbrace{\beta_B}_{\substack{\text{between-unit} \\ \text{slope}}} \underbrace{\bar{X}_j}_{\substack{\text{unit's} \\ \text{average level}}} + \underbrace{u_{0j}}_{\substack{\text{person} \\ \text{RE}}} + \underbrace{\varepsilon_{ij}}_{\substack{\text{residual}}}
\]
Now \(\beta_W\) answers the within-person question and \(\beta_B\) answers the between-person question. They are allowed to be completely different numbers with different interpretations.






NoteThe DGP Interpretation



The decomposed model is a hypothesis about the DGP: it asserts that two distinct mechanisms are at work, one operating at the within-unit level and one at the between-unit level. If \(\beta_W \neq \beta_B\), the DGP truly has two mechanisms. If \(\beta_W \approx \beta_B\), a single mechanism may span both levels — but you only know this after estimating the decomposed model and testing whether they differ.

title: 15  Centering as Decomposition
text:
16.3 Three Archetypal Patterns
The most important practical question is: how different are \(\beta_W\) and \(\beta_B\)?

16.3.1 Pattern 1: Convergent Effects — Exercise and Wellbeing
Both mechanisms point the same direction with similar magnitude. The within-person mechanism (acute endorphins, reduced rumination) and the between-person mechanism (fit people have better overall routines and self-efficacy) both increase wellbeing. True: \(\beta_W \approx \beta_B \approx 3\).


Code
n_ppl <- 60; n_days <- 10

person_ex_avg <- rnorm(n_ppl, 45, 12)
person_wb_base <- 50 + 0.3 * person_ex_avg + rnorm(n_ppl, 0, 6)

df_ex <- expand_grid(person = 1:n_ppl, day = 1:n_days) |>
  mutate(
    exercise  = person_ex_avg[person] + rnorm(n(), 0, 8),
    wellbeing = person_wb_base[person] +
                3 * (exercise - person_ex_avg[person]) +
                rnorm(n(), 0, 5),
    person = factor(person)
  ) |>
  group_by(person) |>
  mutate(ex_mean = mean(exercise), ex_cmc = exercise - ex_mean) |>
  ungroup()

m_ex <- lmer(wellbeing ~ ex_cmc + ex_mean + (1 | person), data = df_ex)
broom.mixed::tidy(m_ex, effects = "fixed") |> filter(term != "(Intercept)") |>
  mutate(mechanism = c("Within-person", "Between-person")) |>
  select(mechanism, estimate, std.error, p.value) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3, caption = "Pattern 1: within ≈ between ≈ 3. One coherent mechanism at two levels.")



Pattern 1: within ≈ between ≈ 3. One coherent mechanism at two levels.


mechanism
estimate
std.error
p.value




Within-person
2.996
0.030
0


Between-person
0.438
0.089
0





Here, the pooled model would give you a decent approximation — but you only know that after running the decomposition. The convergence itself is substantively informative: the same process operates at both timescales.


16.3.2 Pattern 2: Divergent Effects — Income and Life Satisfaction (Easterlin Paradox)
At the between-person level, wealthier people report substantially higher life satisfaction. But within individuals over time, income growth has a surprisingly modest effect — adaptation, shifting aspirations, and social comparison dampen the within-person response. True: \(\beta_W \approx 0.3\), \(\beta_B \approx 1.5\).


Code
n_ppl2 <- 80; n_yrs <- 8

person_inc_avg  <- rnorm(n_ppl2, 50, 15)
person_sat_base <- 50 + 1.5 * person_inc_avg + rnorm(n_ppl2, 0, 8)

df_inc <- expand_grid(person = 1:n_ppl2, year = 1:n_yrs) |>
  mutate(
    income   = person_inc_avg[person] + rnorm(n(), 0, 6),
    life_sat = person_sat_base[person] +
               0.3 * (income - person_inc_avg[person]) + rnorm(n(), 0, 4),
    person   = factor(person)
  ) |>
  group_by(person) |>
  mutate(inc_mean = mean(income), inc_cmc = income - inc_mean) |>
  ungroup()

m_inc    <- lmer(life_sat ~ inc_cmc + inc_mean + (1 | person), data = df_inc)
m_pooled <- lmer(life_sat ~ income  + (1 | person), data = df_inc)

bind_rows(
  broom.mixed::tidy(m_pooled, effects = "fixed") |>
    filter(term == "income") |> mutate(model = "Pooled (blended)"),
  broom.mixed::tidy(m_inc, effects = "fixed") |>
    filter(term == "inc_cmc") |> mutate(model = "Within-person"),
  broom.mixed::tidy(m_inc, effects = "fixed") |>
    filter(term == "inc_mean") |> mutate(model = "Between-person")
) |>
  select(model, estimate, std.error, p.value) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "Pattern 2: pooled ≈ 1.2, within ≈ 0.3, between ≈ 1.5. Pooled is wrong for both questions.")



Pattern 2: pooled ≈ 1.2, within ≈ 0.3, between ≈ 1.5. Pooled is wrong for both questions.


model
estimate
std.error
p.value




Pooled (blended)
0.380
0.026
0


Within-person
0.336
0.027
0


Between-person
1.469
0.054
0





The pooled model gives ~1.2 — it overstates the within-person effect by 4× and understates the between-person effect. A policy conclusion based on this (“raising incomes will substantially improve wellbeing”) relies on the between-person relationship but must deliver on the within-person mechanism.


16.3.3 Pattern 3: Sign Reversal — Study Hours and Grades
Within a student, studying more than their own average helps their grades. But between programs, the harder programs have students who study more and earn lower grades — because the programs are harder, not because studying hurts. True: \(\beta_W \approx +2\), \(\beta_B \approx -3\).


Code
n_students <- 200; n_programs <- 5
prog_difficulty <- c(2, 4, 6, 8, 10)
prog_study_avg  <- c(8, 7, 6, 5, 4)

df_study <- tibble(
  student = 1:n_students,
  program = rep(1:n_programs, each = n_students / n_programs)
) |>
  mutate(
    study = prog_study_avg[program] + rnorm(n_students, 0, 0.8),
    grade = 80 - 3 * prog_difficulty[program] +
            2 * (study - prog_study_avg[program]) + rnorm(n_students, 0, 3),
    program = factor(program)
  ) |>
  group_by(program) |>
  mutate(study_mean = mean(study), study_cmc = study - study_mean) |>
  ungroup()

m_study_decomp <- lmer(grade ~ study_cmc + study_mean + (1 | program), data = df_study)
m_study_pooled <- lm(grade ~ study, data = df_study)

bind_rows(
  broom::tidy(m_study_pooled) |>
    filter(term == "study") |> mutate(model = "Pooled OLS"),
  broom.mixed::tidy(m_study_decomp, effects = "fixed") |>
    filter(term == "study_cmc") |> mutate(model = "Within-program"),
  broom.mixed::tidy(m_study_decomp, effects = "fixed") |>
    filter(term == "study_mean") |> mutate(model = "Between-program")
) |>
  select(model, estimate, std.error, p.value) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "Pattern 3: pooled is negative; within is positive; between is strongly negative. Sign reversal!")



Pattern 3: pooled is negative; within is positive; between is strongly negative. Sign reversal!


model
estimate
std.error
p.value




Pooled OLS
5.013
0.165
0


Within-program
1.783
0.274
0


Between-program
5.790
0.145
0







Code
p1 <- ggplot(df_study, aes(x = study, y = grade)) +
  geom_point(aes(color = program), alpha = 0.4, size = 1.2) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = "black", linewidth = 1.5) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
  labs(title = "Pooled: studying MORE → LOWER grades?",
       subtitle = "Slope is negative — a sign reversal",
       x = "Study hours/week", y = "Grade", color = "Program") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

p2 <- ggplot(df_study, aes(x = study, y = grade, color = program)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.3, size = 1) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, linewidth = 1) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
  labs(title = "Within-program: studying MORE → HIGHER grades",
       subtitle = "Within every program, the slope is positive",
       x = "Study hours/week", y = "Grade", color = "Program") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

p1 + p2









The pooled model says studying hurts — which is wrong. The decomposition reveals the truth: within every program, studying helps, but harder programs demand more studying and are harder by definition. Ignoring the nesting produces a Simpson’s Paradox (see Part 3).

title: 15  Centering as Decomposition
text:
16.4 The Sleep-Mood Example: Applying Decomposition


Code
n_people <- 50; n_days2 <- 7

person_sleep_avg  <- rnorm(n_people, 7, 1)
person_mood_base  <- 40 + 5 * person_sleep_avg + rnorm(n_people, 0, 5)

df <- expand_grid(person = 1:n_people, day = 1:n_days2) |>
  mutate(
    sleep_raw = person_sleep_avg[person] + rnorm(n(), 0, 1),
    mood      = person_mood_base[person] +
                2 * (sleep_raw - person_sleep_avg[person]) + rnorm(n(), 0, 4),
    person    = factor(person)
  ) |>
  group_by(person) |>
  mutate(sleep_mean = mean(sleep_raw), sleep_cmc = sleep_raw - sleep_mean) |>
  ungroup() |>
  mutate(sleep_gmc = sleep_raw - mean(sleep_raw))

m_uncentered <- lmer(mood ~ sleep_raw  + (1 | person), data = df)
m_gmc        <- lmer(mood ~ sleep_gmc  + (1 | person), data = df)
m_cmc        <- lmer(mood ~ sleep_cmc + sleep_mean + (1 | person), data = df)

bind_rows(
  broom.mixed::tidy(m_uncentered, effects = "fixed") |>
    filter(term == "sleep_raw") |>
    mutate(model = "Uncentered", mechanism = "Blended"),
  broom.mixed::tidy(m_gmc, effects = "fixed") |>
    filter(term == "sleep_gmc") |>
    mutate(model = "GMC", mechanism = "Blended"),
  broom.mixed::tidy(m_cmc, effects = "fixed") |>
    filter(term == "sleep_cmc") |>
    mutate(model = "CMC", mechanism = "Within-person"),
  broom.mixed::tidy(m_cmc, effects = "fixed") |>
    filter(term == "sleep_mean") |>
    mutate(model = "CMC", mechanism = "Between-person")
) |>
  select(model, mechanism, estimate, std.error, p.value) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "Sleep → mood: blended ≈ 4.0; within ≈ 2.0; between ≈ 5.0. Neither blended estimate is correct.")



Sleep → mood: blended ≈ 4.0; within ≈ 2.0; between ≈ 5.0. Neither blended estimate is correct.
