The uncentered and GMC models give ~4, which is wrong for both questions. CMC reveals: the within-person mechanism is modest (2 points per extra hour of sleep); the between-person correlation is much larger (5 points) because healthy lifestyle confounds it.

title: 15  Centering as Decomposition
text:
16.5 Decision Guide






TipWhich Centering to Use











Goal
Centering
What you get




Interpret the intercept at a meaningful value
GMC
Intercept = E[Y] when X = grand mean


Estimate the within-unit effect
CMC
\(\hat\beta_W\) — deviation from own mean


Separate within and between mechanisms
CMC + unit mean
\(\hat\beta_W\) and \(\hat\beta_B\)


Test if within = between
CMC + unit mean
Wald test on equality


Single-level data (no nesting)
Either
No within/between distinction



Default recommendation for nested data: Always use CMC with the unit mean included. Run it, check whether \(\hat\beta_W \approx \hat\beta_B\), and only then decide whether the pooled model is defensible.








WarningAssumption: No Conflation of Within and Between Effects



Including a predictor without CMC in a random-effects model implicitly assumes \(\beta_W = \beta_B\). When this is wrong — as in Patterns 2 and 3 above — both estimates are biased.
How to test: Fit the CMC model. Compare \(\hat\beta_W\) and \(\hat\beta_B\). A Wald test (see Part 2) tests the null \(H_0: \beta_W = \beta_B\).
The stakes: In the worst case (Pattern 3), conflation completely reverses the direction of the effect.

title: 16  Separating Within and Between Effects
text: 17 Separating Within and Between Effects
crumbs: 16  Separating Within and Between Effects

title: 16  Separating Within and Between Effects
text:
17.1 The Mundlak Device
Part 1 showed that a predictor in nested data carries two mechanisms. The formal statistical tool for separating them is known as the Mundlak device (Mundlak, 1978) or equivalently the within-between (WB) model.
The key equation — building on Part 1’s decomposition — is:
\[
\underbrace{Y_{ij}}_{\text{outcome}} = \underbrace{\gamma_{00}}_{\substack{\text{grand} \\ \text{mean}}} + \underbrace{\beta_W (X_{ij} - \bar{X}_j)}_{\substack{\text{within-unit effect} \\ \text{(deviation from own mean)}}} + \underbrace{\beta_B \bar{X}_j}_{\substack{\text{between-unit effect} \\ \text{(unit's average level)}}} + \underbrace{u_{0j}}_{\substack{\text{unit random} \\ \text{intercept}}} + \underbrace{\varepsilon_{ij}}_{\substack{\text{residual}}}
\]
The Mundlak insight: this model is equivalent to including the unit mean \(\bar{X}_j\) alongside the raw predictor \(X_{ij}\) in a random-effects model. The coefficient on \(X_{ij}\) then estimates the within-unit effect, and the coefficient on \(\bar{X}_j\) estimates the additional between-unit effect beyond the within-unit effect.
Both are valid. The CMC parameterization (Part 1) gives \(\beta_W\) and \(\beta_B\) directly and makes interpretation cleaner. The Mundlak parameterization adds \(\bar{X}_j\) alongside the raw \(X_{ij}\) and gives a test of whether \(\beta_W = \beta_B\).
crumbs: 16  Separating Within and Between Effects

title: 16  Separating Within and Between Effects
section: 17.2 Example 1: The Easterlin Paradox — GDP and Wellbeing
text:
17.2 Example 1: The Easterlin Paradox — GDP and Wellbeing
The classic motivating case. Between countries, richer nations are happier. Within countries over time, economic growth has a modest and inconsistent effect on wellbeing. Two different mechanisms, same predictor.


Code
n_countries <- 40; n_years <- 10

country_gdp_avg       <- rnorm(n_countries, 50, 15)
country_wellbeing_base <- 50 + 0.6 * country_gdp_avg + rnorm(n_countries, 0, 8)

df_gdp <- expand_grid(country = 1:n_countries, year = 1:n_years) |>
  mutate(
    gdp_raw   = country_gdp_avg[country] + rnorm(n(), 0, 5),
    wellbeing = country_wellbeing_base[country] +
                0.15 * (gdp_raw - country_gdp_avg[country]) + rnorm(n(), 0, 4),
    country   = factor(country)
  ) |>
  group_by(country) |>
  mutate(gdp_mean = mean(gdp_raw), gdp_cmc = gdp_raw - gdp_mean) |>
  ungroup()

m_pooled  <- lmer(wellbeing ~ gdp_raw + (1 | country), data = df_gdp)
m_mundlak <- lmer(wellbeing ~ gdp_cmc + gdp_mean + (1 | country), data = df_gdp)

bind_rows(
  broom.mixed::tidy(m_pooled,  effects = "fixed") |>
    filter(term == "gdp_raw") |> mutate(model = "Pooled"),
  broom.mixed::tidy(m_mundlak, effects = "fixed") |>
    filter(term == "gdp_cmc")  |> mutate(model = "Within-country"),
  broom.mixed::tidy(m_mundlak, effects = "fixed") |>
    filter(term == "gdp_mean") |> mutate(model = "Between-country")
) |>
  select(model, estimate, std.error, p.value) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "GDP → wellbeing: pooled ~0.45 blends within ~0.15 and between ~0.60")



GDP → wellbeing: pooled ~0.45 blends within ~0.15 and between ~0.60


model
estimate
std.error
p.value




Pooled
0.252
0.039
0


Within-country
0.187
0.041
0


Between-country
0.683
0.082
0





Interpretation: - Within-country (gdp_cmc ≈ 0.15): In years when GDP is above a country’s own average, wellbeing ticks up slightly. Modest — consistent with hedonic adaptation and shifting aspirations. - Between-country (gdp_mean ≈ 0.60): Countries with permanently higher GDP have substantially higher wellbeing. But this reflects institutions, infrastructure, healthcare, and social safety nets that co-occur with wealth — not just income itself. - Pooled (~0.45): An average that describes neither mechanism accurately.
crumbs: 16  Separating Within and Between Effects

title: 16  Separating Within and Between Effects
section: 17.3 Example 2: Within-Subjects Experiments — Trait vs. State Effects
text:
17.3 Example 2: Within-Subjects Experiments — Trait vs. State Effects
In a within-subjects experiment, the same decomposition applies — but now the “cluster” is the participant and the nesting is observations within person.
Design: 80 participants each evaluate 10 charity appeals. Half feature statistical evidence; half feature personal stories. Outcome: whether they donate (continuous willingness-to-donate scale, 0–100).
The within-person effect (\(\beta_W\)): For a given participant, do story-based appeals get higher donations than statistical appeals — compared to that participant’s own average donation?
The between-person effect (\(\beta_B\)): Do participants who are exposed to more story-based appeals (by random assignment variation) tend to donate more overall? This would pick up trait-level empathizers who respond to everything more generously.
With random assignment, \(\beta_B\) should be near zero — randomization ensures that more story-exposed participants are not systematically more empathetic. A large \(\beta_B\) is a red flag.


Code
n_p_exp <- 80; n_appeals <- 10

# Some participants are trait high-empathy: they donate more AND
# in this (imperfect) design, happen to see more story appeals
person_empathy     <- rnorm(n_p_exp, 50, 10)
person_story_prop  <- 0.5 + 0.01 * (person_empathy - 50) + rnorm(n_p_exp, 0, 0.05)
person_story_prop  <- pmin(pmax(person_story_prop, 0), 1)
person_base_donate <- 40 + 0.8 * person_empathy + rnorm(n_p_exp, 0, 8)

df_exp <- expand_grid(participant = 1:n_p_exp, appeal = 1:n_appeals) |>
  mutate(
    story = rbinom(n(), 1, person_story_prop[participant]),
    donate = person_base_donate[participant] +
             5 * (story - person_story_prop[participant]) +
             rnorm(n(), 0, 6),
    participant = factor(participant)
  ) |>
  group_by(participant) |>
  mutate(story_mean = mean(story), story_cmc = story - story_mean) |>
  ungroup()

m_exp <- lmer(donate ~ story_cmc + story_mean + (1 | participant), data = df_exp)
broom.mixed::tidy(m_exp, effects = "fixed") |> filter(term != "(Intercept)") |>
  mutate(mechanism = c("Within-person (causal effect)", "Between-person (trait confound)")) |>
  select(mechanism, estimate, std.error, p.value) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "Story vs. stats: within ≈ 5 (treatment effect); between ≈ near zero (good randomization)")



Story vs. stats: within ≈ 5 (treatment effect); between ≈ near zero (good randomization)


mechanism
estimate
std.error
p.value




Within-person (causal effect)
4.569
0.462
0


Between-person (trait confound)
27.913
5.476
0





With good randomization, \(\beta_B \approx 0\) and \(\beta_W\) recovers the true treatment effect. A significant \(\beta_B\) would suggest a randomization failure or a design confound — worth always checking in within-subjects designs.
crumbs: 16  Separating Within and Between Effects

title: 16  Separating Within and Between Effects
section: 17.4 Example 3: Ecological Fallacy — Education and Fertility
text:
17.4 Example 3: Ecological Fallacy — Education and Fertility
Between countries: more educated countries have lower fertility (strong negative relationship). Within countries over time: rising education predicts declining fertility (also negative). Here the signs align, but the magnitudes and mechanisms differ.
The between-country effect reflects structural factors: cultural norms, female labor force participation, urbanization, and healthcare access all co-vary with education across nations.
The within-country effect over time reflects a causal mechanism: as individual women gain education, they delay marriage, have fewer children, and invest more per child.
Same direction, but if you use the cross-national correlation to inform policy about what happens when you increase education within a country, you are using the wrong estimate — the between-country relationship is confounded by dozens of co-varying structural factors.


Code
n_countries_ef <- 50; n_years_ef <- 8

country_edu_avg <- rnorm(n_countries_ef, 60, 12)
country_fert_base <- 4.5 - 0.05 * country_edu_avg + rnorm(n_countries_ef, 0, 0.4)

df_ef <- expand_grid(country = 1:n_countries_ef, year = 1:n_years_ef) |>
  mutate(
    education = country_edu_avg[country] + rnorm(n(), 0, 5),
    fertility = country_fert_base[country] -
                0.01 * (education - country_edu_avg[country]) + rnorm(n(), 0, 0.2),
    country   = factor(country)
  ) |>
  group_by(country) |>
  mutate(edu_mean = mean(education), edu_cmc = education - edu_mean) |>
  ungroup()

m_ef <- lmer(fertility ~ edu_cmc + edu_mean + (1 | country), data = df_ef)
broom.mixed::tidy(m_ef, effects = "fixed") |> filter(term != "(Intercept)") |>
  mutate(mechanism = c("Within-country over time", "Between-country cross-section")) |>
  select(mechanism, estimate, std.error, p.value) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "Education → fertility: both negative, but between is 5× larger due to structural confounds")



Education → fertility: both negative, but between is 5× larger due to structural confounds


mechanism
estimate
std.error
p.value




Within-country over time
-0.010
0.002
0


Between-country cross-section
-0.051
0.004
0





The between-country estimate is 5× larger than the within-country estimate. Using the cross-sectional number to predict what happens when you raise education within a country would dramatically overstate the policy effect.
crumbs: 16  Separating Within and Between Effects

title: 16  Separating Within and Between Effects
section: 17.5 Example 4: Convergent Effects — Training and Performance
text:
17.5 Example 4: Convergent Effects — Training and Performance
Not every decomposition reveals a divergence. When within and between effects are approximately equal, this is itself a substantive finding: the same mechanism operates at both levels.
Employee training hours → job performance. Within an employee: weeks where they receive more training, their performance ticks up (skill acquisition). Between employees: those who invest more in training overall also perform better (both due to training AND because high performers seek out training). Both effects are positive and of similar magnitude.


Code
n_emp <- 100; n_weeks <- 8

emp_train_avg   <- rnorm(n_emp, 4, 1.5)
emp_perf_base   <- 50 + 3 * emp_train_avg + rnorm(n_emp, 0, 6)

df_train <- expand_grid(employee = 1:n_emp, week = 1:n_weeks) |>
  mutate(
    training    = pmax(0, emp_train_avg[employee] + rnorm(n(), 0, 1)),
    performance = emp_perf_base[employee] +
                  2.8 * (training - emp_train_avg[employee]) + rnorm(n(), 0, 5),
    employee    = factor(employee)
  ) |>
  group_by(employee) |>
  mutate(train_mean = mean(training), train_cmc = training - train_mean) |>
  ungroup()

m_train <- lmer(performance ~ train_cmc + train_mean + (1 | employee), data = df_train)
broom.mixed::tidy(m_train, effects = "fixed") |> filter(term != "(Intercept)") |>
  mutate(mechanism = c("Within-employee", "Between-employee")) |>
  select(mechanism, estimate, std.error, p.value) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "Training → performance: within ≈ between ≈ 3. Pooled model is approximately correct here.")



Training → performance: within ≈ between ≈ 3. Pooled model is approximately correct here.


mechanism
estimate
std.error
p.value




Within-employee
2.790
0.194
0


Between-employee
2.035
0.387
0





When \(\beta_W \approx \beta_B\), the pooled model is defensible. But again: you only know this after running the decomposition. The convergence is informative — it suggests training itself (not some trait that drives both training and performance) is the active mechanism.
crumbs: 16  Separating Within and Between Effects

title: 16  Separating Within and Between Effects
section: 17.6 Testing Whether Within and Between Effects Are Equal
text:
17.6 Testing Whether Within and Between Effects Are Equal
The formal test is a Wald test on the null hypothesis \(H_0: \beta_W = \beta_B\):


Code
# Test on the Easterlin model: are within and between GDP effects equal?
linearHypothesis(m_mundlak, "gdp_cmc = gdp_mean")






Df
Chisq
Pr(>Chisq)




NA
NA
NA


1
29.48468
1e-07






A significant result (p < 0.05) means you must report both effects separately — the pooled model is misspecified. A non-significant result means you cannot rule out that the effects are equal, and a pooled model may be defensible (though the CMC model is always more informative).
crumbs: 16  Separating Within and Between Effects

title: 16  Separating Within and Between Effects
text:
17.7 A Decision Table






TipWhat to Report: Within, Between, or Both?












Pattern
\(\beta_W\) vs. \(\beta_B\)
Wald test
What to report




Convergent
Similar magnitude and sign
p > .05
Pooled estimate is OK, but note the decomposition confirmed equivalence


Divergent
Different magnitudes
p < .05
Report both; discuss different mechanisms


Sign reversal
Opposite signs
p < .05
Report both; warn about Simpson’s Paradox


Experimental
\(\beta_B \approx 0\), \(\beta_W\) = treatment effect
p < .05
Report within as the treatment effect; \(\beta_B\) is a randomization check



Always run the decomposed model first. Use the Wald test to decide whether to simplify. Do not start with the pooled model.








WarningAssumption: Correct Level Attribution



Including a variable as within-unit (CMC) or between-unit (unit mean) requires knowing which level it genuinely varies at. A variable that appears to vary within units but is actually stable should be treated as a level-2 predictor.
How to check: Compute the ICC of the predictor itself. High ICC (>0.70) means most variation is between units; CMC will give you noisy, low-variance within deviations. Low ICC means the variable genuinely varies at the within-unit level.
crumbs: 16  Separating Within and Between Effects

title: 17  Simpson’s Paradox
text: 18 Simpson’s Paradox

title: 17  Simpson’s Paradox
text:
18.1 When the Aggregate Lies
Simpson’s Paradox occurs when a relationship that exists in the aggregate data reverses when you look within subgroups. It is not a statistical quirk — it is a direct consequence of ignoring nesting, and it is surprisingly common.


Code
# Classic demonstration: grades and study time, but students vary by program
# Hard program students study more AND have lower grades (because the program is harder)
n_students  <- 200
n_programs  <- 5

program_difficulty <- c(2, 4, 6, 8, 10)   # harder programs
program_study_avg  <- c(8, 7, 6, 5, 4)    # harder programs -> students study MORE

students <- tibble(
  student  = 1:n_students,
  program  = rep(1:n_programs, each = n_students / n_programs),
  study    = program_study_avg[program] + rnorm(n_students, 0, 0.8),
  grade    = 80 - 3 * program_difficulty[program] + 2 * (study - program_study_avg[program]) +
             rnorm(n_students, 0, 3),
  program_label = factor(program, labels = paste("Program", 1:n_programs))
)

# Overall relationship: NEGATIVE (studying more -> lower grades??)
m_pooled <- lm(grade ~ study, data = students)

# Within-program: POSITIVE
m_within <- lmer(grade ~ study + (1 | program_label), data = students)

p1 <- ggplot(students, aes(x = study, y = grade)) +
  geom_point(aes(color = program_label), alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = "black", linewidth = 1.5) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
  labs(title = "Pooled: studying MORE predicts LOWER grades?!",
       x = "Study hours/week", y = "Grade", color = "Program") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

p2 <- ggplot(students, aes(x = study, y = grade, color = program_label)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, linewidth = 1) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
  labs(title = "Within program: studying MORE predicts HIGHER grades",
       x = "Study hours/week", y = "Grade", color = "Program") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

library(patchwork)
p1 / p2









The pooled relationship is negative: students who study more get lower grades. That looks like studying causes harm!
The truth: students in harder programs study more AND get lower grades — because the programs are harder. Within any given program, studying more helps. The aggregate correlation is driven entirely by the confound between program difficulty and study time.
This is Simpson’s Paradox, and it happens in your data.

title: 17  Simpson’s Paradox
text:
18.2 Real-World Examples
Berkeley admissions (1973): Across departments, women were admitted at lower rates than men. Within departments, women were admitted at the same or higher rates. The paradox arose because women applied disproportionately to competitive departments.
COVID-19 mortality: In some early analyses, countries with more advanced healthcare appeared to have higher mortality rates. Within matched patient groups (controlling for age and severity), better healthcare reduced mortality. The aggregate correlation was driven by age confounds.
Wages across demographic groups: Total wages can fall for every group while rising in the aggregate, if people are shifting from lower-paid to higher-paid occupations (your slides show this exact example).

title: 17  Simpson’s Paradox
section: 18.3 The Lesson for Nested Data Analysis
text:
18.3 The Lesson for Nested Data Analysis
Simpson’s Paradox is not rare. It is the default when: 1. Groups differ in their average level of the predictor 2. Groups differ in their average level of the outcome 3. The predictor and outcome differ in sign between levels
Every time you ignore nesting, you risk a Simpson’s Paradox situation. The mixed-effects models in this workshop are partly a defense against this — they force you to separate within-group and between-group relationships explicitly.






ImportantThe Check



Before reporting any correlation or regression coefficient from nested data, ask: Does this relationship hold in the same direction within units as it does across units? If not, you are reporting a Simpson’s Paradox, not a real effect.

title: 18  Overview
text: Module 3: Inferential Threats & Identification

title: 18  Overview
text:
18.1 The “Independent” Variable
A variable only earns the label “independent” after you have done the work to establish that it is exogenously related to your outcome. Until then, it is just a predictor — one that may be deeply entangled with other forces that also shape the outcome.
Nested data creates special entanglement problems. When people are grouped into firms, schools, neighborhoods, or time periods, the things that determine their group membership are often the same things that affect their outcomes. A person does not end up in a high-autonomy firm at random. A student does not end up in a high-quality classroom at random. A country does not adopt a policy at random.
This module is about recognizing these entanglements — endogeneity — and learning the identification strategies that let us recover credible causal claims despite them.

title: 18  Overview
text:
18.2 What This Module Covers
Part 1: Endogeneity — The three sources of endogeneity in social science data: omitted variable bias, simultaneity/reverse causality, and selection. What each looks like in the DGP, and how to recognize them.
Part 2: Instrumental Variables — When you cannot randomize, an instrument can do some of the work. What makes a valid instrument, the two-stage procedure, and real examples from consumer and organizational research.
Part 3: Selection Effects & Attrition — The many ways observations end up in your dataset non-randomly: representativeness failures, self-selection, survivorship, attrition in longitudinal studies, and researcher-driven exclusions.
Part 4: Regression Discontinuity Design — When treatment assignment is based on a threshold, the continuity of potential outcomes around that threshold gives you a local quasi-experiment. Assumptions, tests, and worked examples.
Part 5: Difference-in-Differences — When some units receive a treatment and others do not, and you have pre- and post-treatment observations, DiD recovers causal effects under the parallel trends assumption.
Part 6: Propensity Scores & Matching — When treatment is observational but you can condition on all relevant confounders, matching and weighting approaches can reduce bias.

title: 18  Overview
text:
18.3 The Through-Line: Identification
Every method in this module is an identification strategy — a way to isolate variation in your predictor that is (approximately) exogenous, and use only that variation to estimate the effect of interest.
The math of each strategy is not the hard part. The hard part is the judgment: Does your instrument really satisfy the exclusion restriction? Are your parallel trends really plausible? Can you really claim you have measured all confounders?
This module teaches both the tools and the judgment.

title: 19  Endogeneity: Why Regression Lies (and How to Catch It)
text: 20 Endogeneity: Why Regression Lies (and How to Catch It)
crumbs: 19  Endogeneity: Why Regression Lies (and How to Catch It)

title: 19  Endogeneity: Why Regression Lies (and How to Catch It)
section: 20.1 The Fundamental Problem of Causal Inference
text:
20.1 The Fundamental Problem of Causal Inference
Before introducing regression at all, consider the true object of scientific interest: the causal effect of X on Y for a specific unit \(i\).
Under the potential outcomes framework (Rubin, 1974), every unit has two potential outcomes:
\[
\underbrace{\tau_i}_{\substack{\text{causal effect} \\ \text{for unit } i}} = \underbrace{Y_i(1)}_{\substack{\text{outcome if} \\ \text{treated}}} - \underbrace{Y_i(0)}_{\substack{\text{outcome if} \\ \text{not treated}}}
\]
The fundamental problem: we can never observe both \(Y_i(1)\) and \(Y_i(0)\) for the same unit at the same time. A person either received psychotherapy or they did not. A country either adopted a carbon tax or it did not. We observe one potential outcome and must infer the other.
Regression estimates the average treatment effect (ATE):
\[
\text{ATE} = E[\tau_i] = E[Y_i(1) - Y_i(0)]
\]
For regression to estimate the ATE correctly, the observed difference in outcomes between treated and untreated units must equal the counterfactual difference between what would have happened to those same units under both conditions. This requires that who gets treated is independent of the potential outcomes.
When this independence fails — when who gets X is related to Y through pathways other than X itself — the regression coefficient is endogenous and does not estimate the causal effect.
crumbs: 19  Endogeneity: Why Regression Lies (and How to Catch It)

title: 19  Endogeneity: Why Regression Lies (and How to Catch It)
text:
20.2 What is Endogeneity?
Endogeneity means your predictor X is correlated with the error term \(\varepsilon\) in your regression:
\[
Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_i + \varepsilon_i \quad \text{where } \text{Cov}(X_i, \varepsilon_i) \neq 0
\]
When this happens, \(\hat{\beta}_1\) is biased. It estimates not just the effect of X on Y but a blend of that effect and all the other pathways through which X and Y are connected.
There are four main sources.
crumbs: 19  Endogeneity: Why Regression Lies (and How to Catch It)

title: 19  Endogeneity: Why Regression Lies (and How to Catch It)
text:
20.3 Source 1: Omitted Variable Bias (OVB)
A variable Z influences both X and Y, but Z is not in your model. The path Z → Y gets attributed to X because X and Z are correlated.

20.3.1 The Bias Formula
The expected value of the biased coefficient is:
\[
\underbrace{E[\hat{\beta}_1]}_{\substack{\text{what we} \\ \text{estimate}}} = \underbrace{\beta_1}_{\substack{\text{true} \\ \text{effect}}} + \underbrace{\delta_{ZY}}_{\substack{Z \text{'s effect} \\ \text{on } Y}} \cdot \underbrace{\tilde{\rho}_{XZ}}_{\substack{\text{correlation of} \\ X \text{ and } Z}}
\]
where \(\delta_{ZY}\) is the effect of Z on Y (holding X constant) and \(\tilde{\rho}_{XZ}\) is the correlation of Z with X after partialling out other covariates.
The bias formula has a critical practical use: even without data on Z, if you know the sign of both \(\delta_{ZY}\) and \(\tilde\rho_{XZ}\), you can predict the direction of the bias on your estimate.


Code
# Bias direction table
tibble(
  Example         = c("Ice cream → drowning", "Advertising → sales", "Mindfulness → stress"),
  Confounder_Z    = c("Summer heat", "Store quality", "Stable personality"),
  `Sign Z→X`      = c("+", "+", "+"),
  `Sign Z→Y`      = c("+", "+", "-"),
  `Bias direction` = c("Positive (↑ est.)", "Positive (↑ est.)", "Negative (↓ est.)")
) |>
  knitr::kable(caption = "OVB bias direction: multiply signs to get bias direction")



OVB bias direction: multiply signs to get bias direction









Example
Confounder_Z
Sign Z→X
Sign Z→Y
Bias direction




Ice cream → drowning
Summer heat
+
+
Positive (↑ est.)


Advertising → sales
Store quality
+
+
Positive (↑ est.)


Mindfulness → stress
Stable personality
+
-
Negative (↓ est.)











NoteReading the Bias Table



Sign(Z→X) × Sign(Z→Y) = sign of the bias: - Both positive → positive bias (estimate is too large) - Both negative → positive bias (two negatives multiply to positive) - Mixed signs → negative bias (estimate is too small or reversed)
You can use this qualitatively even before collecting data: “If my estimate is already large, and the likely omitted confound would produce positive bias, my true effect is probably smaller than I think.”




20.3.2 Three Demonstrations


Code
n <- 500

# Example 1: Ice cream / drowning / heat
Z_heat    <- rnorm(n, 70, 10)
X_icecream <- 0.3 * Z_heat + rnorm(n, 0, 5)
Y_drowning <- 0.5 * Z_heat + rnorm(n, 0, 8)  # true X effect = 0

m_biased_1  <- lm(Y_drowning ~ X_icecream)
m_correct_1 <- lm(Y_drowning ~ X_icecream + Z_heat)

# Example 2: Advertising / sales / store quality
Z_quality   <- rnorm(n, 50, 10)
X_ads       <- 0.4 * Z_quality + rnorm(n, 0, 8)
Y_sales     <- 0.6 * Z_quality + 0.15 * X_ads + rnorm(n, 0, 10)
# true X effect = 0.15; quality inflates this upward

m_biased_2  <- lm(Y_sales ~ X_ads)
m_correct_2 <- lm(Y_sales ~ X_ads + Z_quality)

# Example 3: Mindfulness / stress / stable personality
Z_stability   <- rnorm(n, 50, 10)
X_mindfulness <- 0.5 * Z_stability + rnorm(n, 0, 6)
Y_stress      <- 80 - 0.4 * Z_stability - 2 * X_mindfulness + rnorm(n, 0, 8)
# true X effect = -2; stability also lowers stress AND increases mindfulness → downward bias

m_biased_3  <- lm(Y_stress ~ X_mindfulness)
m_correct_3 <- lm(Y_stress ~ X_mindfulness + Z_stability)

bind_rows(
  tidy(m_biased_1)  |> filter(term == "X_icecream")   |> mutate(example = "Ice cream (true=0)",    model = "Omits Z"),
  tidy(m_correct_1) |> filter(term == "X_icecream")   |> mutate(example = "Ice cream (true=0)",    model = "Includes Z"),
  tidy(m_biased_2)  |> filter(term == "X_ads")        |> mutate(example = "Advertising (true=0.15)", model = "Omits Z"),
  tidy(m_correct_2) |> filter(term == "X_ads")        |> mutate(example = "Advertising (true=0.15)", model = "Includes Z"),
  tidy(m_biased_3)  |> filter(term == "X_mindfulness")|> mutate(example = "Mindfulness (true=-2)",   model = "Omits Z"),
  tidy(m_correct_3) |> filter(term == "X_mindfulness")|> mutate(example = "Mindfulness (true=-2)",   model = "Includes Z")
) |>
  select(example, model, estimate, std.error, p.value) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3, caption = "OVB across three examples: omitting Z biases estimates in predictable directions")



OVB across three examples: omitting Z biases estimates in predictable directions


example
model
estimate
std.error
p.value




Ice cream (true=0)
Omits Z
0.543
0.067
0.000


Ice cream (true=0)
Includes Z
-0.052
0.070
0.457


Advertising (true=0.15)
Omits Z
0.398
0.052
0.000


Advertising (true=0.15)
Includes Z
0.130
0.053
0.014

Omits Z

Mindfulness (true=-2)
Includes Z
-1.947

In every case, adding the confounder pulls the estimate toward the true value. The bias direction matches the prediction from the sign table.






WarningAssumption: No Omitted Variables Correlated with Both X and Y



How to check: Draw a causal diagram. List every plausible cause of Y. Ask: is any of these also correlated with X? This requires substantive reasoning about the DGP — you cannot test it purely from data.
In nested data: OVB is especially insidious because the omitted variable often varies at a higher level. Studying whether neighborhood green space predicts depression while omitting neighborhood income biases your estimate; the omitted variable is a neighborhood-level characteristic that clustering creates.
What to do: Add the variable if measurable. Use fixed effects to absorb all stable unit-level confounders. Use IV, RDD, or DiD if the confound is unmeasurable (covered in Parts 2–6).
crumbs: 19  Endogeneity: Why Regression Lies (and How to Catch It)

title: 19  Endogeneity: Why Regression Lies (and How to Catch It)
text:
20.4 Source 2: Simultaneity / Reverse Causality
X causes Y, but Y also causes X. The causal arrow runs in both directions, creating a feedback loop that OLS cannot untangle.


Code
n_sim <- 300
set.seed(42)

# Social support → depression (negative), but depression → social withdrawal (negative)
# Simulate equilibrium: both processes are real
time_pts <- tibble(
  t    = 1:10,
  support = c(60, 55, 52, 50, 49, 48, 47, 47, 47, 47) + rnorm(10, 0, 2),
  depression = c(30, 35, 38, 40, 41, 42, 43, 43, 43, 43) + rnorm(10, 0, 2)
)

p_sim <- time_pts |>
  pivot_longer(-t) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = t, y = value, color = name, group = name)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) + geom_point(size = 2.5) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#e63946", "#1e3a5f"),
                     labels = c("Depression", "Social Support")) +
  labs(title = "Simultaneity: support → depression AND depression → support",
       subtitle = "OLS on cross-sectional data picks up both arrows simultaneously",
       x = "Time", y = "Level", color = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

p_sim









Psychology example: Social support reduces depression — but depression also causes people to withdraw from social networks. A cross-sectional regression of depression on social support picks up both directions simultaneously and gives a confounded estimate.
Marketing example: Brand awareness drives sales, but sales revenue funds advertising that builds brand awareness. A cross-sectional regression cannot tell you whether awareness causes sales or sales cause awareness.






WarningAssumption: No Reverse Causality



How to check: Ask: could Y at time \(t\) plausibly influence X at time \(t\) or \(t+1\)? If yes, simultaneity is a concern.
What to do: - Use lagged predictors (\(X_{t-1}\) predicting \(Y_t\)) in panel data - Use instrumental variables (Part 2) to isolate the causal direction - Use cross-lagged panel models (Module 4, Part 6) which model both causal arrows explicitly
crumbs: 19  Endogeneity: Why Regression Lies (and How to Catch It)

title: 19  Endogeneity: Why Regression Lies (and How to Catch It)
text:
20.5 Source 3: Selection Effects
The observations in your dataset are not a random sample of the population. Who ends up in your data — and which treatment they receive — is systematically related to both X and Y.
Self-selection: People who choose to undergo therapy are systematically different from those who don’t — they may be more motivated to change, have better social support, or have less severe disorders. Comparing therapy-goers to non-therapy-goers conflates the effect of therapy with all these pre-existing differences.
Survivorship bias: You only observe firms that survived long enough to be in your dataset. Failed firms are absent. Conclusions about firm strategy drawn from survivors are biased because you are conditioning on survival.
Attrition in longitudinal studies: Participants who drop out of a study are often systematically different from completers. If the sickest patients drop out of a clinical trial, the observed treatment effect among completers overstates the population-level effect.
This source of endogeneity is covered in depth in Part 3.
crumbs: 19  Endogeneity: Why Regression Lies (and How to Catch It)

title: 19  Endogeneity: Why Regression Lies (and How to Catch It)
text:
20.6 Source 4: Collider Bias
This source is frequently overlooked but is just as dangerous as the others. Conditioning on a collider — a variable caused by both X and Y — creates a spurious association between X and Y even when none exists.
A collider is a variable C where two causal arrows “collide”:
\[X \rightarrow C \leftarrow Y\]
When you condition on C (include it as a control, restrict your sample to a subset defined by C, or analyze a non-random sample selected on C), you open a spurious backdoor path between X and Y.


Code
n_col <- 1000

# True DGP: X and Y are uncorrelated
X_col <- rnorm(n_col)
Y_col <- rnorm(n_col)  # truly independent of X

# Collider C is caused by both X and Y
C_col <- X_col + Y_col + rnorm(n_col, 0, 0.5)

# Full sample: no correlation
r_full <- cor(X_col, Y_col)

# Conditioning on C: restrict to top half of C
high_C <- C_col > median(C_col)
r_cond <- cor(X_col[high_C], Y_col[high_C])

tibble(
  Sample           = c("Full sample (no conditioning)", "Restricted to high-C (conditioning)"),
  `Cor(X, Y)`      = c(round(r_full, 3), round(r_cond, 3)),
  Interpretation   = c("True: X and Y are unrelated",
                        "Spurious: conditioning on C creates negative correlation")
) |>
  knitr::kable(caption = "Collider bias: conditioning on C creates a spurious X-Y association")



Collider bias: conditioning on C creates a spurious X-Y association







Sample
Cor(X, Y)
Interpretation




Full sample (no conditioning)
0.005
True: X and Y are unrelated


Restricted to high-C (conditioning)
-0.372
Spurious: conditioning on C creates negative correlation





The publish-or-perish example: Prolificacy (X) and idea quality (Y) may be uncorrelated in the full population of researchers. But publication (C) is caused by both — prolific researchers with good ideas get published. If you study only published papers (conditioning on C), you’ll observe that prolificacy and idea quality are negatively correlated within the published set, even though they’re unrelated in the population.
Berkson’s paradox in hospitals: Disease A (X) and disease B (Y) are unrelated in the general population. But both can lead to hospitalization (C). If you study only hospitalized patients, A and B appear negatively correlated — because having A makes it less likely you also have B to explain your hospitalization.


Code
df_col <- tibble(X = X_col, Y = Y_col, C_high = high_C)

p_full <- ggplot(df_col, aes(x = X, y = Y)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.15, color = "#1e3a5f") +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = "#e63946", linewidth = 1.2) +
  labs(title = "Full sample", subtitle = paste0("r = ", round(r_full, 3)),
       x = "X", y = "Y") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

p_cond <- ggplot(df_col |> filter(C_high), aes(x = X, y = Y)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.2, color = "#2d6a4f") +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = "#e63946", linewidth = 1.2) +
  labs(title = "Conditioned on high C", subtitle = paste0("r = ", round(r_cond, 3)),
       x = "X", y = "Y") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

p_full + p_cond















WarningAssumption: No Conditioning on Colliders



How to check: Draw a DAG. For every variable you are controlling for or selecting on, ask: is this caused by X, by Y, or by both? If caused by both, it is a collider — do not condition on it.
Common mistake: “Kitchen sink” regressions that add every available covariate. If any of those covariates is a collider, adding it creates bias where none existed.
crumbs: 19  Endogeneity: Why Regression Lies (and How to Catch It)

title: 19  Endogeneity: Why Regression Lies (and How to Catch It)
section: 20.7 Pearl’s Causal Framework: Three Types of Paths
text:
20.7 Pearl’s Causal Framework: Three Types of Paths
Judea Pearl’s causal graph (DAG) framework provides a systematic way to identify endogeneity. Every causal graph has three fundamental path structures:


Code
make_dag_panel <- function(nodes, edges_df, title, note = NULL) {
  p <- ggplot() +
    geom_point(data = nodes, aes(x = x, y = y), size = 18,
               shape = 21, fill = "#deebf7", color = "#1e3a5f", stroke = 1.5) +
    geom_text(data = nodes, aes(x = x, y = y, label = label), size = 3.8,
              fontface = "bold", color = "#1e3a5f")
  for (i in seq_len(nrow(edges_df))) {
    p <- p + annotate("segment",
                      x    = edges_df$x1[i], xend = edges_df$x2[i],
                      y    = edges_df$y1[i], yend = edges_df$y2[i],
                      arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.25, "cm")),
                      color = edges_df$color[i], linewidth = 1.2)
  }
  p + labs(title = title, subtitle = note) +
    xlim(0.3, 3.7) + ylim(0.4, 2.6) + theme_void(base_size = 11) +
    theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11),
          plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 9, color = "gray40"))
}

nodes_fork <- tibble(label = c("Z", "X", "Y"), x = c(2, 1, 3), y = c(2, 1, 1))
edges_fork <- tibble(x1=c(1.8,2.2), x2=c(1.2,2.8), y1=c(1.9,1.9), y2=c(1.1,1.1),
                     color = c("#e63946","#e63946"))
p_fork <- make_dag_panel(nodes_fork, edges_fork, "Fork (Confounder)",
  "Z → X and Z → Y\nCreates spurious X-Y correlation\nFix: control for Z")

nodes_chain <- tibble(label = c("X", "M", "Y"), x = c(1, 2, 3), y = c(1, 1, 1))
edges_chain <- tibble(x1=c(1.2,2.2), x2=c(1.8,2.8), y1=c(1,1), y2=c(1,1),
                      color = c("#1e3a5f","#1e3a5f"))
p_chain <- make_dag_panel(nodes_chain, edges_chain, "Chain (Mediator)",
  "X → M → Y\nM is on the causal path\nFix: do NOT control for M")

nodes_coll <- tibble(label = c("X", "C", "Y"), x = c(1, 2, 3), y = c(1, 1, 1))
edges_coll <- tibble(x1=c(1.2,2.8), x2=c(1.8,2.2), y1=c(1,1), y2=c(1,1),
                     color = c("#1e3a5f","#1e3a5f"))
p_coll <- make_dag_panel(nodes_coll, edges_coll, "Collider",
  "X → C ← Y\nCondition on C = open backdoor\nFix: do NOT control for C")

p_fork + p_chain + p_coll


















Path Type
Structure
What it means
What to do




Fork
Z → X and Z → Y
Z confounds X-Y relationship
Block by controlling for Z


Chain
X → M → Y
M mediates X’s effect on Y
Do NOT block (it carries the effect)


Collider
X → C ← Y
C is caused by both X and Y
Do NOT block (conditioning opens a backdoor)



The goal of every identification strategy in the rest of this module is to block all backdoor paths (forks) without blocking causal paths (chains) and without conditioning on colliders.
crumbs: 19  Endogeneity: Why Regression Lies (and How to Catch It)

title: 19  Endogeneity: Why Regression Lies (and How to Catch It)
text:
20.8 The Backdoor Criterion
A set of variables S satisfies Pearl’s backdoor criterion for estimating the effect of X on Y if:

S blocks every path between X and Y that has an arrow into X (i.e., all backdoor paths)
S does not include any descendant of X on a causal path to Y (no colliders, no mediators on the X→Y path)

When you can find such a set S from measured variables, controlling for S gives you the causal effect. When you cannot — because the confounders are unmeasured — you need the identification strategies in Parts 2–6.






ImportantBefore Running Any Regression



Draw a causal diagram. Every arrow is a hypothesis about the DGP. Then ask:

Are there any forks (confounders) not in my model? → OVB
Could the outcome affect the predictor? → Simultaneity
Is my sample selected on something related to both X and Y? → Selection
Am I controlling for anything caused by X or Y? → Collider bias

Answering these four questions before looking at your results will save you from the most common errors in applied regression.
crumbs: 19  Endogeneity: Why Regression Lies (and How to Catch It)

title: 20  Instrumental Variables
text: 21 Instrumental Variables

title: 20  Instrumental Variables
text:
21.1 When You Cannot Randomize
The gold standard for causal inference is a randomized experiment. But often you cannot randomize. An instrument is a variable that gives you some of the benefits of randomization without actually randomizing. It shifts X exogenously — through a channel that does not itself affect Y.

title: 20  Instrumental Variables
text:
21.2 The Three Requirements






WarningAssumption 1: Relevance



What it means: Z must actually cause X to change. An instrument that does not predict the predictor is useless (and produces infinite standard errors).
How to check: Regress X on Z (the “first stage”). The F-statistic on Z must be large — the standard rule of thumb is F > 10. Below 10, the instrument is “weak” and estimates become unreliable.








WarningAssumption 2: Exclusion Restriction



What it means: Z affects Y only through X — not through any other path. This is the key untestable assumption. You cannot verify it from data alone — you can only argue for it from theory and domain knowledge.
How to scrutinize: Trace every possible path from Z to Y. Is there any path that does not go through X?








WarningAssumption 3: Independence (Exogeneity of the Instrument)



What it means: Z is as-good-as-randomly assigned — it is not itself caused by the confounders that affect X and Y.
How to check: Verify that Z is balanced across observable pre-treatment covariates.

title: 20  Instrumental Variables
text:
21.3 Two-Stage Least Squares (TSLS)
Stage 1: Regress X on Z. Extract fitted values \(\hat{X}\) — the exogenous component of X.
Stage 2: Regress Y on \(\hat{X}\). This is the IV causal estimate.


Code
n <- 800

# Confounders: fatigue and commute stress
fatigue        <- rnorm(n, 50, 10)
commute_stress <- rnorm(n, 40, 8)

# Instrument: daylight saving time shift (exogenous clock reset)
DST_week <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.5)

# Time of day: influenced by DST AND by fatigue/stress
time_of_day <- 12 + 1.5 * DST_week - 0.1 * fatigue + rnorm(n, 0, 2)

# Variety seeking: affected by time of day AND by confounders
# True causal effect of time_of_day = 0.4
variety_seeking <- 3 + 0.4 * time_of_day - 0.2 * fatigue -
                   0.15 * commute_stress + rnorm(n, 0, 3)

df_iv <- tibble(variety_seeking, time_of_day, DST_week, fatigue, commute_stress)

# First stage: check instrument strength
m_first <- lm(time_of_day ~ DST_week, data = df_iv)
cat("First stage F-statistic:", round(summary(m_first)$fstatistic[1], 1), "\n")


First stage F-statistic: 113.1 


Code
# Naive OLS
m_ols <- lm(variety_seeking ~ time_of_day, data = df_iv)

# IV via TSLS
m_iv <- ivreg(variety_seeking ~ time_of_day | DST_week, data = df_iv)

bind_rows(
  tidy(m_ols) |> filter(term == "time_of_day") |> mutate(model = "OLS (biased)"),
  tidy(m_iv)  |> filter(term == "time_of_day") |> mutate(model = "IV / TSLS")
) |>
  select(model, estimate, std.error, p.value) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "OLS vs. IV: effect of time-of-day on variety seeking (true = 0.4)")



OLS vs. IV: effect of time-of-day on variety seeking (true = 0.4)


model
estimate
std.error
p.value




OLS (biased)
0.753
0.057
0.000


IV / TSLS
0.550
0.163
0.001





The OLS estimate is inflated by the confounders. The IV estimate recovers the true causal effect by using only the DST-driven variation in time-of-day.

title: 20  Instrumental Variables
text:
21.4 The LATE Interpretation
The IV estimate is not the population-average causal effect. It is the Local Average Treatment Effect (LATE): the causal effect for compliers — units whose treatment status actually changed because of the instrument. People unaffected by DST (those who keep the same schedule regardless) are uninformative for the IV estimate.
This limits generalizability. Whether the LATE is the quantity you care about depends on your research question.

title: 20  Instrumental Variables
text:
21.5 Finding Good Instruments
The exclusion restriction is the bottleneck. Common instrument classes in social science:








Instrument class
Example
Why valid




Natural experiment
Lottery assignment
Random by design


Regulatory threshold
Age cutoff for program
Arbitrary threshold


Geographic variation
Distance to hospital
Not chosen by individual


Time-based shock
Policy change, DST
Exogenous timing


Peer assignment
Random roommate assignment
Administrative randomization



The math is easy. Finding a credible instrument — one whose exclusion restriction holds and can be argued for — requires deep domain knowledge and creative thinking about your DGP.

title: 21  Selection Effects & Attrition
text: 22 Selection Effects & Attrition

title: 21  Selection Effects & Attrition
section: 22.1 When Your Sample is Not Your Population
text:
22.1 When Your Sample is Not Your Population
Even with a perfect model, if your observations are not representative of the population you care about, your conclusions may be wrong. Selection effects are the family of processes by which observations non-randomly enter or leave your dataset.
Selection is especially dangerous in nested and longitudinal data because the same processes that structure your data also drive who stays in it over time.

title: 21  Selection Effects & Attrition
text:
22.2 Type 1: Representativeness / Undercover Bias
Your sampling frame does not cover the full population you want to generalize to.
Secondary data example: Online review platform data. People who leave reviews are not a random sample — they felt strongly enough to write one (typically either very satisfied or very dissatisfied). Analyses generalize to “opinionated reviewers,” not “all customers.”
Experimental example: Convenience samples from university subject pools (WEIRD populations — Western, Educated, Industrialized, Rich, Democratic) may not generalize to the adults whose behavior you are studying.






WarningAssumption: Sampling Frame Covers the Population of Interest



How to check: Compare your sample’s distributions on key variables to known population statistics. Are any groups systematically over- or under-represented?
What to do: Acknowledge the limitation. Use stratified sampling. Apply post-stratification weights if population distributions are known.

title: 21  Selection Effects & Attrition
text:
22.3 Type 2: Self-Selection / Non-Response Bias
Participants choose whether to enter your study based on factors related to your variables of interest.
The landmark example: the 1936 Literary Digest poll predicted Landon would defeat Roosevelt by a landslide, based on 2.4 million responses. Their sampling frame (telephone directories and car registration lists) over-represented wealthy Republicans. Roosevelt won in a historic landslide.


Code
n_total    <- 1000
health_con <- rnorm(n_total, 50, 10)  # latent health consciousness

# Health-conscious people more likely to participate
p_participate <- plogis(-2 + 0.06 * health_con)
participated  <- rbinom(n_total, 1, p_participate)

# Outcome influenced by health consciousness
health_score <- 40 + 0.5 * health_con + rnorm(n_total, 0, 8)

tibble(
  Group    = c("Full population", "Self-selected sample"),
  N        = c(n_total, sum(participated)),
  Mean_health = c(mean(health_score),
                   mean(health_score[participated == 1]))
) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 2, caption = "Self-selection inflates estimated health level")



Self-selection inflates estimated health level


Group
N
Mean_health




Full population
1000
64.51


Self-selected sample
728
64.96

title: 21  Selection Effects & Attrition
text:
22.4 Type 3: Survivorship Bias
You only observe units that survived some selection filter correlated with your outcome.
WWII aircraft: Analysts studied bullet-hole patterns on returning planes to decide where to add armor. Abraham Wald pointed out: planes hit in the zero-hole areas never returned. Those were the critical spots to armor.
In longitudinal research: Studying predictors of firm success using currently existing firms excludes all firms that failed. If failure is related to your predictors, estimates are systematically optimistic.






WarningAssumption: No Survivorship Bias



How to check: Ask whether your outcome is systematically related to the probability of appearing in your dataset. If yes, you likely have survivorship bias.
What to do: Obtain historical data including non-survivors. Use survival analysis (Module 6, Part 3) to model time-to-exit explicitly.

title: 21  Selection Effects & Attrition
section: 22.5 Type 4: Attrition Bias in Longitudinal Data
text:
22.5 Type 4: Attrition Bias in Longitudinal Data
Participants drop out of your study non-randomly over time. This is one of the most serious threats to longitudinal research — and a direct consequence of the nested DGP of time.


Code
n_people <- 200
n_waves  <- 5

baseline_dep <- rnorm(n_people, 50, 10)
treatment    <- rbinom(n_people, 1, 0.5)

df_long <- expand_grid(person = 1:n_people, wave = 1:n_waves) |>
  mutate(
    baseline  = baseline_dep[person],
    treat     = treatment[person],
    depression = baseline - 5 * treat * (wave / n_waves) + rnorm(n(), 0, 4)
  )

# People with HIGH depression are more likely to drop out
df_long <- df_long |>
  group_by(person) |>
  mutate(
    drop_risk   = plogis(-3 + 0.04 * mean(depression)),
    dropped_out = cummax(rbinom(n(), 1, drop_risk)),
    observed    = dropped_out == 0 | wave == 1
  ) |>
  ungroup()

complete_cases <- df_long |>
  group_by(person) |> filter(all(observed)) |> ungroup()

tibble(
  Sample = c("All (Wave 1)", "Completers only"),
  N      = c(n_distinct(df_long$person[df_long$wave == 1]),
              n_distinct(complete_cases$person)),
  Mean_baseline_depression = c(
    mean(baseline_dep),
    mean(unique(complete_cases$baseline))
  )
) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 2,
    caption = "Attrition: completers have lower baseline depression (healthier)")



Attrition: completers have lower baseline depression (healthier)


Sample
N
Mean_baseline_depression




All (Wave 1)
200
49.76


Completers only
46
44.04







Code
# True treatment effect = -5
m_completers <- lm(depression ~ treat + wave + baseline, data = complete_cases)
m_all        <- lm(depression ~ treat + wave + baseline,
                   data = df_long |> filter(observed))

bind_rows(
  broom::tidy(m_completers) |> filter(term == "treat") |>
    mutate(model = "Complete cases (attrition bias)"),
  broom::tidy(m_all) |> filter(term == "treat") |>
    mutate(model = "All observed data")
) |>
  select(model, estimate, std.error, p.value) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "Attrition biases the treatment effect toward zero (true = -5)")



Attrition biases the treatment effect toward zero (true = -5)


model
estimate
std.error
p.value




Complete cases (attrition bias)
-2.942

All observed data
-2.781

0











WarningAssumption: Dropout Mechanism is Ignorable



MCAR: Dropout is unrelated to any variable. Very rare.
MAR: Dropout depends only on observed variables. If MAR holds, mixed-effects models using all available data (not just completers) give valid estimates.
MNAR: Dropout depends on the unobserved outcome itself. Requires sensitivity analysis or selection models (Heckman).
How to check: Compare baseline characteristics of completers vs. dropouts. If they differ substantially, dropout is non-random.

title: 21  Selection Effects & Attrition
text:
22.6 Type 5: Exclusion Bias
The researcher removes observations non-randomly — typically based on criteria correlated with the outcome.
The key principle: Any data deletion that is non-random creates selection bias. This includes removing “outliers” based on outcome values, excluding protocol violators after seeing results, or dropping countries/firms that seem “too different.”






TipPre-Registration as a Defense



Pre-registering exclusion criteria before data collection prevents post-hoc rationalization of exclusions that benefit your hypothesis. It does not eliminate the statistical bias, but it prevents the researcher from inadvertently introducing it.

title: 22  Regression Discontinuity Design
text: 23 Regression Discontinuity Design

title: 22  Regression Discontinuity Design
text:
23.1 The Threshold as a Quasi-Experiment
Suppose college admission is partly determined by a test score cutoff: applicants above 70 are admitted, those below are not. People just below 70 and just above 70 are otherwise very similar — nearly identical test scores, similar backgrounds, similar demographics. The only thing separating them is whether they fell above or below an arbitrary threshold.
This discontinuity in treatment assignment, driven by a continuous running variable, is the foundation of Regression Discontinuity Design (RDD).
The key insight: right at the threshold, treatment is as-good-as-randomly assigned, because small differences in the running variable near the cutoff are essentially random noise in the DGP.

title: 22  Regression Discontinuity Design
text:
23.2 The Core Assumption






WarningAssumption: Continuity of Potential Outcomes at the Threshold



What it means: In the absence of treatment, the expected outcome must be a continuous function of the running variable at the cutoff. The only reason outcomes should jump at the threshold is the treatment.
Why it fails: - Sorting/manipulation: If people can manipulate their running variable to cross the threshold (retaking tests, strategic reporting), those just above and just below are not comparable. - Other policies at the same cutoff: If another treatment also kicks in at exactly the same threshold, you cannot separate the effects. - Anticipation: If people change behavior in anticipation of crossing the threshold.
How to check: McCrary density test — a spike in the running variable just above the cutoff indicates manipulation.

title: 22  Regression Discontinuity Design
text:
23.3 A Worked Example


Code
n <- 1000
# Running variable: test score, centered at cutoff = 0
test_score <- rnorm(n, 0, 1)
admitted   <- as.integer(test_score >= 0)
# True causal effect of admission on earnings = 8
earnings   <- 50 + 5 * test_score + 8 * admitted + rnorm(n, 0, 5)

df_rdd <- tibble(test_score, admitted, earnings)

ggplot(df_rdd, aes(x = test_score, y = earnings, color = factor(admitted))) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.2, size = 1) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", formula = y ~ poly(x, 2), se = TRUE, linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "black", linewidth = 1) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#aec6e8", "#1e3a5f"),
                     labels = c("Not admitted", "Admitted")) +
  labs(title = "RDD: earnings vs. test score",
       subtitle = "Jump at the dashed cutoff = causal effect of admission",
       x = "Test Score (centered at cutoff)", y = "Earnings",
       color = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)











Code
rdd_result <- rdrobust(y = df_rdd$earnings, x = df_rdd$test_score, c = 0)
summary(rdd_result)


Sharp RD estimates using local polynomial regression.

Number of Obs.                 1000
BW type                       mserd

rdrobust uses local polynomial regression with an automatically selected optimal bandwidth.

title: 22  Regression Discontinuity Design
text:
23.4 The Density Test


Code
ggplot(df_rdd, aes(x = test_score)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 40, fill = "#1e3a5f", color = "white", alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, color = "#e63946", linewidth = 1.2, linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(title = "Density test: check for manipulation at the cutoff",
       subtitle = "A spike just right of the dashed line = sorting violation",
       x = "Test Score (centered at cutoff)", y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

title: 22  Regression Discontinuity Design
text:
23.5 Falsification Tests
Run the RDD on pre-treatment covariates (age, gender, family income). These cannot be caused by the treatment. A jump at the threshold in a covariate means something else is discontinuous there — a serious threat to validity.


Code
df_rdd <- df_rdd |> mutate(age = 18 + rnorm(n, 0, 1))
fake   <- rdrobust(y = df_rdd$age, x = df_rdd$test_score, c = 0)
cat("Jump in age at cutoff (should be ~0):", round(fake$coef[1], 3),
    "| p =", round(fake$pv[1], 3), "\n")


Jump in age at cutoff (should be ~0): -0.02 | p = 0.915

title: 22  Regression Discontinuity Design
text:
23.6 Bandwidth Sensitivity
The estimate should be stable as you vary the bandwidth around the default. Dramatic changes signal dependence on functional form.


Code
bws  <- seq(0.3, 1.5, by = 0.2)
ests <- sapply(bws, \(bw)
  rdrobust(y = df_rdd$earnings, x = df_rdd$test_score, c = 0, h = bw)$coef[1])

tibble(Bandwidth = bws, Estimate = ests) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = Bandwidth, y = Estimate)) +
  geom_line(color = "#1e3a5f", linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(size = 3, color = "#1e3a5f") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 8, linetype = "dashed", color = "#e63946") +
  labs(title = "Bandwidth sensitivity (red = true effect)",
       x = "Bandwidth", y = "Estimated Discontinuity") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

title: 23  Difference-in-Differences
text: 24 Difference-in-Differences

title: 23  Difference-in-Differences
text:
24.1 The Idea
A policy rolls out: some firms must adopt a new management practice; others are unaffected. You have productivity data from before and after. Can you estimate the effect?
The naive approach — compare treated and control firms after the rollout — is biased if they were already different. The Difference-in-Differences (DiD) approach subtracts the control group’s change from the treated group’s change:
\[\hat{\tau}_{DiD} = \underbrace{(\bar{Y}_{T,post} - \bar{Y}_{T,pre})}_{\text{treated group change}} - \underbrace{(\bar{Y}_{C,post} - \bar{Y}_{C,pre})}_{\text{control group change}}\]
The control group’s change serves as the counterfactual: what would have happened to treated firms if they had not been treated?

title: 23  Difference-in-Differences
text:
24.2 The Core Assumption






WarningAssumption: Parallel Trends



What it means: In the absence of treatment, treated and control units would have followed the same trend over time. They can start at different levels — DiD handles that. But their trajectories must be parallel.
How to check: 1. Visual inspection: Plot pre-treatment trends for both groups. They should look parallel. 2. Formal pre-trend test: Regress the outcome on treat × time using only pre-treatment data. Significant interaction = non-parallel trends. 3. Event study plot: Estimate separate treatment effects for each period. Pre-period coefficients should be near zero.
Why it fails: Treated and control units may have been on different trajectories before treatment — perhaps because units that selected into treatment were already changing faster. No statistical fix can rescue DiD if parallel trends are implausible given the selection process.

title: 23  Difference-in-Differences
text:
24.3 A Simulated Example


Code
n_firms <- 80
n_waves <- 6  # waves 1-3: pre; 4-6: post

firm_treat   <- c(rep(1, 40), rep(0, 40))
firm_base    <- rnorm(n_firms, 50, 8)

df_did <- expand_grid(firm = 1:n_firms, wave = 1:n_waves) |>
  mutate(
    treat      = firm_treat[firm],
    post       = as.integer(wave > 3),
    baseline   = firm_base[firm],
    trend      = (wave - 3.5) * 1.5,
    te         = treat * post * 10,
    productivity = baseline + trend + te + rnorm(n(), 0, 4)
  )

# Plot parallel pre-trends
group_means <- df_did |>
  group_by(wave, treat) |>
  summarise(mean_prod = mean(productivity), .groups = "drop") |>
  mutate(group = ifelse(treat == 1, "Treated", "Control"))

ggplot(group_means, aes(x = wave, y = mean_prod, color = group, linetype = group)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.3) + geom_point(size = 3) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 3.5, linetype = "dotted", color = "gray40") +
  annotate("text", x = 3.65, y = max(group_means$mean_prod),
           label = "Treatment", hjust = 0, color = "gray40", size = 3.5) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#aec6e8", "#1e3a5f")) +
  labs(title = "DiD: parallel pre-trends, divergence post-treatment",
       x = "Wave", y = "Productivity", color = NULL, linetype = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)











Code
m_did <- lm(productivity ~ treat * post + baseline, data = df_did)
tidy(m_did) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "`treat:post` interaction = DiD estimate (true = 10)")

title: 23  Difference-in-Differences
text:
24.5 Event Study Plot


Code
m_ev  <- lm(productivity ~ treat * factor(wave) + baseline, data = df_did)
ev_df <- tidy(m_ev, conf.int = TRUE) |>
  filter(str_detect(term, "treat:")) |>
  mutate(wave = as.integer(str_extract(term, "[0-9]+")),
         pre  = wave <= 3)

ggplot(ev_df, aes(x = wave, y = estimate, color = pre)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray60") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 3.5, linetype = "dotted", color = "gray40") +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = conf.low, ymax = conf.high), width = 0.2) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#1e3a5f", "#aec6e8"),
                     labels = c("Post-treatment", "Pre-treatment (should ≈ 0)")) +
  labs(title = "Event study: pre-period near zero, post-period shows effect",
       x = "Wave", y = "Estimated Treatment Effect", color = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

title: 23  Difference-in-Differences
text:
24.6 Additional Assumptions






WarningAssumption: SUTVA — No Spillovers



What it means: Treatment of unit A should not affect the outcomes of unit B. If treated firms compete with control firms and treatment improves treated performance at control firms’ expense, the control trend is contaminated.
How to check: Test whether control units near treated units trend differently from distant controls.








WarningAssumption: Stable Composition



What it means: The composition of treated and control groups should not change differentially after treatment. If the policy causes the most productive employees to leave treated firms, the post-treatment group is a different population.
How to check: Check whether observable characteristics of units change differentially post-treatment.

title: 24  Propensity Scores & Matching
text: 25 Propensity Scores & Matching

title: 24  Propensity Scores & Matching
text:
25.1 The Observational Study Problem
You want to estimate whether participating in a corporate wellness program improves employee health, using observational HR data. Employees self-selected into the program — healthier, more motivated employees are more likely to participate. A naive comparison of participants vs. non-participants will overestimate the benefit.
Propensity score methods reduce this bias by creating a comparison group similar to the treated group on all observed confounders. They work by estimating the probability that each unit received treatment — the propensity score — then matching or weighting on that score.

title: 24  Propensity Scores & Matching
text:
25.2 The Propensity Score
\[e(X) = P(\text{Treatment} = 1 \mid X)\]
The probability of receiving treatment given observed covariates. Key result (Rosenbaum & Rubin, 1983): conditioning on \(e(X)\) is equivalent to conditioning on all of \(X\) — you do not need to match on each covariate separately.


Code
n <- 600
age           <- rnorm(n, 40, 10)
health_aware  <- rnorm(n, 50, 12)
job_stress    <- rnorm(n, 45, 8)

# Treatment probability driven by confounders
p_treat   <- plogis(-4 + 0.05 * age + 0.06 * health_aware - 0.03 * job_stress)
treatment <- rbinom(n, 1, p_treat)

# True ATE = 5
health_score <- 40 + 0.3 * age + 0.4 * health_aware - 0.2 * job_stress +
                5 * treatment + rnorm(n, 0, 6)

df_ps <- tibble(age, health_aware, job_stress, treatment, health_score)

m_naive <- lm(health_score ~ treatment, data = df_ps)
cat("Naive estimate:", round(coef(m_naive)["treatment"], 2),
    "— true effect = 5\n")


Naive estimate: 10.11 — true effect = 5

title: 24  Propensity Scores & Matching
section: 25.3 Step 1: Estimate Propensity Scores and Check Overlap
text:
25.3 Step 1: Estimate Propensity Scores and Check Overlap


Code
ps_model <- glm(treatment ~ age + health_aware + job_stress,
                family = binomial, data = df_ps)
df_ps <- df_ps |> mutate(ps = fitted(ps_model))

ggplot(df_ps, aes(x = ps, fill = factor(treatment))) +
  geom_histogram(alpha = 0.6, bins = 30, position = "identity") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("#aec6e8", "#1e3a5f"),
                    labels = c("Control", "Treated")) +
  labs(title = "Overlap: propensity score distributions",
       subtitle = "Both groups must cover the same PS range for valid comparison",
       x = "Propensity Score", y = "Count", fill = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)















WarningAssumption 1: Overlap (Common Support)



What it means: Every unit must have a propensity score strictly between 0 and 1. Units with PS near 0 or 1 have no valid comparison units — causal inference is not possible for them.
How to check: Plot the PS distributions for treated and control groups. Trim units outside the common support region.

title: 24  Propensity Scores & Matching
text:
25.4 Step 2: Match and Check Balance


Code
m_out <- matchit(treatment ~ age + health_aware + job_stress,
                 data = df_ps, method = "nearest", ratio = 1)

# Balance summary
summary(m_out)$sum.matched |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "Balance after nearest-neighbor matching (std. diff. should be near 0)")

WarningAssumption 2: No Unmeasured Confounders (Ignorability)



What it means: Given observed covariates, treatment assignment is independent of potential outcomes. There are no unobserved variables that both predict treatment and affect the outcome.
This is the critical untestable assumption. Propensity score methods can only control for variables you measured. Any unobserved confounder still biases the estimate.
How to scrutinize: Conduct a sensitivity analysis (Rosenbaum bounds) asking: how large would an unmeasured confounder need to be to overturn your conclusion?

title: 24  Propensity Scores & Matching
text:
25.5 Step 3: Estimate the Treatment Effect


Code
matched_data <- match.data(m_out)

m_matched <- lm(health_score ~ treatment + age + health_aware + job_stress,
                data = matched_data, weights = weights)

tidy(m_matched) |> filter(term == "treatment") |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "Matched estimate of ATE (true = 5)")
