Code
# Who is missing? Compare baseline scores of completers vs. dropouts
df_person_missing <- df_obs |>
  group_by(person, baseline, treatment) |>
  summarise(
    complete = all(!is.na(Y_obs)),
    n_observed = sum(!is.na(Y_obs)),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

p1 <- ggplot(df_person_missing, aes(x = factor(n_observed), y = baseline)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "#1e3a5f", alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(
    title    = "Baseline score by number of waves observed",
    subtitle = "Lower baseline → more dropout (MAR mechanism)",
    x = "Number of waves with data",
    y = "Baseline score"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

p2 <- df_obs |>
  group_by(wave) |>
  summarise(pct_missing = mean(is.na(Y_obs)) * 100) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = wave, y = pct_missing)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", fill = "#e63946", alpha = 0.8) +
  labs(
    title    = "Missingness rate by wave",
    subtitle = "Attrition compounds across waves",
    x = "Wave", y = "% Missing"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

p1 + p2









The diagnostic pattern confirms the MAR mechanism: people with lower baseline scores drop out more over time. This is predictable from observed baseline — which means multiple imputation can recover unbiased estimates.
crumbs: 34  Missing Data in Longitudinal Studies

title: 34  Missing Data in Longitudinal Studies
crumbs: 34  Missing Data in Longitudinal Studies

title: 34  Missing Data in Longitudinal Studies
text:
35.5 Step 3: Multiple Imputation
Multiple Imputation (MI) is the gold standard for MAR data. The procedure has three steps:

Impute: Create \(m\) plausible complete datasets, each with missing values filled in by draws from the posterior predictive distribution given observed data.
Analyze: Run your analysis model on each of the \(m\) complete datasets.
Pool: Combine estimates using Rubin’s rules, which correctly propagate the uncertainty from imputation into the final standard errors.

For longitudinal data with person-level predictors, a practical strategy is to impute at the person level (one row per person, using person-level summaries) and then merge the imputed values back into the long-format data for analysis.


Code
# Create person-level summary for imputation
df_person <- df_obs |>
  group_by(person, treatment, baseline) |>
  summarise(
    mean_Y = mean(Y_obs, na.rm = TRUE),  # observed mean (available data)
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# Impute mean_Y at the person level using predictive mean matching
imp <- mice(
  df_person |> select(treatment, baseline, mean_Y),
  method = "pmm",
  m      = 20,
  maxit  = 10,
  print  = FALSE
)

# Merge back into long format and analyze
results_list <- lapply(1:20, function(k) {
  # Complete dataset k
  df_imp_person <- complete(imp, k) |>
    mutate(person = df_person$person)

  # Merge imputed mean back — for MI we impute the full longitudinal dataset
  # by filling in person-level missing observations proportionally
  df_long_imp <- df_obs |>
    left_join(df_imp_person |> select(person, mean_Y_imp = mean_Y),
              by = "person") |>
    mutate(
      Y_mi = ifelse(is.na(Y_obs), mean_Y_imp + rnorm(n(), 0, 3), Y_obs)
    )

  lmer(Y_mi ~ wave * treatment + (1 | person), data = df_long_imp, REML = FALSE)
})

# Pool results using Rubin's rules
pooled <- pool(results_list)

summary(pooled) |>
  filter(term == "wave:treatment") |>
  select(term, estimate, std.error, p.value) |>
  knitr::kable(
    digits = 3,
    caption = "MI pooled estimate: treatment × wave interaction. True effect ≈ 5."
  )



MI pooled estimate: treatment × wave interaction. True effect ≈ 5.


term
estimate
std.error
p.value




wave:treatment
3.535
0.367
0






35.5.1 Rubin’s Rules
The pooled estimate from \(m\) imputations is the simple average: \[\hat{\beta}_{pool} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{k=1}^m \hat{\beta}_k\]
The pooled variance combines within-imputation variance (average of the \(m\) standard errors) and between-imputation variance (variability across the \(m\) estimates):
\[V_{pool} = \bar{W} + \left(1 + \frac{1}{m}\right) B\]
where \(\bar{W} = \frac{1}{m}\sum V_k\) is the average within-imputation variance and \(B = \frac{1}{m-1}\sum(\hat\beta_k - \hat\beta_{pool})^2\) is the between-imputation variance. The term \((1 + 1/m)B\) inflates the standard error to reflect the extra uncertainty from imputing the missing values.
crumbs: 34  Missing Data in Longitudinal Studies

title: 34  Missing Data in Longitudinal Studies
text:
35.6 Step 4: Sensitivity Analysis for MNAR
Multiple imputation assumes MAR. If the dropout mechanism is MNAR — people drop out because of their outcome trajectory itself — imputation is biased. The appropriate response is a sensitivity analysis that asks: “How large would the MNAR bias have to be to change my conclusions?”
A simple approach: worst-case analysis with a tipping point calculation.


Code
# Worst-case sensitivity: what if dropouts had worse outcomes?
# Shift imputed values by a sensitivity parameter delta

delta_values <- seq(-10, 0, by = 2)

sensitivity_results <- map_dfr(delta_values, function(delta) {
  results_delta <- lapply(1:10, function(k) {
    df_imp_person <- complete(imp, k) |>
      mutate(person = df_person$person)

    df_long_imp <- df_obs |>
      left_join(df_imp_person |> select(person, mean_Y_imp = mean_Y), by = "person") |>
      mutate(
        Y_mi = ifelse(
          is.na(Y_obs),
          mean_Y_imp + delta + rnorm(n(), 0, 3),  # shift imputed values by delta
          Y_obs
        )
      )

    lmer(Y_mi ~ wave * treatment + (1 | person), data = df_long_imp, REML = FALSE)
  })

  pooled_delta <- pool(results_delta)
  est <- summary(pooled_delta) |> filter(term == "wave:treatment")
  tibble(delta = delta, estimate = est$estimate, p_value = est$p.value)
})

ggplot(sensitivity_results, aes(x = delta, y = estimate)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2, color = "#1e3a5f") +
  geom_point(size = 3, color = "#1e3a5f") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "#e63946") +
  labs(
    title    = "Sensitivity analysis: MNAR robustness check",
    subtitle = "Delta = assumed shift in imputed outcomes for dropouts (0 = MAR; negative = dropouts did worse)",
    x = "MNAR shift parameter (delta)",
    y = "Estimated treatment × time interaction"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)









The tipping point is the value of \(\delta\) at which the main conclusion changes (e.g., the treatment effect becomes non-significant). If the tipping point requires implausibly large deviations from MAR, the conclusion is robust. If the tipping point is small, the finding is fragile.
crumbs: 34  Missing Data in Longitudinal Studies

title: 34  Missing Data in Longitudinal Studies
text:
35.7 Step 5: Full Information Maximum Likelihood (FIML)
An alternative to multiple imputation is Full Information Maximum Likelihood (FIML). Instead of imputing missing values, FIML uses the likelihood of the observed data only — but the likelihood is computed across all available time points for each person, not just the complete cases.
In practice, lmer already implements FIML-like estimation when you use all available observations (available-case analysis). For structural equation models with missing data, the lavaan package’s missing = "fiml" argument provides explicit FIML.


Code
# FIML in lavaan (for SEM applications)
library(lavaan)

# Example: two-wave cross-lagged model with FIML for missingness
model_fiml <- "
  Y2 ~ Y1 + X1
  X2 ~ X1 + Y1
"

fit_fiml <- sem(model_fiml, data = your_data, missing = "fiml")


FIML and MI give asymptotically equivalent results under MAR when both are correctly specified. MI has the advantage of being applicable to any analysis (including those outside SEM), while FIML is baked into the estimation of a specific model. For standard mixed models with MAR data, available-case lmer is already essentially doing FIML.
crumbs: 34  Missing Data in Longitudinal Studies

title: 34  Missing Data in Longitudinal Studies
section: 35.8 A Decision Framework for Missing Data
text:
35.8 A Decision Framework for Missing Data






TipMissing Data Analysis Workflow




Diagnose: What is the rate of missingness by wave? Do dropouts differ from completers on observed variables?
Classify mechanism: MCAR (rare), MAR (common, treatable), or MNAR (common, serious)?
Choose primary analysis:

Available-case lmer is unbiased under MAR and correctly specified models
MI adds efficiency and allows including auxiliary variables that predict missingness but are not substantively of interest

Run sensitivity analysis: Tipping point analysis quantifies how much MNAR bias would be needed to invalidate conclusions.
Report: Report the missingness rate, the mechanism assumption, the analytic approach, and the sensitivity analysis. Transparency about missing data is a marker of rigorous longitudinal research.

title: 35  Overview
text: Module 5: Experimental Designs with Nesting

title: 35  Overview
text:
35.1 Experiments Are Not Flat
A well-designed randomized experiment is often treated as the gold standard for causal inference. And it is — when properly analyzed. The problem is that most experiments are analyzed as if every observation is an independent draw from a single population. They are not.
Even the simplest between-subjects experiment has nesting baked in:

Participants are a sample from a population of possible participants
Stimuli (vignettes, words, products, images) are a sample from a population of possible stimuli
Conditions may be nested within experimenters, sessions, labs, or time periods

When stimuli are treated as fixed — as if your 20 vignettes are the only 20 that exist — the generalization to other stimuli is silent. Your p-values are too small because stimulus-level variance is being absorbed into the residual, artificially tightening standard errors.
Judd, Westfall, & Kenny (2012, 2017) documented that this mistake is pervasive in social psychology and leads to systematic Type I error inflation.

title: 35  Overview
text:
35.2 What This Module Covers
Part 1: Crossed Random Effects in Experiments — Why stimuli are random, what happens when you treat them as fixed, and how to fix it with crossed random effects models in lme4.
Part 2: Partially Nested Designs — When one condition has nesting (e.g., treatment participants have therapists/coaches) and the other does not, standard MLM misspecifies the design. The Bauer et al. partially nested model handles this correctly.
Part 3: Maximal vs. Parsimonious Random Effects — The Barr et al. (2013) vs. Matuschek et al. (2017) debate: how complex should your random effects structure be? When does complexity help and when does it hurt?
Part 4: Multi-Site Experiments — When the same experiment runs at multiple sites (labs, countries, platforms), sites introduce clustering. The many-labs design and how to model treatment effect heterogeneity across sites.
Part 5: Power Analysis for Nested Designs — Why standard power formulas are wrong for nested data, the role of ICC, simulation-based power analysis, and practical sample size guidance.

title: 36  Crossed Random Effects in Experiments
text: 37 Crossed Random Effects in Experiments
crumbs: 36  Crossed Random Effects in Experiments

title: 36  Crossed Random Effects in Experiments
text:
37.1 The Stimuli Problem
Imagine a social psychology experiment studying whether a message framed as a gain (“you will save $100”) versus a loss (“you will lose $100”) influences willingness to act. You write 12 gain-framed messages and 12 loss-framed messages. Each of 80 participants reads 6 messages (3 gain, 3 loss) and rates their likelihood of acting.
The standard analysis: participant is the random factor, message condition is the fixed factor. This treats your 12+12 messages as if they were the only gain and loss messages that exist — as if your results generalize to the condition (framing), not to the population of possible messages.
But your 12 gain messages are a sample from all possible gain-framing messages. Some are compelling; others are weak. If you happened to write unusually persuasive loss messages, your “framing effect” is inflated. The variance among messages is not residual noise — it is a second source of random variation that your model must account for.
Failing to include message as a random factor inflates Type I error. Judd, Westfall & Kenny (2012) showed this using the “languages as fixed effects fallacy” as the model, but the problem extends to any stimulus set.
crumbs: 36  Crossed Random Effects in Experiments

title: 36  Crossed Random Effects in Experiments
text:
37.2 The Crossed Random Effects Solution
Both participants and messages are random. Every participant sees multiple messages; every message is seen by multiple participants. They are crossed — neither is nested within the other.


Code
n_participants <- 60
n_messages     <- 24  # 12 gain, 12 loss

participant_effects <- rnorm(n_participants, 0, 8)
message_effects     <- rnorm(n_messages, 0, 6)
message_condition   <- rep(c("gain", "loss"), each = 12)

# Each participant sees 6 messages (3 gain, 3 loss)
# We'll simulate a fully crossed subset for clarity
df_exp <- expand_grid(
  participant = 1:n_participants,
  message     = 1:n_messages
) |>
  filter((message <= 12 & participant %% 2 == 1) |
         (message > 12  & participant %% 2 == 0) |
         sample(c(TRUE, FALSE), n(), replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.25, 0.75))) |>
  mutate(
    condition    = message_condition[message],
    p_eff        = participant_effects[participant],
    m_eff        = message_effects[message],
    # True framing effect: loss > gain by 3 points
    WTP          = 50 + 3 * (condition == "loss") + p_eff + m_eff + rnorm(n(), 0, 5),
    participant  = factor(participant),
    message      = factor(message)
  )




Code
# Wrong: only participant as random effect (ignores message variance)
m_wrong <- lmer(WTP ~ condition + (1 | participant), data = df_exp)

# Correct: both participant and message as random effects
m_correct <- lmer(WTP ~ condition + (1 | participant) + (1 | message), data = df_exp)

# Compare SE for the condition effect
bind_rows(
  broom.mixed::tidy(m_wrong,   effects = "fixed") |> filter(term == "conditionloss") |>
    mutate(model = "Wrong (no message RE)"),
  broom.mixed::tidy(m_correct, effects = "fixed") |> filter(term == "conditionloss") |>
    mutate(model = "Correct (participant + message RE)")
) |>
  select(model, estimate, std.error, statistic, p.value) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "Ignoring message variance shrinks the SE — anti-conservative inference")



Ignoring message variance shrinks the SE — anti-conservative inference









model
estimate
std.error
statistic
p.value




Wrong (no message RE)
1.579
0.710
2.224
0.026


Correct (participant + message RE)
1.776
2.935
0.605
0.551





The model that ignores message variance has a smaller standard error — it looks more precise — but it is wrong. Message-level variance is being folded into the residual, making the estimate look more reliable than it is.
crumbs: 36  Crossed Random Effects in Experiments

title: 36  Crossed Random Effects in Experiments
text:
37.3 Assumptions






WarningAssumption: Both Participants and Stimuli are Representative Samples



What it means: The crossed random effects model generalizes over both the sampled participants and the sampled stimuli. If your stimuli are not representative of the population you want to generalize to (e.g., you hand-selected the most extreme examples), the generalization is invalid.
How to check: Reflect on how your stimuli were selected. Were they sampled systematically, or chosen to maximize the effect? If the latter, your results may not replicate with a different stimulus set.








WarningAssumption: Random Effects are Independent of the Experimental Manipulation



What it means: The random intercept for message \(m\) should be unrelated to whether that message is in the gain or loss condition. This is automatically satisfied in a well-designed experiment where condition is randomly assigned to messages.
How to check: In a fully crossed design (every participant sees every message), this is guaranteed. In partial crossing, verify that assignment was randomized.
crumbs: 36  Crossed Random Effects in Experiments

title: 36  Crossed Random Effects in Experiments
section: 37.4 The Westfall, Kenny & Judd (2014) Power Argument
text:
37.4 The Westfall, Kenny & Judd (2014) Power Argument
Westfall, Kenny & Judd (2014) extended this analysis to show that power depends on both the number of participants and the number of stimuli. Adding more participants when you have only a few stimuli can give you very little additional power — because stimulus-level variance is the binding constraint.
This has direct implications for sample size planning: you should increase your stimulus set, not just your participant count.
crumbs: 36  Crossed Random Effects in Experiments

title: 37  Partially Nested Designs
text: 38 Partially Nested Designs

title: 37  Partially Nested Designs
section: 38.1 When Only One Condition Has Clustering
text:
38.1 When Only One Condition Has Clustering
Consider a psychotherapy trial. The treatment group receives group therapy: 8 clients per therapist, 15 therapists. The control group receives no treatment (waitlist) — there is no therapist and no group structure. Clients in the control condition are independent of each other.
This design is partially nested: the treatment condition has a natural cluster structure (clients within therapists/groups), but the control condition does not.
If you fit a standard two-level model treating all participants the same way, you either: 1. Ignore clustering in the treatment group — deflating standard errors and inflating Type I error for the treatment effect 2. Apply random effects to all participants — misspecifying the control group, which has no actual clustering
The partially nested model (Bauer, Sterba & Hallfors, 2008) handles this correctly by allowing different variance structures in different conditions.

title: 37  Partially Nested Designs
text:
38.2 Simulating a Partially Nested Design


Code
n_therapists <- 15
n_per_group  <- 8
n_control    <- n_therapists * n_per_group  # same total N

# Treatment group: therapist clustering (ICC ≈ 0.15)
therapist_effects <- rnorm(n_therapists, 0, 3)

df_treatment <- tibble(
  condition   = "treatment",
  therapist   = rep(1:n_therapists, each = n_per_group),
  client      = 1:(n_therapists * n_per_group),
  therapist_e = rep(therapist_effects, each = n_per_group),
  outcome     = 50 + 5 + therapist_e + rnorm(n_therapists * n_per_group, 0, 6)
)

# Control group: no clustering (independent)
df_control <- tibble(
  condition   = "control",
  therapist   = NA_integer_,  # no therapist
  client      = 1:n_control + max(df_treatment$client),
  therapist_e = 0,
  outcome     = 50 + rnorm(n_control, 0, 6)
)

df_pn <- bind_rows(df_treatment, df_control)

title: 37  Partially Nested Designs
section: 38.3 The Partially Nested Model in lme4
text:
38.3 The Partially Nested Model in lme4
The trick: create a dummy therapist variable for control participants (unique ID for each, indicating no shared clustering) and fit a model where the random effect only applies to the treatment group.


Code
# Assign unique "therapist" IDs to control participants
df_pn <- df_pn |>
  mutate(
    cluster_id = ifelse(condition == "treatment",
                        paste0("T", therapist),
                        paste0("C", client))  # each control is own "cluster"
  )

# Partially nested: cluster RE only in treatment
# Use dummy coding and interaction for the random effect
df_pn <- df_pn |>
  mutate(treat_dummy = as.integer(condition == "treatment"))

# Fit: allow random effect only within treatment clusters
m_pn <- lmer(outcome ~ condition + (1 | cluster_id), data = df_pn)

# Compare to naive model (ignores all clustering)
m_naive <- lm(outcome ~ condition, data = df_pn)

bind_rows(
  broom::tidy(m_naive) |> filter(term == "conditiontreatment") |>
    mutate(model = "Naive (no clustering)"),
  broom.mixed::tidy(m_pn, effects = "fixed") |>
    filter(term == "conditiontreatment") |>
    mutate(model = "Partially nested model")
) |>
  select(model, estimate, std.error, p.value) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "Partially nested model: correct SE for clustered treatment group")



Partially nested model: correct SE for clustered treatment group


model
estimate
std.error
p.value




Naive (no clustering)
4.295
0.795
0


Partially nested model
4.295
0.932
0











WarningAssumption: Correct Identification of Which Condition is Clustered



What it means: You must correctly specify which condition has clustering and which does not. If clustering is misattributed (e.g., you apply cluster random effects to the control group when there is none), variance components will be estimated incorrectly.
How to check: Draw the design. Which participants share a natural group, coach, therapist, or classroom? That condition has the nested structure.








WarningAssumption: Homogeneity of Within-Cluster Variance



What it means: The standard partially nested model assumes the residual variance is the same in the treatment and control groups (only the random effect variance differs).
How to check: Compare within-group variance across conditions. If the treatment group has substantially more or less residual variance than control, allow heteroskedastic residuals.

title: 38  Maximal vs. Parsimonious Random Effects
text: 39 Maximal vs. Parsimonious Random Effects
crumbs: 38  Maximal vs. Parsimonious Random Effects

title: 38  Maximal vs. Parsimonious Random Effects
text:
39.1 The Debate
In 2013, Barr, Levy, Scheepers, & Tily published an influential paper titled “Random Effects Structure for Confirmatory Hypothesis Testing: Keep It Maximal.” Their argument:

To control Type I error at the nominal level, researchers should include the maximal random effects structure — every random slope that is justified by the experimental design.

In a within-subjects design where every participant sees every condition, you must include a random slope for condition by participants. Omitting this random slope will produce anti-conservative p-values.
Two years later, Matuschek, Kliegl, Vasishth, Baayen, & Bates (2017) pushed back in a paper titled “Balancing Type I Error and Power in Linear Mixed Models.” Their argument:

Maximal models frequently fail to converge or produce degenerate variance components. Over-parameterized models can actually have worse power than well-specified parsimonious models. Use likelihood ratio tests to decide what to include.

Both papers are correct. The question is when each applies.
crumbs: 38  Maximal vs. Parsimonious Random Effects

title: 38  Maximal vs. Parsimonious Random Effects
text:
39.2 Why Maximal Models Can Fail


Code
# Simulate a within-subjects design
# Each participant sees each condition 4 times (trials)
# 40 participants × 3 conditions × 4 trials = 480 observations
# Random effects needed: 40 × 3 = 120 — well below 480
n_participants <- 40
n_conditions   <- 3
n_trials       <- 4

participant_intercepts <- rnorm(n_participants, 50, 8)
# Small (but nonzero) random slopes
participant_slopes     <- rnorm(n_participants, 0, 0.5)

df_within <- expand_grid(
  participant = 1:n_participants,
  condition   = 1:n_conditions,
  trial       = 1:n_trials
) |>
  mutate(
    condition_eff = c(-2, 0, 2)[condition],  # condition effect
    p_slope       = participant_slopes[participant],
    Y = participant_intercepts[participant] + condition_eff +
        p_slope * condition_eff + rnorm(n(), 0, 5),
    participant = factor(participant),
    condition   = factor(condition)
  )

# Maximal model
m_max <- lmer(Y ~ condition + (condition | participant),
              data    = df_within,
              control = lmerControl(optimizer = "bobyqa"))

# Does it converge?
library(performance)
check_convergence(m_max)


[1] TRUE
attr(,"gradient")
[1] 7.545173e-07


Code
# Parsimonious model (random slope variance is tiny — can drop it)
m_pars <- lmer(Y ~ condition + (1 | participant), data = df_within)




Code
# Compare: do the p-values differ substantially?
anova(m_pars, m_max)

title: 38  Maximal vs. Parsimonious Random Effects
text:
39.3 The Decision Framework
The current consensus among methodologists:






NoteWhen to Use Maximal Random Effects




Within-subjects experimental manipulations: Always include random slopes for within-subjects factors. This is the critical case. Barr et al. (2013) are correct: omitting random slopes for within-subject conditions inflates Type I error.
When theory or pilot data suggests heterogeneous effects: If you have prior reason to believe the treatment effect varies substantially across participants, include the random slope.
Large samples: With enough data, complex models converge and the efficiency loss from over-parameterization is small.









NoteWhen to Simplify (Parsimonious)




Convergence failures: If the maximal model does not converge, you must simplify. Start by removing the correlation between random intercept and slope ((1 | participant) + (0 + condition | participant)). Then consider removing the random slope itself.
Degenerate variance components: If the random slope variance is estimated at exactly zero (or very near), it is contributing nothing and can be dropped.
Very small variance components: Use an LRT to test whether the random slope significantly improves fit. If not, the parsimonious model is appropriate.
crumbs: 38  Maximal vs. Parsimonious Random Effects

title: 38  Maximal vs. Parsimonious Random Effects
text:
39.4 A Practical Simplification Protocol


Code
# Step 1: Try the maximal model
m_max_2 <- tryCatch(
  lmer(Y ~ condition + (condition | participant),
       data = df_within, control = lmerControl(optimizer = "bobyqa")),
  warning = function(w) { cat("Warning:", conditionMessage(w), "\n"); NULL }
)

# Step 2: If it fails/has issues, remove correlation first
m_no_corr <- lmer(Y ~ condition + (1 | participant) + (0 + condition | participant),
                  data = df_within)

# Step 3: Test: does the random slope improve fit?
anova(m_pars, m_no_corr)

# If p > 0.05 on the LRT, the random slope does not help — use m_pars








WarningAssumption: The Random Effects Structure Matches the Design



What it means: The random effects you include should reflect the actual nesting structure of your design. A random slope for a between-subjects factor makes no statistical sense (between-subjects factors cannot vary within a subject).
Common mistake: Including a random slope for a between-subjects condition. This will either produce a degenerate model or absorb all variance in a nonsensical way.
How to check: For each predictor, ask: does this variable vary within the grouping unit (participant, stimulus)? If yes, it can have a random slope for that unit. If no (between-subjects), it cannot.
crumbs: 38  Maximal vs. Parsimonious Random Effects

title: 39  Multi-Site Experiments
text: 40 Multi-Site Experiments

title: 39  Multi-Site Experiments
text:
40.1 Generalizability is a Nesting Problem
The replication crisis in psychology revealed that many findings were fragile — they held at one university, with one sample, run by one experimenter, but failed to replicate elsewhere. The problem is that site is a nesting variable that standard analysis ignores.
A multi-site experiment (also called a many-labs design) runs the same study across multiple locations: different universities, countries, online platforms, or time periods. Each site contributes data. Sites are treated as a random sample from a larger population of sites.
This design answers a richer question: not “does this effect occur at location X?” but “does this effect occur, on average, across the population of contexts?” And it allows you to ask: how much does the effect size vary across sites?

title: 39  Multi-Site Experiments
text:
40.2 The Model
Sites are random. The treatment effect may vary across sites (cross-level interaction between treatment and site).
\[Y_{ijk} = \gamma_{00} + \gamma_{10} \text{Treat}_{ij} + u_{0k} + u_{1k} \text{Treat}_{ij} + \varepsilon_{ijk}\]
Where: - \(k\) = site; \(j\) = participant; \(i\) = observation - \(u_{0k}\): site-level random intercept (baseline differences across sites) - \(u_{1k}\): site-level random slope for treatment (heterogeneity in treatment effect) - \(\gamma_{10}\): average treatment effect across sites


Code
n_sites       <- 20
n_per_site    <- 40  # 20 treatment, 20 control

site_intercepts <- rnorm(n_sites, 50, 5)   # sites differ in baseline
site_effects    <- rnorm(n_sites, 5, 3)    # treatment effect varies across sites
# Average effect = 5; some sites show 8, some show 2

df_ml <- expand_grid(
  site = 1:n_sites,
  participant = 1:n_per_site
) |>
  mutate(
    treatment   = rep(c(rep(0, 20), rep(1, 20)), n_sites),
    site_base   = site_intercepts[site],
    site_effect = site_effects[site],
    outcome     = site_base + site_effect * treatment + rnorm(n(), 0, 6),
    site        = factor(site)
  )




Code
# Random intercepts and slopes for sites
m_ml <- lmer(outcome ~ treatment + (treatment | site), data = df_ml)

broom.mixed::tidy(m_ml, effects = "fixed") |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "Average treatment effect across sites")

# Forest plot: site-specific effect estimates
site_estimates <- coef(m_ml)$site |>
  as.data.frame() |>
  rownames_to_column("site") |>
  rename(intercept = `(Intercept)`, effect = treatment) |>

  geom_hline(yintercept = fixef(m_ml)["treatment"],
             color = "#e63946", linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(size = 3, color = "#1e3a5f") +

title: 39  Multi-Site Experiments
text:
40.3 The ICC for Treatment Effects
The random slope for treatment tells you how much the effect varies across sites. The I² statistic (from meta-analysis) provides a related measure: what proportion of the total variation in effect size estimates is due to genuine heterogeneity vs. sampling error?


Code
# Variance of the treatment effect across sites
var_treatment_effect <- as.data.frame(VarCorr(m_ml))$vcov[2]  # slope variance
cat("Standard deviation of treatment effect across sites:",
    round(sqrt(var_treatment_effect), 2), "\n")


Standard deviation of treatment effect across sites: 2.7 


Code
cat("Average effect:", round(fixef(m_ml)["treatment"], 2), "\n")


Average effect: 5.04 


Code
cat("Effect ranges approximately from",
    round(fixef(m_ml)["treatment"] - 2*sqrt(var_treatment_effect), 1),
    "to",
    round(fixef(m_ml)["treatment"] + 2*sqrt(var_treatment_effect), 1),
    "across sites\n")


Effect ranges approximately from -0.4 to 10.4 across sites








WarningAssumption: Sites are a Representative Sample



What it means: The multi-site model generalizes to the population of sites from which your sites were drawn. If your 20 labs are all top-10 research universities, the results generalize to that population — not to community colleges, online platforms, or international contexts.
How to check: Describe your site-selection process. Were sites systematically sampled? Opportunistically recruited? The inference should match the selection.








WarningAssumption: Treatment Procedure is Comparable Across Sites



What it means: The “treatment” must be operationally equivalent across sites. If different labs implemented the procedure differently — different materials, different timing, different experimenter instructions — site-to-site variability reflects procedure differences, not true effect heterogeneity.
How to check: Pre-specify and enforce standardized protocols. Measure implementation fidelity. Include implementation fidelity as a moderator.

title: 40  Power Analysis for Nested Designs
text: 41 Power Analysis for Nested Designs
crumbs: 40  Power Analysis for Nested Designs

title: 40  Power Analysis for Nested Designs
section: 41.1 Why Standard Power Formulas Are Wrong
text:
41.1 Why Standard Power Formulas Are Wrong
Standard power formulas (Cohen’s power tables, G*Power) assume independent observations. They compute: given my expected effect size and desired power, how many participants do I need?
In nested data, this question is incomplete. You also need to know: - How many clusters? (classrooms, firms, sites, therapists) - How many observations per cluster? - What is the ICC? (how similar are observations within clusters)
The ICC and cluster size together determine the design effect (DEFF), which shrinks your effective sample size:
\[N_{effective} = \frac{N}{1 + (n_{cluster} - 1) \times \text{ICC}}\]
Ignoring this leads to severely underpowered studies.
crumbs: 40  Power Analysis for Nested Designs

title: 40  Power Analysis for Nested Designs
text:
41.2 The ICC-Power Relationship


Code
# Power as a function of ICC, for a fixed N = 300 in clusters of 15
compute_power <- function(icc, n_total = 300, cluster_size = 15, effect = 0.3, alpha = 0.05) {
  deff    <- 1 + (cluster_size - 1) * icc
  n_eff   <- n_total / deff
  # Two-sample t-test power with effective N
  power.t.test(n = n_eff / 2, delta = effect, sd = 1, sig.level = alpha)$power
}

icc_vals <- seq(0, 0.5, by = 0.01)
power_vals <- sapply(icc_vals, compute_power)

tibble(ICC = icc_vals, Power = power_vals) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = ICC, y = Power)) +
  geom_line(color = "#1e3a5f", linewidth = 1.3) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.80, linetype = "dashed", color = "#e63946") +
  annotate("text", x = 0.35, y = 0.82, label = "80% power threshold",
           color = "#e63946", size = 3.5) +
  labs(
    title    = "Power declines as ICC increases (N = 300, cluster size = 15)",
    subtitle = "Even modest ICC substantially reduces effective power",
    x        = "ICC",
    y        = "Statistical Power"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)









At ICC = 0, you have the expected power for 300 independent observations. At ICC = 0.2, power drops below 80% — you would need more observations to compensate.
crumbs: 40  Power Analysis for Nested Designs

title: 40  Power Analysis for Nested Designs
text:
41.3 Simulation-Based Power Analysis
For complex nested designs, analytic formulas do not always exist. The gold standard is simulation-based power analysis: generate data from the assumed DGP, fit the model, check whether the effect is significant, repeat 1000 times.


Code
simulate_power <- function(n_clusters, n_per_cluster, icc,
                            true_effect = 0.3, n_sims = 500) {
  sigma_between <- sqrt(icc)
  sigma_within  <- sqrt(1 - icc)

  p_values <- replicate(n_sims, {
    # Simulate two groups of clusters
    n_c <- ceiling(n_clusters / 2)
    cluster_effects <- rnorm(n_clusters, 0, sigma_between)

    df <- tibble(
      cluster = rep(1:n_clusters, each = n_per_cluster),
      group   = ifelse(cluster <= n_c, 0, 1)
    ) |>
      mutate(
        Y = true_effect * group + cluster_effects[cluster] +
            rnorm(n(), 0, sigma_within)
      )

    tryCatch({
      m <- lmer(Y ~ group + (1 | cluster), data = df, REML = FALSE)
      coef(summary(m))["group", "Pr(>|t|)"]
    }, error = function(e) NA_real_,
       warning = function(w) {
         m <- lmer(Y ~ group + (1 | cluster), data = df, REML = FALSE)
         tryCatch(coef(summary(m))["group", "Pr(>|t|)"],
                  error = function(e) NA_real_)
       })
  })
  p_values <- p_values[!is.na(p_values)]

  mean(p_values < 0.05)
}

# Power across different designs
designs <- expand_grid(
  n_clusters      = c(10, 20, 30, 40),
  n_per_cluster   = c(10, 20),
  icc             = c(0.05, 0.15)
) |>
  mutate(
    power = pmap_dbl(
      list(n_clusters, n_per_cluster, icc),
      ~simulate_power(..1, ..2, ..3, n_sims = 200)  # reduce for speed
    ),
    total_N = n_clusters * n_per_cluster,
    design  = paste0("ICC=", icc, ", n/cluster=", n_per_cluster)
  )

ggplot(designs, aes(x = n_clusters, y = power, color = design, linetype = design)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) + geom_point(size = 2.5) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.80, linetype = "dotted", color = "gray50") +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  labs(
    title    = "Power by number of clusters (simulated)",
    subtitle = "Effect size d = 0.3; n_sims = 200 per design",
    x        = "Number of Clusters",
    y        = "Power",
    color    = "Design", linetype = "Design"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)
crumbs: 40  Power Analysis for Nested Designs

title: 40  Power Analysis for Nested Designs
section: 41.4 The simr Package for Complex Designs
text:
41.4 The simr Package for Complex Designs
For multilevel models with random slopes, crossed random effects, or other complex structures, the simr package provides a convenient simulation-based power analysis directly from a fitted model:


Code
library(simr)

# Fit a model with your expected parameters
m_pilot <- lmer(Y ~ group + (1 | cluster),
                data = your_pilot_data)

# Change the effect size to your expected value
fixef(m_pilot)["group"] <- 0.3

# Simulate power for different sample sizes
power_curve <- powerCurve(m_pilot,
                           along = "cluster",   # vary number of clusters
                           nsim  = 200)
plot(power_curve)
crumbs: 40  Power Analysis for Nested Designs

title: 40  Power Analysis for Nested Designs
text:
41.5 Key Takeaways for Power Planning






NoteRules of Thumb




More clusters > more per cluster when ICC is high. If ICC = 0.20, doubling the number of clusters gains much more power than doubling the size of each cluster.
The ICC threshold: If ICC > 0.10 and cluster size > 10, standard power formulas underestimate required N by 50–100%.
For crossed designs (participants × stimuli): Power depends on both the number of participants and the number of stimuli. Westfall, Kenny & Judd (2014) showed that many published studies are severely underpowered on the stimulus side.
Collect pilot ICC estimates: Run a small pilot study and compute the ICC before committing to a full design.
crumbs: 40  Power Analysis for Nested Designs

title: 41  Binary and Count Outcomes in Experiments
text: 42 Binary and Count Outcomes in Experiments
crumbs: 41  Binary and Count Outcomes in Experiments

title: 41  Binary and Count Outcomes in Experiments
section: 42.1 The Problem: When Experimental Outcomes Aren’t Continuous
text:
42.1 The Problem: When Experimental Outcomes Aren’t Continuous
The experimental designs covered in this module — between-subjects, within-subjects, and mixed factorial — all assumed a continuous, roughly normally distributed outcome. But many experimental outcomes in psychology, marketing, and behavioral science are not continuous:

Behavioral choices: Did the participant donate? Choose the prosocial option? Click the ad?
Count behaviors: How many times did the participant help a confederate? How many words did they produce? How many items did they correctly recall?
Errors and successes: How many trials did the participant complete correctly?

Fitting a linear mixed model to a binary or count outcome is not just inefficient — it misspecifies the DGP. Predicted probabilities can exceed 1 or fall below 0. Residuals are structured (they depend on the mean), not random. Parameter estimates are biased.
Generalized Linear Mixed Models (GLMMs) extend the experimental mixed model framework to handle these outcome types correctly. The nesting structure (observations within participants, participants within conditions or groups) is handled identically to the continuous case. The difference is in the link function and distributional family.
crumbs: 41  Binary and Count Outcomes in Experiments

title: 41  Binary and Count Outcomes in Experiments
text:
42.2 Part 1: Binary Outcomes in Experiments

42.2.1 The Logistic Mixed Model for Experiments
For a binary outcome \(Y_{ij} \in \{0,1\}\) at trial \(i\) for participant \(j\), in a two-condition within-subjects design:
\[\text{logit}\bigl(P(Y_{ij} = 1)\bigr) = \underbrace{\beta_0}_{\substack{\text{average log-odds} \\ \text{at baseline}}} + \underbrace{u_{0j}}_{\substack{\text{person's tendency} \\ \text{to say yes}}} + \underbrace{\beta_1 \cdot \text{condition}_{ij}}_{\substack{\text{condition effect} \\ \text{on log-odds}}}\]
The random intercept \(u_{0j}\) captures stable individual differences in response tendency — some people are more likely to help, donate, or choose the prosocial option regardless of the manipulation. Failing to include the random intercept would ignore the clustering from repeated measurements, deflate standard errors, and produce overconfident inferences.


42.2.2 Example: Framing Effects on Donation Decisions
We simulate a within-subjects framing experiment. 80 participants each evaluate 16 donation appeals — half framed in gain terms (“saves 200 lives”), half in loss terms (“200 people will die”). The outcome is binary: did they decide to donate (1) or not (0)?
The true donation effect of loss framing is an odds ratio of 1.8 — loss-framed appeals have 80% higher odds of donation, on average. People also differ substantially in their baseline donation tendency (captured by the random intercept).


Code
n_participants <- 80
n_trials_each  <- 8  # 8 gain-framed + 8 loss-framed trials per participant

# Person-level random intercepts (log-odds scale)
u0_person <- rnorm(n_participants, 0, 1.2)  # substantial individual differences

df_donate <- expand_grid(
  participant = 1:n_participants,
  framing     = c("gain", "loss"),
  trial       = 1:n_trials_each
) |>
  mutate(
    u0       = u0_person[participant],
    framing_dummy = as.integer(framing == "loss"),
    log_odds = -0.5 + u0 + 0.59 * framing_dummy,  # log(1.8) ≈ 0.59
    prob     = plogis(log_odds),
    donated  = rbinom(n(), 1, prob),
    participant = factor(participant),
    framing  = factor(framing, levels = c("gain", "loss"))
  )

# Observed donation rates by condition
df_donate |>
  group_by(framing) |>
  summarise(
    n_trials      = n(),
    donation_rate = mean(donated),
    .groups       = "drop"
  ) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3, caption = "Observed donation rates by framing condition")



Observed donation rates by framing condition


framing
n_trials
donation_rate




gain
640
0.384


loss
640
0.506







42.2.3 Fitting and Interpreting the Model


Code
m_donate <- glmer(
  donated ~ framing + (1 | participant),
  data   = df_donate,
  family = binomial
)

# Results as odds ratios
broom.mixed::tidy(m_donate, effects = "fixed", exponentiate = TRUE, conf.int = TRUE) |>
  filter(term != "(Intercept)") |>
  select(term, estimate, conf.low, conf.high, p.value) |>
  knitr::kable(
    digits = 3,
    caption = "Framing effect as odds ratio. True OR = 1.8; loss framing increases donation odds."
  )



Framing effect as odds ratio. True OR = 1.8; loss framing increases donation odds.


term
estimate
conf.low
conf.high
p.value




framingloss
1.872
1.458
2.404
0





The odds ratio says: among participants with the same random intercept, the odds of donating in the loss-framing condition are estimate times the odds in the gain-framing condition.


42.2.4 Marginal Predicted Probabilities
Odds ratios are the correct internal currency of the logistic model, but they are unintuitive for most audiences. Report predicted probabilities:


Code
preds_donate <- ggpredict(m_donate, terms = "framing", type = "fixed")

plot(preds_donate) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#1e3a5f", "#e63946")) +
  labs(
    title    = "Predicted donation probability by framing condition",
    subtitle = "Marginal predictions (averaging over individual differences)",
    x = "Framing condition",
    y = "Predicted probability of donating"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)











42.2.5 The Nonlinearity of Marginal Effects
In logistic models, the effect of a predictor on the probability scale depends on where you are. An odds ratio of 1.8 translates to a larger probability increase when the baseline probability is 0.5 than when it is 0.1 or 0.9. This is a property of the logit link, not a problem.
A practical consequence: the same experimental manipulation can produce different probability effects for different sub-populations (people who generally give a lot vs. a little), even if the log-odds effect is identical. Reporting odds ratios alongside predicted probabilities at different baseline levels gives a complete picture.


Code
# Show how OR translates to different probability differences at different baselines
baseline_logodds <- seq(-3, 3, by = 0.5)
effect_logodds   <- 0.59  # log(1.8)

tibble(
  baseline_logodds = baseline_logodds,
  baseline_prob    = plogis(baseline_logodds),
  treatment_prob   = plogis(baseline_logodds + effect_logodds),
  prob_difference  = plogis(baseline_logodds + effect_logodds) - plogis(baseline_logodds)
) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = baseline_prob, y = prob_difference)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.3, color = "#1e3a5f") +
  geom_point(size = 2.5, color = "#1e3a5f") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(
    title    = "Same log-odds effect → different probability differences",
    subtitle = sprintf("Log-odds effect = %.2f (OR = 1.8). The probability difference peaks at p ≈ 0.5.", effect_logodds),
    x = "Baseline probability of outcome",
    y = "Probability difference due to treatment"
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format()) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format()) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)
crumbs: 41  Binary and Count Outcomes in Experiments

title: 41  Binary and Count Outcomes in Experiments
text:
42.3 Part 2: Count Outcomes in Experiments

42.3.1 The Poisson Mixed Model for Experiments
For count outcomes in a between-subjects design with \(j\) participants nested in condition \(g\):
\[\log\bigl(E[Y_{ij}]\bigr) = \underbrace{\beta_0}_{\substack{\text{log expected count} \\ \text{in control}}} + \underbrace{u_{0j}}_{\substack{\text{person's count rate} \\ \text{deviation}}} + \underbrace{\beta_1 \cdot \text{treatment}_j}_{\substack{\text{treatment effect} \\ \text{on log-count}}}\]
Exponentiating \(\beta_1\) gives the rate ratio — the multiplicative factor by which the count changes in the treatment condition relative to control.


42.3.2 Example: Prosocial Helping Counts in a Scarcity Experiment
We simulate a between-subjects experiment with 100 participants (50 per condition). Participants in the scarcity condition are told resources are scarce before an opportunity to help a confederate. The outcome is the number of helping behaviors observed in a structured lab task (counts: 0–10).
The true treatment effect is a rate ratio of 0.65 — scarcity reduces helping by 35%.


Code
n_per_cond <- 50

df_help <- tibble(
  participant = 1:(n_per_cond * 2),
  condition   = rep(c("control", "scarcity"), each = n_per_cond)
) |>
  mutate(
    u0        = rnorm(n(), 0, 0.4),
    log_mu    = 1.8 + u0 + (-0.43) * as.integer(condition == "scarcity"),  # log(0.65)
    mu        = exp(log_mu),
    helping   = rpois(n(), mu),
    condition = factor(condition, levels = c("control", "scarcity"))
  )

# Summarize counts
df_help |>
  group_by(condition) |>
  summarise(
    mean_helping = mean(helping),
    sd_helping   = sd(helping),
    prop_zero    = mean(helping == 0),
    .groups      = "drop"
  ) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3, caption = "Helping count summary by condition")



Helping count summary by condition


condition
mean_helping
sd_helping
prop_zero




control
5.50
3.052
0.02


scarcity
4.26
2.530
0.04







42.3.3 Fitting and Checking the Count Model


Code
m_help <- glmmTMB(
  helping ~ condition + (1 | participant),
  data   = df_help,
  family = poisson
)

# Check overdispersion
pearson_resid <- residuals(m_help, type = "pearson")
dispersion    <- sum(pearson_resid^2) / df.residual(m_help)
cat("Dispersion ratio:", round(dispersion, 3), "\n")


Dispersion ratio: 0.656 


Code
cat("Values near 1.0 indicate no overdispersion.\n\n")


Values near 1.0 indicate no overdispersion.


Code
# Results as rate ratios
broom.mixed::tidy(m_help, effects = "fixed", exponentiate = TRUE, conf.int = TRUE) |>
  filter(term != "(Intercept)") |>
  select(term, estimate, conf.low, conf.high, p.value) |>
  knitr::kable(
    digits = 3,
    caption = "Scarcity effect as rate ratio. True RR = 0.65 (35% reduction in helping)."
  )



Scarcity effect as rate ratio. True RR = 0.65 (35% reduction in helping).


term
estimate
conf.low
conf.high
p.value




conditionscarcity
0.775
0.618
0.971
0.027







42.3.4 When Poisson Is Not Enough: Overdispersion in Experiments
In within-subjects designs, participants complete multiple trials per condition, and individual differences generate more variance in counts than a Poisson model allows. This overdispersion is captured by switching to a negative binomial or adding observation-level random effects.


Code
n_par_within  <- 60
n_trials_cond <- 6  # 6 trials per condition

df_help_within <- expand_grid(
  participant = 1:n_par_within,
  condition   = c("control", "scarcity"),
  trial       = 1:n_trials_cond
) |>
  mutate(
    u0        = rnorm(n_par_within, 0, 0.5)[participant],
    log_mu    = 1.5 + u0 - 0.43 * as.integer(condition == "scarcity"),
    mu        = exp(log_mu),
    # Add extra-Poisson variability (overdispersion)
    phi       = rgamma(n(), shape = 3, rate = 3),
    helping   = rpois(n(), mu * phi),
    participant = factor(participant),
    condition = factor(condition, levels = c("control", "scarcity"))
  )

m_nb_within <- glmmTMB(
  helping ~ condition + (1 | participant),
  data   = df_help_within,
  family = nbinom2
)

broom.mixed::tidy(m_nb_within, effects = "fixed", exponentiate = TRUE, conf.int = TRUE) |>
  filter(term != "(Intercept)") |>
  select(term, estimate, conf.low, conf.high, p.value) |>
  knitr::kable(
    digits = 3,
    caption = "Negative binomial mixed model: within-subjects scarcity on helping count."
  )

title: 41  Binary and Count Outcomes in Experiments
section: 42.4 Part 3: Mixed Factorial Designs with Non-Normal Outcomes
text:
42.4 Part 3: Mixed Factorial Designs with Non-Normal Outcomes
Many experiments have both a between-subjects factor (e.g., treatment vs. control) and a within-subjects factor (e.g., pre vs. post, gain vs. loss framing). When outcomes are binary or counts, the GLMM handles this naturally through the random effects structure.


Code
n_par_mf <- 100  # 50 per between-subjects condition
n_items   <- 8   # 4 items per within-subjects condition

df_mf <- expand_grid(
  participant  = 1:n_par_mf,
  ws_condition = c("gain", "loss"),
  item         = 1:4
) |>
  mutate(
    bs_condition = ifelse(participant <= 50, "low_stakes", "high_stakes"),
    u0           = rnorm(n_par_mf, 0, 0.9)[participant],
    log_odds     = -0.3
      + u0
      + 0.4 * (ws_condition == "loss")         # within-subjects framing effect
      + 0.7 * (bs_condition == "high_stakes")  # between-subjects stakes effect
      + 0.3 * (ws_condition == "loss") * (bs_condition == "high_stakes"),  # interaction
    prob         = plogis(log_odds),
    chose_risky  = rbinom(n(), 1, prob),
    participant  = factor(participant),
    ws_condition = factor(ws_condition, levels = c("gain", "loss")),
    bs_condition = factor(bs_condition, levels = c("low_stakes", "high_stakes"))
  )

m_mf <- glmer(
  chose_risky ~ ws_condition * bs_condition + (1 | participant),
  data   = df_mf,
  family = binomial
)

broom.mixed::tidy(m_mf, effects = "fixed", exponentiate = TRUE, conf.int = TRUE) |>
  select(term, estimate, conf.low, conf.high, p.value) |>
  knitr::kable(
    digits = 3,
    caption = "Mixed factorial binary outcome: framing × stakes on risky choice (odds ratios)."
  )



Mixed factorial binary outcome: framing × stakes on risky choice (odds ratios).

The interaction term on the odds ratio scale tells you whether the framing effect differs by stakes condition — multiplicatively. A significant interaction (OR ≠ 1) means the framing effect is larger or smaller in the high-stakes condition. As with continuous outcomes, plotting predicted probabilities is essential for communicating the interaction to readers.
crumbs: 41  Binary and Count Outcomes in Experiments

title: 41  Binary and Count Outcomes in Experiments
text:
42.5 Assumptions and Diagnostics






WarningAssumption: Correct Link Function



The logit link assumes that the log-odds of the outcome are a linear function of predictors. This is generally robust and appropriate for most binary outcomes near the center of the probability range. If many outcomes are near 0 or 1 (rare or near-certain events), consider whether the probit or complementary log-log link fits better — though in practice, logit and probit rarely disagree meaningfully.








WarningAssumption: No Separation



Complete separation occurs when one predictor perfectly predicts the outcome (e.g., everyone in condition A donated, no one in condition B did). Maximum likelihood estimation fails in this case — the log-odds estimate goes to infinity. This typically signals a design problem (conditions too extreme, too few observations) rather than a modeling problem. Warning signs: very large standard errors, coefficients in the hundreds.








WarningAssumption: Correct Distributional Family for Counts



Start with Poisson. Check the dispersion ratio (Pearson \(\chi^2\) / df). If > 1.5, switch to negative binomial (glmmTMB(..., family = nbinom2)). If zeros are more prevalent than either model predicts, add a zero-inflation component (ziformula = ~1). The distributional family should match the DGP — it is a substantive assumption about how the data were generated.








WarningAssumption: Sufficient Cluster Size for GLMMs



Logistic mixed models can have convergence problems with small cluster sizes (few trials per participant) because the likelihood is flat near the random effects variance. A rule of thumb: at least 5–10 observations per cluster for reliable random intercept estimation. With fewer observations, consider Bayesian estimation (brms) which handles small-cluster GLMMs more stably.
crumbs: 41  Binary and Count Outcomes in Experiments

title: 42  Effect Sizes, Reporting, and Reproducibility
text: 43 Effect Sizes, Reporting, and Reproducibility
crumbs: 42  Effect Sizes, Reporting, and Reproducibility

title: 42  Effect Sizes, Reporting, and Reproducibility
text:
43.1 Why This Chapter Exists
Nested data creates reporting problems that do not exist in simple ANOVA or OLS regression. When effects are estimated at multiple levels, when random effects have their own variance components, and when outcomes live on transformed scales, the standard playbook for effect sizes (Cohen’s \(d\), \(\eta^2\), \(R^2\)) breaks down or becomes ambiguous.
This chapter covers:

Effect size measures for mixed models — what they mean and how to compute them
Translating fixed effect estimates to interpretable metrics
Conditional vs. marginal \(R^2\)
Reporting standards for nested experimental data
Reproducibility practices: seeds, version pinning, and transparent reporting
crumbs: 42  Effect Sizes, Reporting, and Reproducibility

title: 42  Effect Sizes, Reporting, and Reproducibility
section: 43.2 Part 1: Effect Size in Mixed Models
text:
43.2 Part 1: Effect Size in Mixed Models

43.2.1 The Problem with Standard Effect Sizes
Cohen’s \(d\) is defined as the standardized mean difference:
\[d = \frac{\mu_1 - \mu_2}{\sigma}\]
But what is \(\sigma\) in a mixed model? There are multiple variance components: the residual variance \(\sigma^2_\varepsilon\), the random intercept variance \(\tau^2_{00}\), and possibly additional random slope variances. Each gives a different denominator — and therefore a different \(d\).
The answer depends on the question you are asking:

Conditional \(d\): standardizes by the within-cluster (residual) variance. Answers: “How large is the effect within a person/cluster, relative to how much observations vary within that cluster?”
Marginal \(d\): standardizes by the total variance (random effects + residual). Answers: “How large is the effect relative to all variability in the outcome?”

Neither is wrong. They answer different questions.


43.2.2 Cohen’s \(d\) for Experimental Effects in Nested Data
In a typical within-subjects experiment, the effect of interest is the difference between conditions, and the relevant variability is the within-person trial-level variance. The residual SD from the mixed model (not including the random effect variance) is the appropriate standardizer for a within-person \(d\).


Code
# Simulate a within-subjects experiment: 80 participants × 10 trials per condition
n_par  <- 80
n_trial <- 10

df_d <- expand_grid(
  participant = 1:n_par,
  condition   = c("control", "treatment"),
  trial       = 1:n_trial
) |>
  mutate(
    u0          = rnorm(n_par, 0, 8)[participant],  # between-person SD = 8
    residual_sd = 5,
    effect      = 3,  # true within-person effect
    response    = 50 + u0 + effect * (condition == "treatment") + rnorm(n(), 0, residual_sd),
    participant = factor(participant),
    condition   = factor(condition, levels = c("control", "treatment"))
  )

m_d <- lmer(response ~ condition + (1 | participant), data = df_d)

# Extract variance components
vc <- as.data.frame(VarCorr(m_d))
sigma_residual <- vc$sdcor[vc$grp == "Residual"]     # within-person SD
sigma_person   <- vc$sdcor[vc$grp == "participant"]   # between-person SD
sigma_total    <- sqrt(sigma_person^2 + sigma_residual^2)

# Fixed effect estimate
beta_treatment <- fixef(m_d)["conditiontreatment"]

# Two versions of d
d_conditional <- beta_treatment / sigma_residual
d_marginal    <- beta_treatment / sigma_total

tibble(
  metric         = c("Fixed effect (unstandardized)", "Conditional d (÷ residual SD)", "Marginal d (÷ total SD)"),
  value          = c(beta_treatment, d_conditional, d_marginal),
  denominator    = c("—", round(sigma_residual, 2), round(sigma_total, 2))
) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3, caption = "Three ways to express the same treatment effect")



Three ways to express the same treatment effect


metric
value
denominator




Fixed effect (unstandardized)
3.010
—


Conditional d (÷ residual SD)
0.615
4.9


Marginal d (÷ total SD)
0.316
9.53





Interpreting the two \(d\) values: - Conditional \(d\): standardizes within the trial-level noise. A \(d\) of 0.6 here means the treatment shifts the mean by 0.6 trial-level SDs — controlling for stable person differences. - Marginal \(d\): standardizes relative to all variability including between-person differences. This will always be smaller than conditional \(d\) when there is substantial between-person variance, because the denominator is larger. It represents the treatment’s effect size in the full distribution of responses.
The conditional \(d\) is usually more appropriate for experimental reporting, because the design specifically removes between-person variance through random assignment and repeated measurement. Marginal \(d\) deflates the effect by variance that the design was explicitly not trying to explain.
crumbs: 42  Effect Sizes, Reporting, and Reproducibility

title: 42  Effect Sizes, Reporting, and Reproducibility
section: 43.3 Part 2: Conditional and Marginal \(R^2\) for Mixed Models
text:
43.3 Part 2: Conditional and Marginal \(R^2\) for Mixed Models

43.3.1 The Nakagawa-Schielzeth \(R^2\)
For linear mixed models, Nakagawa & Schielzeth (2013) define two \(R^2\) values:

Marginal \(R^2_m\): Variance explained by fixed effects only — how much the predictors in the model account for, ignoring individual differences.
Conditional \(R^2_c\): Variance explained by fixed effects plus random effects — how much the whole model (predictors + clustering) accounts for.

The difference \(R^2_c - R^2_m\) is the variance uniquely attributable to the random effects (the clustering).


Code
# Compute R² using the performance package
r2_results <- performance::r2(m_d)
print(r2_results)


# R2 for Mixed Models

  Conditional R2: 0.742
     Marginal R2: 0.024




43.3.2 Interpreting \(R^2\) in Nested Designs
Consider what these values tell you:

A large marginal \(R^2\) means your experimental manipulations account for substantial variance in the outcome.
A large gap between conditional and marginal \(R^2\) means individual differences (the random effects) account for substantial variance — much of the outcome is determined by who the person is, not what condition they were in.

In a typical within-subjects experiment, conditional \(R^2\) can be quite high (0.5–0.8) while marginal \(R^2\) is low (0.03–0.15). This is not a sign that the experiment failed — it is a sign that the random effects structure is doing its job, absorbing the stable between-person variance and leaving the experimental effect clearly visible in the residuals.


Code
# Show R² decomposition visually
r2_vals <- performance::r2(m_d)

r2_df <- tibble(
  component = c("Fixed effects (marginal R²)", "Random effects (conditional − marginal)", "Unexplained"),
  proportion = c(
    r2_vals$R2_marginal,
    r2_vals$R2_conditional - r2_vals$R2_marginal,
    1 - r2_vals$R2_conditional
  )
)

ggplot(r2_df, aes(x = "", y = proportion, fill = component)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.5) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("#1e3a5f", "#4a90d9", "#d0d0d0")) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format()) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Variance decomposition in the mixed model",
    subtitle = "Fixed effects (marginal R²) + Random effects contribution + Residual unexplained",
    x = NULL, y = "Proportion of total variance", fill = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
crumbs: 42  Effect Sizes, Reporting, and Reproducibility

title: 42  Effect Sizes, Reporting, and Reproducibility
section: 43.4 Part 3: ICC as an Effect Size
text:
43.4 Part 3: ICC as an Effect Size
The Intraclass Correlation Coefficient (ICC) — covered in Module 1 — is itself an effect size: it quantifies how much of the outcome’s variance is attributable to the clustering structure.
\[\text{ICC} = \frac{\tau^2_{00}}{\tau^2_{00} + \sigma^2_\varepsilon}\]
In experimental reporting, the ICC tells readers how much stable between-person differences matter:

High ICC (> 0.5): Most variance is between people. Within-subjects designs are very efficient here (they remove the large between-person noise). Between-subjects designs require large samples to detect effects against this noise.
Low ICC (< 0.1): Little between-person clustering. Within- and between-subjects designs are roughly equivalent in power.

Reporting the ICC in an experimental methods section tells readers how much person-to-person variability was present and why a particular design choice (within vs. between subjects) was efficient.
crumbs: 42  Effect Sizes, Reporting, and Reproducibility

title: 42  Effect Sizes, Reporting, and Reproducibility
section: 43.5 Part 4: Translating Model Output to Interpretable Metrics
text:
43.5 Part 4: Translating Model Output to Interpretable Metrics

43.5.1 From Log-Odds to Probabilities
For logistic mixed models, the fixed effect is in log-odds units. Always translate to probabilities for reporting:


Code
# Example: framing experiment on donation probability
# Log-odds effect = 0.59 (OR = 1.8)

log_odds_effect    <- 0.59
baseline_log_odds  <- -0.5  # average person's log-odds in control

control_prob   <- plogis(baseline_log_odds)
treatment_prob <- plogis(baseline_log_odds + log_odds_effect)

tibble(
  metric = c("Log-odds effect", "Odds ratio", "Control probability", "Treatment probability", "Probability difference"),
  value  = c(log_odds_effect, exp(log_odds_effect), control_prob, treatment_prob, treatment_prob - control_prob)
) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3, caption = "Translating from log-odds to probabilities for reporting")



Translating from log-odds to probabilities for reporting


metric
value




Log-odds effect
0.590


Odds ratio
1.804


Control probability
0.378


Treatment probability
0.522


Probability difference
0.145





Reporting template: “Loss-framed appeals had higher donation rates than gain-framed appeals (OR = 1.81, 95% CI [1.42, 2.31], \(z\) = 4.7, \(p\) < .001). At the average baseline donation probability of 38%, loss framing increased donation probability by approximately 12 percentage points (to 50%).”


43.5.2 From Log-Counts to Rate Ratios
For Poisson/negative binomial models, exponentiate to get rate ratios:


Code
# Example: scarcity on helping count
# Log effect = -0.43 (rate ratio = 0.65)

log_rate_effect    <- -0.43
baseline_log_count <- 1.8  # average log(expected count) in control

control_count   <- exp(baseline_log_count)
treatment_count <- exp(baseline_log_count + log_rate_effect)

tibble(
  metric = c("Log-count effect", "Rate ratio", "Control mean count", "Treatment mean count", "Count reduction"),
  value  = c(log_rate_effect, exp(log_rate_effect), control_count, treatment_count, control_count - treatment_count)
) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3, caption = "Translating from log-counts to counts for reporting")



Translating from log-counts to counts for reporting


metric
value




Log-count effect
-0.430


Rate ratio
0.651


Control mean count
6.050


Treatment mean count
3.935


Count reduction
2.114





Reporting template: “Scarcity reduced helping behavior relative to control (RR = 0.65, 95% CI [0.48, 0.88], \(p\) = .005). In the control condition, participants helped on average 6.0 times; in the scarcity condition, 3.9 times — a reduction of approximately 2.1 helping episodes.”
crumbs: 42  Effect Sizes, Reporting, and Reproducibility

title: 42  Effect Sizes, Reporting, and Reproducibility
section: 43.6 Part 5: Reporting Standards for Nested Experimental Data
text:
43.6 Part 5: Reporting Standards for Nested Experimental Data

43.6.1 What to Report
A complete report for a mixed model analysis includes:






TipReporting Checklist for Mixed Models



Design description: - [ ] Whether the design is within-subjects, between-subjects, or mixed factorial - [ ] Number of participants, clusters, and observations per cell - [ ] ICC of the outcome (how much between-person variance was present)
Model specification: - [ ] Fixed effects included (and their coding: treatment, contrast, etc.) - [ ] Random effects structure (intercept only? slopes? which predictors vary by person?) - [ ] Software and package versions used
Results for each effect: - [ ] Unstandardized estimate with units - [ ] Standard error and 95% confidence interval - [ ] Test statistic and degrees of freedom (\(t\), \(z\), or \(F\) with Satterthwaite/Kenward-Roger df) - [ ] \(p\)-value - [ ] Effect size (\(d\), \(\eta^2\), OR, RR as appropriate)
Model-level fit: - [ ] Marginal and conditional \(R^2\) - [ ] Variance components (\(\tau^2_{00}\), \(\sigma^2_\varepsilon\), and any random slopes)
For non-normal outcomes: - [ ] Overdispersion check (for count models) - [ ] Predicted probabilities or counts at substantively meaningful covariate values




43.6.2 Example APA-Style Results Paragraph

“We analyzed donation decisions using a logistic mixed model with participant random intercepts, framing condition (gain vs. loss) as a fixed effect, and trial nested within participant. Loss-framing significantly increased donation probability (b = 0.59, OR = 1.81, 95% CI [1.42, 2.31], \(z\) = 4.7, \(p\) < .001). At the sample’s average baseline donation rate (38%), loss framing increased donation probability by approximately 12 percentage points. The ICC was .48, indicating substantial stable individual differences in donation tendency; the random intercept accounted for 31% of total variance (conditional \(R^2_c\) = .35; marginal \(R^2_m\) = .04).”
crumbs: 42  Effect Sizes, Reporting, and Reproducibility

title: 42  Effect Sizes, Reporting, and Reproducibility
text:
43.7 Part 6: Reproducibility Practices

43.7.1 Setting Seeds
Simulations, multiple imputation, bootstrapped CIs, and permutation tests all depend on random number generation. A fixed seed ensures the analysis is reproducible:


Code
# Set seed at the top of every analysis script
set.seed(42)

# For parallel computing, set seeds for each worker explicitly
library(parallel)
RNGkind("L'Ecuyer-CMRG")  # safe for parallel streams
set.seed(42)


A single set.seed() call at the top of the script is sufficient for non-parallel analyses. Document the seed in the manuscript methods section.


43.7.2 Package Version Pinning
Results can change between package versions (model fitting algorithms improve, bugs are fixed, defaults change). Record the environment at the time of analysis:


Code
# At the end of every analysis script:
sessionInfo()

# Or more concisely:
writeLines(capture.output(sessionInfo()), "session_info.txt")


For fully reproducible environments, use renv:
# Initialize renv in the project root
renv::init()

# After installing/updating packages:
renv::snapshot()  # saves renv.lock with exact package versions

# To restore the environment on a new machine:
renv::restore()


43.7.3 Preregistration for Experimental Analyses
Preregistration specifies your analysis plan before data collection, separating confirmatory from exploratory findings. For mixed model experiments, the preregistration should specify:

The exact model formula (including random effects structure)
The primary outcome variable
The family and link function (for GLMMs)
How multiple comparisons will be handled
Any planned sensitivity analyses (e.g., random slopes model as robustness check)

Deviations from the preregistered plan should be disclosed and labeled as exploratory.
crumbs: 42  Effect Sizes, Reporting, and Reproducibility

title: 42  Effect Sizes, Reporting, and Reproducibility
section: 43.8 Part 7: Common Reporting Mistakes in Nested Data
text:
43.8 Part 7: Common Reporting Mistakes in Nested Data






WarningMistake 1: Reporting Only the Pooled Estimate



If you ran a random-intercepts model but forgot to decompose within- and between-person effects (Module 2), the fixed effect coefficient is a blend of two mechanisms. Report both if they differ.








WarningMistake 2: Using Total SD for Within-Subjects d



Reporting Cohen’s \(d\) using the total SD (including between-person variance) dramatically understates the within-person effect. Use the residual SD from the mixed model for within-subjects standardization.








WarningMistake 3: Interpreting ICC-Corrected SEs Incorrectly



Standard errors from lmer already account for the nested structure. Do not additionally “correct” them for clustering (e.g., by applying cluster-robust SEs on top of a random intercept model). That would double-count the clustering adjustment.








WarningMistake 4: Reporting Conditional R² as the “Effect Size” of a Fixed Effect



Conditional \(R^2\) includes variance explained by the random effects. It is not an effect size for your experimental manipulation. Use marginal \(R^2\) for the fixed-effects explanation, or report individual \(\eta^2\) or \(d\) for specific contrasts.








WarningMistake 5: Omitting the Random Effects Structure from the Methods



Reporting “we used a mixed model” without specifying the random effects structure (intercept only? random slopes for condition?) makes the analysis non-reproducible. Another researcher cannot verify or extend your work without knowing the exact model formula.
crumbs: 42  Effect Sizes, Reporting, and Reproducibility

title: 43  Overview
text: Module 6: Extended Toolkit

title: 43  Overview
text:
43.1 Beyond Linear Outcomes
The previous modules built the core framework for nested data analysis, assuming a continuous, approximately normally distributed outcome. Most of the real world does not cooperate with that assumption.
Outcomes in social science research are often: - Binary: Did the customer purchase? Did the patient recover? Did the participant choose option A? - Counts: How many times did the participant make an aggressive move? How many days was the patient hospitalized? - Time-to-event: When did the employee quit? When did the patient relapse? When did the firm go bankrupt?
And the data have additional complications: - Missing observations that are not randomly missing — the patients who dropped out are systematically different from those who stayed - Effect sizes that are hard to interpret because variance exists at multiple levels
This module addresses each of these extensions.

title: 43  Overview
text:
43.2 What This Module Covers
Part 1: Binary Outcomes — Logistic MLM — Generalized linear mixed models for binary outcomes. The crucial distinction between marginal and conditional effects, which matters enormously for multilevel logistic models.
Part 2: Count Outcomes — Poisson MLM — Count data, overdispersion, zero-inflation, and when to use negative binomial models.
Part 3: Survival Analysis — Time-to-event data, the hazard function, Kaplan-Meier curves, Cox proportional hazards models, and frailty models for clustered survival data.
Part 4: Missing Data in Nested Contexts — Missing data mechanisms, why longitudinal dropout is especially problematic, and multiple imputation strategies for nested data.
Part 5: Effect Sizes & Reporting — The challenges of R² in multilevel models, marginal vs. conditional R², ICC as an effect size, and how to write up and report multilevel analyses.

title: 44  Binary Outcomes (Logistic MLM)
text: 45 Binary Outcomes: Logistic Mixed Models

title: 44  Binary Outcomes (Logistic MLM)
text:
45.1 When Your Outcome is 0 or 1
Suppose you are studying whether a price promotion increases purchase probability, using transaction data from 5,000 customers across 50 stores. Your outcome is binary: purchased (1) or not (0). Customers are nested within stores.
A standard logistic regression ignores the store clustering. A logistic mixed model accounts for it — allowing stores to differ in their baseline purchase rates, and estimating the promotion effect while accounting for this.

title: 44  Binary Outcomes (Logistic MLM)
text:
45.2 The Model
The logistic mixed model extends the standard logistic regression by adding a random intercept (and optionally a random slope):
\[\text{logit}(P(Y_{ij} = 1)) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_{ij} + u_{0j}\]
Where \(u_{0j} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2_u)\) is the store-level random intercept.


Code
n_stores    <- 50
n_customers <- 100  # per store

store_effects <- rnorm(n_stores, 0, 1)  # store baseline differences

df_purchase <- expand_grid(
  store    = 1:n_stores,
  customer = 1:n_customers
) |>
  mutate(
    promotion   = rbinom(n(), 1, 0.5),
    store_eff   = store_effects[store],
    log_odds    = -1.5 + 0.8 * promotion + store_eff,
    purchased   = rbinom(n(), 1, plogis(log_odds)),
    store       = factor(store)
  )

# Logistic mixed model
m_logit <- glmer(purchased ~ promotion + (1 | store),
                 family  = binomial,
                 data    = df_purchase)

summary(m_logit)

Correlation of Fixed Effects:
          (Intr)

Code
broom.mixed::tidy(m_logit, effects = "fixed", exponentiate = TRUE) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "Fixed effects as odds ratios: promotion increases purchase odds")



Fixed effects as odds ratios: promotion increases purchase odds

The promotion coefficient (as an odds ratio) tells us: customers who receive a promotion have exp(0.8) ≈ 2.2 times the odds of purchasing compared to those who don’t, after accounting for store-level differences.

title: 44  Binary Outcomes (Logistic MLM)
section: 45.3 The Marginal vs. Conditional Effects Problem
text:
45.3 The Marginal vs. Conditional Effects Problem
This is the single most important subtlety in logistic MLMs, and it is often missed.
Conditional effect: The effect of promotion for a specific store at its mean random effect. This is what the model directly estimates.
Marginal effect: The effect of promotion averaged over the distribution of store effects — the population-average effect. This is usually what you want to report.
In linear models, marginal and conditional effects are equal. In logistic models, they are not. The marginal effect is always attenuated relative to the conditional effect — the more the random effects vary, the larger the difference.


Code
# Conditional predictions
pred_conditional <- ggpredict(m_logit, terms = "promotion",
                               type = "random")   # conditional on random effects = 0

# Marginal predictions (averaged over store distribution)
pred_marginal <- ggpredict(m_logit, terms = "promotion",
                            type = "fixed")   # marginal (fixed effects only)

bind_rows(
  as.data.frame(pred_conditional) |> mutate(type = "Conditional (store RE = 0)"),
  as.data.frame(pred_marginal)    |> mutate(type = "Marginal (averaged over stores)")
) |>
  select(type, x, predicted, conf.low, conf.high) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "Conditional vs. marginal predicted purchase probability")
