For policy and generalization purposes, the marginal effect is usually more relevant. For understanding the mechanism within a specific store, the conditional effect is more relevant.

title: 44  Binary Outcomes (Logistic MLM)
text:
45.4 Assumptions






WarningAssumption 1: Correct Link Function



What it means: The logistic model assumes the log-odds of the outcome is linear in the predictors. This is the standard assumption for binary outcomes, but it may not hold at the extremes.
How to check: Examine whether predicted probabilities are reasonable at extreme predictor values. Consider complementary log-log (cloglog) as an alternative if your outcome is rare (P < 0.05).








WarningAssumption 2: Normality of Random Effects (on the Log-Odds Scale)



What it means: The store-level random intercepts are assumed normal on the log-odds scale. If store effects are heavily skewed (e.g., a few stores have dramatically different purchase rates), this assumption may fail.
How to check: Extract random effects with ranef() and examine their distribution. A Q-Q plot of the random intercepts is informative.


Code
re_vals <- ranef(m_logit)$store[, 1]
qqnorm(re_vals, main = "Q-Q plot of store random effects")
qqline(re_vals, col = "#e63946", lwd = 2)

















WarningAssumption 3: No Overdispersion



What it means: For binary outcomes, overdispersion (more variance than the model predicts) can occur if there is residual clustering not accounted for by the random effects.
How to check: Compute the ratio of the Pearson chi-square statistic to its degrees of freedom. Values substantially greater than 1 indicate overdispersion.

title: 45  Count Outcomes (Poisson MLM)
text: 46 Count Outcomes: Poisson and Negative Binomial MLMs

title: 45  Count Outcomes (Poisson MLM)
section: 46.1 When Your Outcome is a Count
text:
46.1 When Your Outcome is a Count
Count outcomes arise frequently in psychology and social science: the number of aggressive acts in a session, the number of days absent from work, the number of times a consumer repurchases, the number of words recalled.
Counts have two properties that make standard linear regression inappropriate: 1. They are non-negative integers (you cannot have −3 absences) 2. Their variance often increases with their mean (heteroskedasticity is built-in)
The Poisson distribution is the natural model for counts. The Poisson mixed model extends it to nested data.

title: 45  Count Outcomes (Poisson MLM)
text:
46.2 The Poisson Mixed Model
\[\log(\mu_{ij}) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_{ij} + u_{0j}, \quad Y_{ij} \sim \text{Poisson}(\mu_{ij})\]
The log link ensures predictions are always positive. Coefficients are interpreted as log rate ratios, or more intuitively as rate ratios after exponentiation.


Code
n_participants <- 60
n_days         <- 14

participant_effects <- rnorm(n_participants, 0, 0.5)

df_count <- expand_grid(person = 1:n_participants, day = 1:n_days) |>
  mutate(
    treatment = rep(rbinom(n_participants, 1, 0.5), each = n_days),
    p_eff     = rep(participant_effects, each = n_days),
    log_mu    = 1.5 - 0.5 * treatment + p_eff,  # true rate ratio = exp(-0.5) ≈ 0.61
    count     = rpois(n(), lambda = exp(log_mu)),
    person    = factor(person)
  )

m_pois <- glmer(count ~ treatment + (1 | person),
                family = poisson,
                data   = df_count)

broom.mixed::tidy(m_pois, effects = "fixed", exponentiate = TRUE) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "Rate ratios: treatment reduces count outcome to 61% of control")



Rate ratios: treatment reduces count outcome to 61% of control

title: 45  Count Outcomes (Poisson MLM)
section: 46.3 Overdispersion: When Poisson is Not Enough
text:
46.3 Overdispersion: When Poisson is Not Enough
The Poisson distribution has a strict constraint: mean = variance. Real count data almost always has variance exceeding the mean — overdispersion. Sources of overdispersion include unobserved heterogeneity, clustering, and zero-inflation.


Code
# Check for overdispersion
pearson_residuals <- residuals(m_pois, type = "pearson")
dispersion_stat   <- sum(pearson_residuals^2) / df.residual(m_pois)
cat("Dispersion statistic:", round(dispersion_stat, 3),
    "(should be ~1 for Poisson; > 1.5 indicates overdispersion)\n")


Dispersion statistic: 1.022 (should be ~1 for Poisson; > 1.5 indicates overdispersion)


When overdispersion is present, use the negative binomial model, which adds a free dispersion parameter:


Code
# Simulate overdispersed count data
df_count_od <- df_count |>
  mutate(count_od = rnbinom(n(), mu = exp(log_mu), size = 2))  # overdispersed

m_nb <- glmmTMB(count_od ~ treatment + (1 | person),
                family = nbinom2,
                data   = df_count_od)

broom.mixed::tidy(m_nb, effects = "fixed", exponentiate = TRUE) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "Negative binomial rate ratios (handles overdispersion)")

title: 45  Count Outcomes (Poisson MLM)
text:
46.4 Zero-Inflation
Some count outcomes have more zeros than any Poisson or negative binomial model can account for. This is common when zeros arise from two different processes: (1) the structural zero — a participant who never engages in the behavior regardless of conditions, and (2) the sampling zero — a participant who could engage but happened not to on this occasion.


Code
# Zero-inflated count
df_count_zi <- df_count |>
  mutate(
    # Some people are "structural zeros" (never engage)
    structural_zero = rbinom(n(), 1, 0.3),
    count_zi = ifelse(structural_zero == 1, 0, count)
  )

cat("Proportion of zeros (standard Poisson):", mean(df_count$count == 0), "\n")


Proportion of zeros (standard Poisson): 0.1119048 


Code
cat("Proportion of zeros (zero-inflated):", mean(df_count_zi$count_zi == 0), "\n")


Proportion of zeros (zero-inflated): 0.3630952 


Code
m_zi <- glmmTMB(count_zi ~ treatment + (1 | person),
                ziformula = ~1,   # intercept-only model for zero-inflation
                family    = poisson,
                data      = df_count_zi)

broom.mixed::tidy(m_zi, effects = "fixed", component = "cond", exponentiate = TRUE) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "Zero-inflated Poisson: count model conditional on non-structural-zero")

text:
46.5 Assumptions






WarningAssumption 1: Correct Distribution Family











Distribution
When to use
Diagnosis




Poisson
Mean ≈ Variance; no excess zeros
Dispersion stat ≈ 1


Negative binomial
Variance > Mean (overdispersion)
Dispersion stat > 1.5


Zero-inflated Poisson

More zeros than Poisson predicts


Zero-inflated NB
Overdispersion + excess zeros
Both overdispersion and excess zeros











WarningAssumption 2: Log Link Linearity



What it means: The log of the expected count is a linear function of the predictors. This is usually reasonable but should be checked for continuous predictors.
How to check: Plot smoothed log-count against the continuous predictor to look for nonlinearity.

title: 46  Survival Analysis
text: 47 Survival Analysis

title: 46  Survival Analysis
text:
47.1 When Time-to-Event is the Outcome
Not all outcomes are measured on a continuous scale or as a snapshot at one moment. Some outcomes are events: the moment an employee quits, the day a patient relapses, the first purchase after acquiring a new customer. The outcome of interest is when the event occurs — or whether it has occurred at all by the time observation ends.
This is the domain of survival analysis (also called event history analysis or time-to-event analysis).
The defining feature: censoring. Some participants have not experienced the event by the end of the study — they were still employed, still in remission, still active users when the observation window closed. Censored observations contribute partial information: we know the event had not occurred up to that point, but we do not know when (or whether) it eventually will.
Standard regression discards this information. Survival analysis uses it.

title: 46  Survival Analysis
text:
47.2 The Hazard Function
The central concept is the hazard function \(h(t)\): the instantaneous rate at which events occur at time \(t\), given that the event has not yet occurred by \(t\).
\[h(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t \le T < t + \Delta t \mid T \ge t)}{\Delta t}\]
Intuitively: if you have survived until time \(t\), what is your risk of the event occurring in the next small interval?

title: 46  Survival Analysis
text:
47.3 Kaplan-Meier Curves
The Kaplan-Meier estimator non-parametrically estimates the survival function \(S(t) = P(T > t)\): the probability of surviving (not experiencing the event) past time \(t\).


Code
# Simulate employee tenure data
n <- 200
group <- rep(c("High autonomy", "Low autonomy"), each = 100)

# High-autonomy employees stay longer
tenure_time  <- c(rexp(100, rate = 0.05),   # ~20 months average
                   rexp(100, rate = 0.10))   # ~10 months average
censored     <- rbinom(200, 1, 0.3)  # 30% are censored (still employed)
tenure_obs   <- pmin(tenure_time, 24)  # cap at 24 months observation window
status       <- ifelse(censored == 1, 0, 1)   # 0 = censored, 1 = event (quit)

df_surv <- tibble(time = tenure_obs, status, group)

# Kaplan-Meier
km_fit <- survfit(Surv(time, status) ~ group, data = df_surv)

ggsurvplot(
  km_fit,
  data        = df_surv,
  conf.int    = TRUE,
  pval        = TRUE,
  risk.table  = TRUE,
  palette     = c("#1e3a5f", "#aec6e8"),
  legend.labs = c("High autonomy", "Low autonomy"),
  xlab        = "Months",
  ylab        = "Probability of staying",
  title       = "Kaplan-Meier: employee tenure by autonomy condition"
)

title: 46  Survival Analysis
text:
47.4 The Cox Proportional Hazards Model
The Cox model estimates the effect of predictors on the hazard rate without specifying the baseline hazard function — a semiparametric approach:
\[h(t \mid X) = h_0(t) \cdot \exp(\beta_1 X_1 + \beta_2 X_2 + \ldots)\]
Coefficients are interpreted as hazard ratios after exponentiation: \(\exp(\beta) > 1\) means higher hazard (shorter survival); \(\exp(\beta) < 1\) means lower hazard (longer survival).


Code
# Add predictors
df_surv <- df_surv |>
  mutate(
    autonomy     = ifelse(group == "High autonomy", 1, 0),
    age          = rnorm(200, 35, 8),
    satisfaction = rnorm(200, 60, 10)
  )

m_cox <- coxph(Surv(time, status) ~ autonomy + age + satisfaction,
               data = df_surv)

broom::tidy(m_cox, exponentiate = TRUE) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "Cox hazard ratios: autonomy reduces hazard of quitting")



Cox hazard ratios: autonomy reduces hazard of quitting

WarningAssumption: Proportional Hazards



What it means: The hazard ratio between groups is constant over time. The effect of autonomy on the hazard of quitting is the same in month 1 as in month 20.
Why it fails: Some effects are strongest early (a drug that prevents relapse mainly in the first few weeks) and fade over time. This is a non-proportional hazard.
How to check: Schoenfeld residuals should be uncorrelated with time. The cox.zph() function tests this.


Code
ph_test <- cox.zph(m_cox)
print(ph_test)


             chisq df      p
autonomy      3.52  1 0.0607
age           6.72  1 0.0096
satisfaction  4.91  1 0.0266
GLOBAL       15.37  3 0.0015

# If significant: stratify by the variable or include time interaction

title: 46  Survival Analysis
section: 47.5 Frailty Models for Clustered Survival Data
text:
47.5 Frailty Models for Clustered Survival Data
When survival times are clustered (patients within hospitals, employees within firms), standard Cox models underestimate standard errors. The frailty model adds a random effect (the “frailty”) to the Cox model — a direct analog of the random intercept in linear MLM.


Code
# Simulate clustered survival data
n_firms    <- 20
n_per_firm <- 10

firm_effects <- rnorm(n_firms, 0, 0.5)

df_cluster <- tibble(
  firm     = rep(1:n_firms, each = n_per_firm),
  autonomy = rbinom(n_firms * n_per_firm, 1, 0.5),
  firm_eff = rep(firm_effects, each = n_per_firm),
  time_raw = rexp(n_firms * n_per_firm,
                   rate = exp(-1.5 - 0.4 * autonomy + firm_eff)),
  status   = rbinom(n_firms * n_per_firm, 1, 0.7),
  time     = pmin(time_raw, 24)
)

# Cox model with gamma frailty
m_frailty <- coxph(Surv(time, status) ~ autonomy + frailty(firm),
                   data = df_cluster)

broom::tidy(m_frailty) |>
  filter(term == "autonomy") |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "Frailty model: firm clustering accounted for in survival analysis")



Frailty model: firm clustering accounted for in survival analysis


term
estimate
std.error
statistic
p.value




autonomy
-0.334
0.162
4.242
0.039











WarningAssumption: Non-Informative Censoring



What it means: The reason an observation is censored must be unrelated to the outcome. If sicker patients are more likely to drop out of a study (i.e., censoring is related to the hazard of relapse), standard survival estimates are biased.
How to check: Compare characteristics of censored and non-censored participants at their censoring time. Large differences suggest informative censoring.

title: 47  Missing Data in Nested Contexts
text: 48 Missing Data in Nested Contexts
crumbs: 47  Missing Data in Nested Contexts

title: 47  Missing Data in Nested Contexts
section: 48.1 Why Missing Data is a Nesting Problem
text:
48.1 Why Missing Data is a Nesting Problem
In a cross-sectional study, missing data is annoying but manageable: you lose some participants and hope they are not systematically different from those who stayed. In a longitudinal or nested study, missing data is woven into the very structure of the data — and it is almost always non-random.
Consider a clinical trial tracking depression over 12 months. Patients who are getting worse are more likely to drop out — they are too depressed to fill out questionnaires, or they seek alternative treatment. The patients who complete all 12 measurements are not a random sample. They are the patients who recovered (or at least stayed stable enough to participate). Any analysis based only on completers overestimates the treatment’s effectiveness.
This is attrition bias — but now understood through the lens of missing data mechanisms.
crumbs: 47  Missing Data in Nested Contexts

title: 47  Missing Data in Nested Contexts
text:
48.2 Missing Data Mechanisms
Three mechanisms, with very different implications:
MCAR (Missing Completely At Random): The probability of a value being missing is unrelated to any observed or unobserved variable. Your survey crashed and lost data for participants whose IDs ended in 7. Truly random. Analysis of complete cases is valid, though inefficient.
MAR (Missing At Random): The probability of missingness depends on observed variables, but not on the unobserved value itself. Older participants are more likely to miss the follow-up, but among older participants, the missing and observed values are similar. MAR allows valid inference with appropriate methods (mixed-effects models, multiple imputation).
MNAR (Missing Not At Random): The probability of missingness depends on the unobserved value. The most depressed patients are most likely to drop out — and depression is the outcome being measured. No standard method can fully correct for MNAR; sensitivity analysis is required.
crumbs: 47  Missing Data in Nested Contexts

title: 47  Missing Data in Nested Contexts
text:
48.3 Testing for Non-Random Dropout


Code
n_people <- 150
n_waves  <- 4

# Simulate longitudinal data with selective dropout
df_long <- expand_grid(person = 1:n_people, wave = 1:n_waves) |>
  group_by(person) |>
  mutate(
    baseline  = rnorm(1, 50, 10),
    treatment = rbinom(1, 1, 0.5),
    Y         = baseline - 3 * treatment * (wave - 1) / 3 + rnorm(n(), 0, 4),
    # Dropout: high-depression (high Y) people are more likely to dropout in wave 3+
    drop_prob = plogis(-3 + 0.04 * mean(Y[wave == 1])),
    dropout   = cummax(rbinom(n(), 1, drop_prob)),
    Y_obs     = ifelse(dropout == 1 & wave > 2, NA, Y)
  ) |>
  ungroup()

# Test: do completers differ from dropouts at baseline?
baseline_comparison <- df_long |>
  filter(wave == 1) |>
  mutate(completer = person %in% (df_long |>
    filter(wave == n_waves, !is.na(Y_obs)) |> pull(person))) |>
  group_by(completer) |>
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    mean_baseline = mean(baseline),
    mean_Y_wave1  = mean(Y)
  )

knitr::kable(baseline_comparison, digits = 2,
  caption = "Completers vs. dropouts at baseline: systematic differences indicate MAR/MNAR")



Completers vs. dropouts at baseline: systematic differences indicate MAR/MNAR


completer
n
mean_baseline
mean_Y_wave1




FALSE

51.64
52.14


TRUE

44.41
crumbs: 47  Missing Data in Nested Contexts

title: 47  Missing Data in Nested Contexts
text:
48.4 Mixed-Effects Models Under MAR
A key advantage of mixed-effects models: under the MAR assumption, the likelihood uses all available observations — not just complete cases. A patient who provided data at waves 1, 2, and 3 (but not 4) contributes to the model estimate for waves 1–3. You do not need to discard them.
This is why mixed-effects models are preferable to RM-ANOVA for longitudinal data with dropout: RM-ANOVA requires complete cases; mixed models do not.


Code
# Complete case analysis — keep only people with all 4 waves observed
df_complete <- df_long |>
  group_by(person) |>
  filter(sum(!is.na(Y_obs)) == n_waves) |>
  ungroup()

# Mixed model using all available data
m_all   <- lmer(Y_obs ~ wave * treatment + (wave | person), data = df_long)
m_complete <- lmer(Y_obs ~ wave * treatment + (wave | person), data = df_complete)

bind_rows(
  broom.mixed::tidy(m_all,      effects = "fixed") |> filter(str_detect(term, ":")) |>
    mutate(model = "MLM (all available data)"),
  broom.mixed::tidy(m_complete, effects = "fixed") |> filter(str_detect(term, ":")) |>
    mutate(model = "MLM (complete cases only)")
) |>
  select(model, estimate, std.error, p.value) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "Treatment × wave interaction: using all data reduces bias from selective dropout")



Treatment × wave interaction: using all data reduces bias from selective dropout


model
estimate
std.error
p.value




MLM (all available data)
-0.658
0.410
0.113


MLM (complete cases only)
-1.179
0.476
0.015
crumbs: 47  Missing Data in Nested Contexts

title: 47  Missing Data in Nested Contexts
text:
48.5 Multiple Imputation for Multilevel Data
When MCAR/MAR assumptions hold and you want to impute missing values before analysis (e.g., for predictors that are missing), the mice package supports multilevel-aware multiple imputation.
baseline_obs is a Level-2 variable — it is measured once per person, not once per observation. The right strategy is to impute it at the person level (one row per person), then merge the imputations back into the long-format data. This respects the nesting structure and avoids falsely treating the same missing value as if it were independent across waves.


Code
# Introduce 15% missingness in the person-level baseline covariate
set.seed(2024)
df_long_miss <- df_long |>
  group_by(person) |>
  mutate(
    baseline_obs = ifelse(runif(1) < 0.15, NA_real_, first(baseline))
  ) |>
  ungroup()

# --- Step 1: Impute at the person level ---
# Extract one row per person (Level-2 dataset)
df_person <- df_long_miss |>
  group_by(person) |>
  summarise(
    treatment    = first(treatment),
    baseline_obs = first(baseline_obs),
    mean_Y       = mean(Y_obs, na.rm = TRUE),  # person-level auxiliary variable
    .groups      = "drop"
  )

# Standard mice on the person-level data (pmm = predictive mean matching)
imp_L2 <- mice(
  df_person |> select(treatment, baseline_obs, mean_Y),
  method = "pmm",
  m      = 5,
  maxit  = 5,
  print  = FALSE
)

# --- Step 2: Merge each imputed L2 dataset back into the long-format data ---
# and run the mixed model, then pool
models_imp <- lapply(1:5, function(i) {
  df_person_imp <- df_person |>
    mutate(baseline_obs = complete(imp_L2, i)$baseline_obs)

  df_merged <- df_long_miss |>
    select(person, wave, treatment, Y_obs) |>
    left_join(df_person_imp |> select(person, baseline_obs),
              by = "person")

  lmer(Y_obs ~ wave * treatment + baseline_obs + (1 | person),
       data = df_merged, REML = FALSE)
})

pooled <- pool(models_imp)
summary(pooled) |>
  select(term, estimate, std.error, p.value) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "Pooled MI estimates: imputing at the person level respects nesting structure")



Pooled MI estimates: imputing at the person level respects nesting structure


term
estimate
std.error
p.value




(Intercept)
0.318
1.310
0.809

-0.187
0.284
0.511

1.075
0.902
0.235

0.994

0.000

-0.851

WarningAssumption: MAR (for Imputation and MLM)



What it means: The probability of a value being missing depends only on observed variables. This allows valid inference using all available data or multiple imputation.
How to check: You cannot directly test MAR vs. MNAR. Conduct sensitivity analyses: how different would your conclusions be under a range of MNAR scenarios? The tipping point analysis asks how extreme the missing data would have to be to overturn your result.
crumbs: 47  Missing Data in Nested Contexts

title: 48  Effect Sizes & Reporting
text: 49 Effect Sizes & Reporting

title: 48  Effect Sizes & Reporting
section: 49.1 Why R² is Hard in MLM
text:
49.1 Why R² is Hard in MLM
In standard regression, \(R^2\) has a clean interpretation: the proportion of variance in Y explained by the predictors. In multilevel models, there are multiple sources of variance (level-1 residuals, level-2 random effects) and multiple ways to define “explained variance.”
Nakagawa & Schielzeth (2013) proposed the now-standard solution: two complementary \(R^2\) values for mixed models.
Marginal R² (\(R^2_m\)): The proportion of total variance explained by the fixed effects alone. This captures the “main story” — how much variability in the outcome is accounted for by your predictors.
Conditional R² (\(R^2_c\)): The proportion of total variance explained by both fixed and random effects. This includes the variance absorbed by the random effects structure — how much the model (including the clustering) explains in total.
\[R^2_m = \frac{\sigma^2_f}{\sigma^2_f + \sigma^2_u + \sigma^2_\varepsilon}\]
\[R^2_c = \frac{\sigma^2_f + \sigma^2_u}{\sigma^2_f + \sigma^2_u + \sigma^2_\varepsilon}\]
where \(\sigma^2_f\) = variance of fixed effect predictions, \(\sigma^2_u\) = random effect variance, \(\sigma^2_\varepsilon\) = residual variance.


Code
n_people <- 60
n_days   <- 10

person_int <- rnorm(n_people, 50, 8)
df <- expand_grid(person = 1:n_people, day = 1:n_days) |>
  mutate(
    sleep  = rnorm(n(), 7, 1.5),
    mood   = person_int[person] + 2.5 * (sleep - 7) + rnorm(n(), 0, 5),
    person = factor(person)
  )

m <- lmer(mood ~ sleep + (1 | person), data = df)

# Compute R²
r2_vals <- r2(m)
print(r2_vals)


# R2 for Mixed Models

  Conditional R2: 0.781
     Marginal R2: 0.127


Interpretation: - \(R^2_m\) tells you how much variance is explained by sleep alone - \(R^2_c\) tells you how much is explained by sleep plus person-level clustering - The difference (\(R^2_c - R^2_m\)) is the proportion attributable to person-level random effects — related to the ICC

title: 48  Effect Sizes & Reporting
text:
49.2 ICC as an Effect Size
The ICC from a null model is itself an effect size: it tells you the proportion of total variance that is between-cluster. Reporting this before any predictors is informative — it tells readers how much of the outcome is driven by group membership, regardless of the predictors.


Code
m_null <- lmer(mood ~ 1 + (1 | person), data = df)
icc(m_null)






ICC_adjusted
ICC_unadjusted
optional




0.640216
0.640216
FALSE

title: 48  Effect Sizes & Reporting
text:
49.3 Standardized Coefficients
Fixed effect coefficients are in the original units of X and Y. For comparison across studies or predictors with different scales, standardize:


Code
# Method 1: Standardize inputs
df <- df |>
  mutate(sleep_z = scale(sleep)[,1],
         mood_z  = scale(mood)[,1])

m_std <- lmer(mood_z ~ sleep_z + (1 | person), data = df)
broom.mixed::tidy(m_std, effects = "fixed") |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "Standardized coefficient: 1 SD increase in sleep → β SD increase in mood")

title: 48  Effect Sizes & Reporting
text:
49.4 What to Report
Here is a model results write-up following JARS-Quant guidelines for multilevel models:

Sample write-up:
We modeled daily mood as a function of hours of sleep using a two-level mixed-effects model (lme4; Bates et al., 2015), with days (Level 1) nested within participants (Level 2). A random intercept for participant was included to account for between-person differences in baseline mood.
A null model revealed an intraclass correlation of ICC = .{icc}, indicating that {icc*100}% of the variance in mood was attributable to between-person differences in baseline mood.
Adding sleep as a predictor significantly improved model fit (χ² = X, df = 1, p < .001). Sleep significantly predicted same-day mood (b = 2.48, SE = 0.31, t = 8.0, p < .001), indicating that each additional hour of sleep was associated with a 2.5-point increase in mood (on a 0–100 scale). The marginal R² was .{r2_m} and the conditional R² was .{r2_c}, indicating that sleep explained {r2_m100}% of total variance; together with person-level random effects, the model explained {r2_c100}% of total variance.

title: 48  Effect Sizes & Reporting
text:
49.5 Reporting Checklist






NoteMinimum Reporting Standards for MLM




Fixed effects table: Coefficients, SEs, t/z statistics, p-values (or credible intervals if Bayesian)
Random effects table: Variance components at each level, ICC
Model comparison: AIC/BIC or LRT for key model comparisons
Marginal and conditional R² (Nakagawa & Schielzeth method)
Sample sizes at each level: N participants, N observations (unbalanced patterns if relevant)
Software and packages: R version, lme4/brms version, REML vs. ML specification
Centering decisions: Was the predictor grand-mean or cluster-mean centered?
Random effects structure: What random effects were included and why?





Code
# A clean reporting table
broom.mixed::tidy(m, effects = "fixed", conf.int = TRUE) |>
  mutate(
    p_value = format.pval(p.value, digits = 2, eps = 0.001)
  ) |>
  select(term, estimate, std.error, conf.low, conf.high, p_value) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "APA-style fixed effects table")

title: Appendix A — About
text:
Dan Schley is an Associate Professor in the Department of Marketing Management at Rotterdam School of Management, Erasmus University Rotterdam. His work sits at the intersection of psychology, economics, and statistical methodology, with a particular focus on measurement, experimentation, and causal inference in behavioral research. Contact: schley@rsm.nl

I came to multilevel modeling the way many behavioral researchers do — not as a statistician, but as someone who kept noticing that the standard tools didn’t quite fit the data. My training moved across economics at UC San Diego, quantitative psychology at The Ohio State University, and ultimately marketing research at Erasmus. Each transition brought a different set of methodological assumptions into view.
Repeated-measures experiments. Panel datasets. Longitudinal surveys. Cross-national comparisons. In every case, observations are clustered in ways that basic regression ignores. And in every case, the right question is not just “how do I fit this model?” but “what process generated this data, and how close does my model get to it?”
This workshop grew out of teaching the ERIM doctoral program at RSM, where I found that students’ biggest stumbling block was not the mathematics — it was the conceptual leap from thinking about data as a flat table to thinking about data as a slice through a structured, multi-layered process. The DGP framing — treating your dataset as a snapshot of a larger data-generating process — helped more students than any single equation.
The companion workshop on Measurement, Experimentation, and Causal Inference covers many of the same research design questions from a different angle. Together, these workshops aim to bridge the vocabulary gap between psychology, economics, and quantitative social science.

title: Appendix B — Suggest a Topic
text:
This workshop is a living document. If there is a topic you would like to see covered — or a section you think could be clearer, deeper, or better illustrated — please use the “Report an Issue” link in the sidebar (or click the GitHub icon at the top right of any page).
Suggestions that have been particularly useful from past participants:

More worked examples with real datasets
Additional guidance on reporting standards
Coverage of specific R packages (e.g., lme4 vs. brms comparison)
Connections to SEM software (Mplus, lavaan)
Applied examples from specific subfields (clinical, organizational, consumer behavior)

All feedback is welcome.
